designSystem.spacingAndRadii:
SPACING — 4px base grid, named on an Apple-style geometric-ish ramp. Tokens (--ob-space-*): 0=0, px=1, 0.5=2, 1=4, 1.5=6, 2=8, 3=12, 4=16, 5=20, 6=24, 8=32, 10=40, 12=48, 16=64, 20=80, 24=96, 32=128, 40=160. Component rules: chip/pill padding 6/12, button 10/18, input 12/14, card padding 24 (mobile) / 28-32 (desktop), card-to-card gap 16-24, section vertical rhythm 96-128, content max-width 1200 (app) / 1120 (marketing prose 680). Touch targets >=44x44 always (HIG). Generous whitespace is a brand requirement — when unsure, go one step larger.

RADII (--ob-radius-*): xs=6 (chips, badges, inline code), sm=10 (inputs, small buttons, segmented items), md=14 (buttons, tooltips, toasts), lg=20 (cards, list rows), xl=28 (large feature cards, modals, the big rounded cards in references), 2xl=36 (hero panels, sheets), pill=999 (pill nav, pill CTAs, avatars, status dots), full=9999. Nesting rule: child radius = parent radius − padding, so concentric corners stay optically parallel (e.g. 28px card with 16px inset image -> ~12px image radius). Focus rings and shadows follow the element's own radius.
designSystem.shadowsAndElevation:
Six-tier elevation. Soft, low-spread, multi-layer, near-neutral (a hair warm in light, pure-dark in dark) — Apple-grade, never harsh. Light mode leans on shadow; dark mode leans on borders + a faint top inner-highlight since shadows barely read on #1E1E1E.

LIGHT (--ob-shadow-*):
  e0 (flush): none — flat list rows on surface.
  e1 (raised): 0 1px 2px rgba(20,18,14,.05), 0 1px 1px rgba(20,18,14,.03) — chips, inputs at rest.
  e2 (card): 0 2px 4px rgba(20,18,14,.05), 0 6px 14px rgba(20,18,14,.06) — the default large rounded card.
  e3 (floating): 0 4px 8px rgba(20,18,14,.06), 0 14px 30px rgba(20,18,14,.09) — floating pill nav, hover-lifted card, popovers.
  e4 (overlay): 0 8px 16px rgba(20,18,14,.08), 0 28px 60px rgba(20,18,14,.14) — modals, sheets, command palette.
  e-accent (focus glow): 0 0 0 4px rgba(192,254,111,.30) — the ONE place lime is allowed as a glow, around an active concept node / selected mastery card.

DARK (--ob-shadow-*):
  e1: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,.40) + inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,.04).
  e2: 0 2px 6px rgba(0,0,0,.45), 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,.40) + inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,.05).
  e3: 0 6px 16px rgba(0,0,0,.50), 0 18px 40px rgba(0,0,0,.50) + inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,.06).
  e4: 0 10px 24px rgba(0,0,0,.55), 0 32px 70px rgba(0,0,0,.62) + inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,.06).
  e-accent: 0 0 0 4px rgba(192,254,111,.22), 0 0 24px rgba(192,254,111,.18) (a true glow, since dark backgrounds carry it).

GLASS elevation (premium/spatial surfaces): background var(--ob-glass-fill); backdrop-filter blur(28px) saturate(160%); 1px var(--ob-glass-border) top edge; plus e3 beneath. Reserved for pill nav and the optional spatial/visionOS-style panels seen in references. Hairline borders (var(--ob-border)) accompany e1–e2 cards in both modes for crisp edges on busy backgrounds.
designSystem.motionLanguage:
Philosophy: motion clarifies state change, it never decorates. Short, eased, interruptible — Apple restraint. Everything respects prefers-reduced-motion.

DURATIONS (--ob-dur-*): instant 80ms (token/checkbox toggles), fast 140ms (hover, pressed, focus-ring), base 220ms (most enter/exit, tab/segment slide), slow 320ms (modal/sheet, accordion, route cross-fade), slowest 480ms (page hero stagger, concept-graph relayout).

EASINGS (--ob-ease-*):
  standard cubic-bezier(.2,0,0,1) — default in/out, the 'iOS' feel.
  entrance (decelerate) cubic-bezier(.16,1,.3,1) — elements arriving (sheets up, popovers in).
  exit (accelerate) cubic-bezier(.4,0,1,1) — elements leaving.
  spring (overshoot) cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1) — used ONLY for celebratory beats (mastery achieved, correct answer, streak +1); never on routine UI.

SIGNATURE TRANSITIONS:
  • Hover-lift on cards: translateY(-2px) + shadow e2->e3 over fast/standard. Cursor never feels sticky.
  • Press: scale .98 + shadow drop over instant; releases on standard. Pills/buttons/cards.
  • Floating pill nav: active item is a sliding lime-or-black 'puck' that animates left/right via spring-lite (.34,1.1,.5,1) at base — the layout-id transition (framer-motion layoutId) so the indicator glides between tabs.
  • Concept-graph: nodes fade+scale-in (from .96) staggered 40ms along edges as connections 'draw' (SVG stroke-dashoffset 0->full) over slowest — the learning-native reveal.
  • Streaming AI text: tokens fade-in opacity 0->1 over 120ms each, plus a 2px lime caret that pulses (opacity 1<->.3, 900ms) while generating; caret removed on stop.
  • Mastery ring fill: SVG circle stroke-dashoffset eases over slow with the spring easing on the final 10% for a satisfying 'lock-in'.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS:
  • Correct answer: success-subtle flash + a single 8px lime confetti burst (≤6 particles) + spring scale 1->1.04->1. Wrong: 2-cycle 6px horizontal shake over 180ms (paired with danger color + ✕ icon, never color alone).
  • Toast: slides in from bottom-right translateY(16px)+fade entrance, auto-dismiss slides out exit.
  • Skeleton/loading: shimmer is a slow (1400ms) gradient sweep at 8% contrast — calm, not strobing.
  • Magnifier lens: scales in from .9 with entrance easing, tracks cursor with no transition (1:1) so it never lags.

REDUCED MOTION (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce OR ADHD/[data-reduced-motion]): all translate/scale/spring collapse to opacity cross-fades capped at 120ms; confetti, shake, caret-pulse, shimmer sweep, and graph stroke-draw are disabled (graph nodes simply appear); mastery ring snaps to value. Layout-id puck jumps instantly. We honor it via a global @media block AND a JS-readable data attribute so the ADHD focus mode can force it independently of OS settings.
designSystem.accessibilitySystem:
Accessibility is a first-class, always-on layer (secondary market: ADHD, Dyslexia, Color Blindness, Weak Eyesight). A floating 'Accessibility' control (bottom-left, glass pill) opens a sheet toggling four independent modes, each a data-attribute on <html> so they compose freely and persist to Supabase user prefs.

1) HOVER MAGNIFIER / BOLD TOOL (the signature accessibility flourish). A togglable 'Reading Lens' with two interchangeable modes:
   • Magnify: a circular lens (default 160px, scroll-to-resize 96–280px) follows the cursor and renders a 1.8x–3x zoom (slider) of whatever text/UI is under it. Implemented as a fixed overlay that clones the hovered region via CSS transform scale on a duplicated layer (or background-image of the element box), clipped to a circle with a 1px border + e3 shadow + faint lime ring. Crucially it is an OVERLAY — underlying layout never reflows, so the design is preserved pixel-for-pixel.
   • Bold/Boost: instead of zooming, the hovered text node gets font-weight bumped +200 and letter-spacing +0.01em transiently (class toggled on :hover target) so any text anywhere can be 'boosted' for legibility WITHOUT changing size or layout (we reserve space by transitioning weight only). Works on body, captions, code.
   Keyboard: hold Alt to summon the lens at caret; arrow keys nudge. Disabled automatically inside text inputs. Honors reduced-motion (lens tracks without easing regardless).

2) DYSLEXIA MODE ([data-dyslexia='on']): swaps display+text families to self-hosted OpenDyslexic (SIL-licensed); increases letter-spacing to 0.035em, word-spacing to 0.12em, line-height +0.2, paragraph max-width to ~66ch; left-aligns (never justified); adds an optional tinted reading background (--ob-surface warm cream) and an optional reading-ruler (a translucent horizontal band that follows the line under the cursor). Mono/code stays mono. All purely typographic — zero layout breakage.

3) ADHD FOCUS MODE ([data-focus='on']): dims everything except the active learning card to 35% opacity + slight blur (a 'spotlight' on the current task); hides non-essential chrome (sidebars collapse, decorative textures off); forces reduced-motion; surfaces a single 'Next step' affordance; optional Pomodoro ring in the nav. Reduces choice and visual noise — the platform's active-learning thesis benefits directly. One concept on screen at a time.

4) COLORBLIND-SAFE (default-on philosophy, with [data-cvd='deut|prot|trit'] fine-tuning): status is NEVER conveyed by hue alone — every state carries an icon AND/OR shape AND/OR text (✓ success, ! warning, ✕ danger, i info). The semantic quad is chosen for separability across deuter/protan/tritan: green #2E8B57, amber #B7791F, red #D14343, blue #3F6FE0 differ in lightness as well as hue. The lime brand accent is decorative/focal only — it never alone encodes meaning. Concept-graph edges use line style (solid=prereq, dashed=related) plus color. Focus ring is blue, distinct from lime. CVD sub-modes shift the amber/green pair apart further (e.g. deut: nudge success toward teal, warning toward orange) via a token override layer.

CROSS-CUTTING: WCAG-AA minimum on all text/UI (most pairings hit AAA — see color usage notes); visible 3px blue focus ring + 2px offset on every interactive element; full keyboard operability and logical tab order; :focus-visible only (no mouse-focus rings); prefers-reduced-motion + prefers-contrast honored; min 44px touch targets; respects OS reduce-transparency by dropping glass to solid surfaces; all icons that carry meaning get aria-labels; live regions announce AI streaming completion and quiz results.

designSystem.summaryMarkdown:
## OpenBook Design System — Rationale

A complete, production-ready token set for **OpenBook**, an AI **active-learning** platform. It is engineered for the existing stack found in the repo (**Next.js 14 App Router + TypeScript + Tailwind 3.4 + framer-motion + Supabase**, CSS-variable theming already present in `tailwind.config.ts`/`globals.css`). The system encodes the Apple-HIG philosophy and the curated **design DNA** (tight-tracked bold display headlines, calm editorial whitespace, large rounded soft-shadow cards, floating glass pill nav, black/white pill CTAs, dotted-halftone texture, **one sparing lime pop**, connected concept nodes).

### The core idea: lime is a scalpel, not a paintbrush
`#C0FE6F` is gorgeous but high-luminance — flooding it kills contrast and cheapens the brand. So the accent is **structurally reserved**: primary CTAs are the **black pill (light) / white pill (dark)** seen in the references; brand lime appears **once per view** as the focal beat (active concept node, mastery ring, a single highlighted word, the correct-answer burst). System status uses a **separate, colorblind-safe quad** (green/amber/red/blue) so "brand accent" never collides with "success," and the **focus ring is blue** — unmistakable and never confused with lime.

### Dual mode, both first-class
| | Light | Dark |
|---|---|---|
| Canvas | warm paper `#F7F6F3` | `#1E1E1E` (one step under brand base) |
| Card | `#FFFFFF` | `#242424` (official base) |
| Elevated | `#FFFFFF` + stronger shadow | `#2C2C2C` (official base) |
| Primary CTA | black pill `#1A1A1A` | white pill `#F4F3EF` |
| Text primary | `#1A1A1A` (15.8:1) | `#F4F3EF` (14.9:1) |

Off-white/off-black (not `#FFF`/`#000`) reduce halation for the **weak-eyesight** market; dark mode leans on **borders + inner top-highlight** because shadows barely read on near-black, while light mode leans on **soft multi-layer shadows**.

### Contrast & colorblindness (WCAG-AA, mostly AAA)
Every text token is annotated with its measured ratio in the color tokens. Body/secondary text hits **AAA**; tertiary/status hit **AA**. Status is **never hue-only** — always icon + shape + text — and the semantic quad is chosen to separate by *lightness* as well as hue across deuter/protan/tritan, with `[data-cvd]` sub-mode overrides.

### Typography — SF Pro, done correctly
- **SF Pro Display** (Semibold/Bold) for `title-1`↑ and all `display-*`, with the **tight negative tracking** that is the brand's signature (down to −0.035em at 76px). **SF Pro Text** for `headline`↓. **SF Mono / ui-monospace** for code and **tabular numerals** (counters/timers/scores don't jitter while animating).
- We **do not ship SF Pro woff** (Apple-licensed). Strategy: `@font-face local()` bridge → real SF Pro on Apple devices, with `-apple-system`/`system-ui` fallback for everyone else. **No Inter/Roboto** (the existing Geist fonts are dropped) — that would violate the brand. A self-hosted **OpenDyslexic** (SIL-licensed, shippable) powers the dyslexia toggle via `[data-dyslexia]` without touching layout.

### Spacing, radii, elevation, motion
4px grid; concentric-corner radii (xs 6 → 2xl 36 → pill); six-tier soft elevation with a single sanctioned **lime focus-glow** (`e-accent`); glassmorphic tier for the pill nav/spatial panels. Motion is **short, eased, interruptible** (80–480ms, iOS-style `cubic-bezier(.2,0,0,1)`), with spring **only** for celebratory beats (mastery, correct answer, streak). Signature moments: sliding pill-nav puck (`layoutId`), concept-graph stroke-draw reveal, streaming-AI fade-in with a pulsing lime caret. Full **`prefers-reduced-motion`** story (collapses to ≤120ms cross-fades; disables confetti/shake/shimmer).

### Accessibility as a product pillar (not an afterthought)
Four composable modes on `<html>` data-attributes, persisted to Supabase:
1. **Reading Lens** — the signature flourish: a cursor-following **magnifier** (1.8–3×, scroll-to-resize) *or* **bold/boost** that thickens any hovered text — both implemented as **overlays / weight-only transitions so layout never reflows**.
2. **Dyslexia mode** — OpenDyslexic + looser tracking/leading + 66ch measure + reading ruler, purely typographic.
3. **ADHD focus mode** — spotlight the active learning card, dim/blur the rest, kill decorative texture, force reduced-motion, surface one "Next step." Directly serves the **active-learning thesis** (one concept at a time).
4. **Colorblind-safe** — always-on icon/shape encoding + `[data-cvd]` tuning + blue (non-lime) focus ring.

### Why this is "active learning," not a generator
The inventory includes **learning-native** components competitors lack: connected **concept-node graph** with mastery rings, **flashcard/quiz** with active-recall micro-interactions (confidence rating, correct/incorrect feedback), a **streaming chat tutor** with inline citations, and a **focus mode** that enforces single-task study. The tokens make the *interactive learning loop* — not one-shot output — feel premium, calm, and unmistakably Apple-grade.

### Build notes (wiring)
- Define every token as a CSS variable under `:root` (light) and `[data-theme='dark']` (dark) in `globals.css`; map semantic names in `tailwind.config.ts theme.extend.colors/boxShadow/borderRadius/fontFamily`.
- Set `--font-display/--font-text/--font-mono/--font-dyslexic` and reference via Tailwind `fontFamily`.
- Mode flags: `data-theme`, `data-dyslexia`, `data-focus`, `data-cvd`, `data-reduced-motion`, `data-lens` on `<html>` — composable and SSR-safe.
- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion`, `prefers-contrast`, and `prefers-reduced-transparency` (drop glass → solid).

features.feature: Q&A — Chat with material. A Socratic, citation-grounded chat that never just hands over the answer: it locates the exact source passage, decides whether to answer or to push the student to reason, and closes most exchanges with a one-tap understanding-check that converts the question into a retrieval-practice moment logged against the concept. Built on Next.js 14 (App Router) + Supabase (pgvector RAG, Auth, Realtime, Storage) + Claude (Haiku 4.5 router → Opus 4.8 streaming tutor → Sonnet 4.6 check grader).
features.oneLine: Chat that answers WITH the source, teaches you to find it, then makes you prove you got it — turning every question into a retrieval-practice rep.
features.coreInteraction:
The loop (5 steps, every turn is a learning rep, not a lookup):

1) ASK. Student types a question (or taps a suggested-question chip generated from the doc on empty state). Question is embedded and run against pgvector over the document's chunks; top-k (k=6) passages are retrieved with their source coordinates (page/section/char-span).

2) DIAGNOSE + ROUTE (invisible, ~300ms). Haiku 4.5 classifies the question via structured output into one of: factual-recall, conceptual-why, problem-solving, off-material, meta ("explain like I'm 5", "quiz me"). This routing decides the tutor's STANCE — it is the difference between OpenBook and a generator.

3) SOCRATIC ANSWER (streamed). Opus 4.8 answers, but stance-aware:
   - For conceptual-why / problem-solving it leads with a probe first ("Before I answer — what do you think happens to enzyme rate above optimal temp, and why?") and a 'Skip to answer' affordance for students who are stuck or time-pressed (ADHD-respecting — never a forced gate).
   - For factual-recall it answers directly but anchored: every claim is followed by an inline citation pill [1] that, on hover/tap, opens the exact source passage in the right rail with the sentence highlighted. No citation = the model is told to say "I can't find this in your material" rather than hallucinate.
   - Tone calibrated to the routed difficulty; math rendered as plain text unless the doc is LaTeX-native.

4) UNDERSTANDING-CHECK (the retention engine). After answering a conceptual/problem question, the tutor surfaces ONE inline check — a free-response "In your own words, why…?" or a 1-tap MCQ generated from the same passage. The student answers in the same chat. Sonnet 4.6 grades it against the cited passage (structured output: correct | partial | misconception + the specific gap + a targeted follow-up). A wrong answer does NOT just say "incorrect" — it re-explains the exact misconception and re-asks, so the loop closes on understanding, not on the student giving up.

5) LOG + SCHEDULE. The check result writes a mastery signal to concept_mastery (SM-2-style: ease, interval, next_review). The concept node tied to that passage updates its mastery ring in the document's concept map. Questions the student got wrong resurface later as spaced-review prompts on the dashboard. The chat is thus a feeder into the whole platform's retention system, not a dead-end transcript.

WHY THIS RETAINS (vs passive): three learning-science levers fire on every turn — (a) the generation effect (the probe in step 3 forces the student to attempt before seeing the answer), (b) retrieval practice + the testing effect (the check in step 4 is a low-stakes recall rep, the single most evidence-backed driver of long-term retention), and (c) spaced repetition (step 5 schedules the resurfacing). Competitors stop at step 3's answer; OpenBook makes the student DO something with it and remember it later.
features.claudeUsage:
Three-model pipeline, cheapest-model-that-meets-the-bar per task. SDK: @anthropic-ai/sdk 0.102 in Next.js Route Handlers (Node runtime). All doc context is referenced via the Files API (upload once at ingest, reference by file_id) with citations enabled, so we never re-upload and we get span-accurate source coordinates back.

1) ROUTER + RETRIEVAL-QUALITY GATE — claude-haiku-4-5. Non-streaming, structured output via output_config.format (json_schema, strict). Input: the question + a compact list of the 6 retrieved chunk headers. Output: { intent: enum, needs_socratic: bool, retrieval_confidence: 0-1, suggested_stance: enum }. Cheap, ~300ms, sets the whole turn's behavior. If retrieval_confidence < 0.4 we short-circuit to an honest "not in your material" instead of guessing.

2) SOCRATIC TUTOR — claude-opus-4-8, STREAMING (messages.stream, .finalMessage() to persist), thinking:{type:'adaptive'}, output_config.effort:'high'. Streaming is mandatory (long output + low TTFT for a chat UI). The retrieved passages ride as a `document` block with citations:{enabled:true} so Claude emits citation blocks bound to exact char spans — we map those to the [1] pills, no homegrown "find the quote" hack. System prompt (frozen, cache_control ephemeral 1h — prefix-stable so it caches): defines the Socratic contract — 'probe-before-answer for conceptual/problem intents; cite every factual claim to the provided passages; if a claim isn't supported, say so; never invent a citation; keep the probe to one question.' Stance + intent injected as a mid-conversation system message (role:"system" in messages, beta mid-conversation-system-2026-04-07) so per-turn routing never invalidates the cached system+document prefix. The document block is the cache anchor — the large stable prefix that makes multi-turn chat cheap.

3) CHECK GENERATOR + GRADER — claude-sonnet-4-6, structured output (strict json_schema), non-streaming. Generation: from the cited passage produce one check {type, prompt, options?, model_answer, target_concept_id}. Grading: input = student's free-response + the passage; output = { verdict: correct|partial|misconception, gap: string, reexplanation: string, followup_question: string|null, mastery_delta: number }. Sonnet (not Opus) because grading-against-a-given-passage is a well-scoped judgment task that doesn't need Opus; Sonnet hits the bar at 0.6x the cost. Adaptive thinking on for the misconception diagnosis.

Embeddings for RAG are NOT from Claude (Anthropic has no embeddings endpoint) — use an embedding provider at ingest, store vectors in pgvector. Claude is reserved for routing, tutoring, and grading.

Key prompt strategy: the citation contract is enforced structurally (Files API citations + 'no support → say so'), the Socratic stance is enforced by routing not by a vague 'be a good tutor' prompt, and the check is a separate strict-schema call so the UI can render deterministic MCQ/free-response widgets.

features.uiLayout:
Three-pane, calm-editorial, consistent with OpenBook DNA (SF Pro Display headers tight-tracked, SF Pro Text body, dark base #242424 / light off-white #F4F1EA, ONE green #C0FE6F accent used only on the active understanding-check and mastery rings). Large rounded cards, soft shadows, floating pill nav at top.

LEFT (collapsible, 240px): document switcher + the connected concept-node map (the learning-native graph from the DNA) — nodes glow their mastery ring as checks are logged; clicking a node filters chat history to that concept. This is the platform-tying surface.

CENTER (chat, max-w-3xl, generous whitespace): message stream. Student bubbles right-aligned, tutor responses left in a borderless editorial column (no chat-app cramping). Inline [1] citation pills are subtle dark/green-tinted chips. The understanding-check renders as a distinct rounded card with a hairline green left-border and a black pill 'Submit' CTA (light mode) — visually a moment, not just more text. Composer is a floating rounded pill at the bottom with a dotted-texture focus state.

RIGHT (source rail, 360px, slides in on citation hover/tap): renders the cited PDF/doc passage with the exact sentence highlighted in a soft green wash; page/section breadcrumb at top; 'open full document' link. Closes to reclaim space.

STATES:
- EMPTY: not a blank box. A warm 'Ask anything about [Document]' heading + 4 auto-generated starter-question chips (Haiku, generated at ingest) + a one-line 'I'll show you where the answer lives and check that it stuck.' This teaches the product's promise in the empty state.
- LOADING: router phase shows a slim shimmer line (no spinner); tutor phase streams tokens immediately (TTFT < 1s). Citation pills appear progressively as citation blocks stream in. Skeleton uses dotted-halftone shimmer, not a generic gray pulse.
- ERROR: retrieval-miss → honest card 'I couldn't find this in your material — want me to answer from general knowledge instead?' (explicit opt-in, never silent hallucination). API/stream error → inline 'Reconnecting…' with the partial response preserved and a retry pill; rate-limit → 'High demand, retrying' from the SDK's typed RateLimitError.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS (framer-motion): citation pill → source-rail highlight is a shared-layout slide; the check card has a satisfying spring on submit; a correct verdict pulses the concept-map ring once in green (the only celebratory green); 'Skip to answer' is a quiet ghost link, never a loud button — respecting students who need the answer now.
features.accessibilityNotes:
Accessibility is woven in, not bolted on — serving the ADHD/Dyslexia/Colorblind/Weak-eyesight secondary market within the same premium design:

- HOVER MAGNIFIER / BOLDER (universal tool, lives in the pill nav): hold a modifier or hover any text — chat, citation, source passage — to zoom or embolden it in place without breaking layout. Critical in the source rail where dense PDF text is the failure point for weak-eyesight users.
- DYSLEXIA MODE: toggles increased letter/word/line spacing (the dyslexia-friendly spacing from the brief) and an optional OpenDyslexic fallback face, applied via a data-attr on <html> so it cascades to chat AND rendered source text. Tutor prose already favors short sentences and chunked paragraphs, which helps.
- ADHD FOCUS MODE: dims everything except the current message + its check card, hides the concept map and history; the Socratic 'Skip to answer' affordance and one-question-at-a-time check design prevent the wall-of-text overwhelm that makes ADHD students bounce off competitors.
- COLORBLIND-SAFE: the green accent is never the sole carrier of meaning — check verdicts pair color with an icon + label (correct ✓ / partial ◐ / revisit ↻) and mastery rings use fill-fraction + numeric %, not hue alone. Citation pills are shape+number, not color-coded.
- Full keyboard loop (ask → read → open citation → answer check → next), ARIA-live on the streaming region so screen readers get tutor output incrementally, prefers-reduced-motion disables the spring/pulse animations, WCAG-AA contrast verified for both #242424 and #F4F1EA bases.
- Per-user preferences persist in user_prefs so the accommodation follows the student across sessions and documents.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The understanding-check that closes the loop. NotebookLM, StudyFetch, and YouLearn all stop at a cited answer — you ask, they emit, you scroll on and forget. OpenBook's chat refuses to end there: after answering, it makes you reason (Socratic probe before the answer) and then makes you prove it (a one-tap retrieval-practice check graded against the exact cited passage), and that result feeds a spaced-repetition schedule so wrong answers resurface later. The single sharpest line: competitors answer your question; OpenBook turns your question into a graded memory you'll actually retain — every chat turn is a logged retrieval-practice rep, not a disposable lookup. The Files-API span-accurate citation rail (no hallucinated quotes, 'not in your material' when unsupported) is table-stakes trust; the testing-effect loop is the moat.
features.activeLearningRationale: This design drives genuine retention because it engineers the three highest-leverage findings in the learning-science literature into the mandatory path of every turn, where competitors trigger zero of them. (1) Testing effect / retrieval practice: the step-4 understanding-check is a recall attempt, and recalling information strengthens memory far more than re-reading it — a passive generator that just shows a cited answer is re-reading, the weakest form of study. (2) Generation effect: the step-3 probe forces the student to produce an attempt before seeing the answer; self-generated answers (even wrong ones, when corrected) are retained better than received ones. (3) Spaced repetition: step-5 schedules wrong concepts to resurface, exploiting the spacing effect that one-shot exposure can't. Crucially, the misconception-aware grader closes the loop on understanding rather than on a binary 'wrong' — it re-explains the specific gap and re-asks, which is the corrective-feedback condition under which testing actually helps. The result is not a chat transcript you abandon; it's a stream of graded reps wired into the concept map and review schedule. A passive 'upload → cited answer' generator cannot produce retention because it never asks the student to retrieve, generate, or revisit — it optimizes for the feeling of understanding (fluency), which is exactly the illusion retrieval practice is known to break.
features.buildNotes:
Concrete implementation notes / gotchas:

INGEST (prereq, shared with other features): on upload, store the file in Supabase Storage, push it to Claude Files API once (persist the returned file_id on documents.claude_file_id — reference forever, never re-upload), chunk the text (~500 tokens, section-aware), embed each chunk with your embedding provider, store vectors in pgvector (documents/chunks tables, ivfflat or hnsw index on the vector column). Generate the 4 starter-question chips + initial concept nodes here with one Haiku call.

RAG: pgvector cosine query in a Postgres RPC (match_chunks(query_embedding, doc_id, k)) — keep it in the DB, return chunk text + page/section/char_start/char_end so the source rail can highlight precisely. Pair with the Files-API citation blocks: the doc block gives Claude span-accurate citing; pgvector decides WHICH spans are in-context. Use both.

CLAUDE CALLS live in Route Handlers (app/api/chat/route.ts), Node runtime, stream back to the client with a ReadableStream / Server-Sent Events; persist the final message + citation map with stream.finalMessage(). Do NOT call Claude from the browser (key exposure).

CACHING: system prompt + the document block are the stable prefix → cache_control:{type:'ephemeral', ttl:'1h'} on the last system block so multi-turn chat over the same doc is ~90% cheaper. Per-turn stance/intent goes in a role:"system" message inside messages[] (beta mid-conversation-system-2026-04-07) so it does NOT invalidate the cached prefix — verify cache_read_input_tokens > 0 in dev. Opus 4.8 has a 4096-token minimum cacheable prefix; the document block clears it easily.

MODELS: exact IDs claude-opus-4-8 / claude-sonnet-4-6 / claude-haiku-4-5 (no date suffixes). Opus 4.8: adaptive thinking only — budget_tokens / temperature / top_p will 400. max_tokens ~64000 with streaming for the tutor.

STRUCTURED OUTPUT: router and grader use output_config.format with strict json_schema; validate the returned JSON with zod (already a dep) before trusting it. Handle stop_reason 'refusal' (surface gracefully) and 'max_tokens' (rare for checks).

REALTIME: Supabase Realtime on messages + concept_mastery so the concept-map mastery rings update live across the student's open tabs/devices the instant a check is graded.

SECURITY/RLS: every table keyed by user_id with Row-Level Security; @supabase/ssr for auth in Route Handlers. Never let one student read another's chat or mastery rows.

LIBS: framer-motion (already installed) for the citation→rail shared-layout transition and the check spring; lucide-react for the verdict icons (colorblind-safe pairing); clsx + tailwind-merge for the dyslexia/focus-mode data-attr class toggles.

features.feature: Summary — a progressive, three-layer "Study Ladder" that turns any uploaded source into an actively-recalled, source-anchored map of understanding instead of a wall of text.
features.oneLine: Not a generated summary you read — a layered study ladder you climb, where every point is earned with a recall checkpoint and one-tap traceable to its exact source line.
features.coreInteraction:
THE LOOP (one "rung" at a time — the student is never handed the whole summary at once):

1. ORIENT (Layer 0 — the one-liner). On open, the student sees a single tight-tracked thesis sentence for the whole document plus a "concept spine": 5–9 connected nodes (the key ideas) rendered as a vertical/branching map (DNA ref: connected concept nodes). Everything below is collapsed. This is the "you are here" — it primes a schema before detail, which is the single biggest lever for retention (advance organizer effect).

2. PREDICT-BEFORE-REVEAL (the active-recall hook). To expand any key-idea node into its Layer 1 "key ideas" bullets, the student must first make a lightweight commitment: either (a) tap "I think I know this" / "Show me", or (b) for nodes flagged high-yield, answer a one-tap prediction ("Before you read: does X increase or decrease Y?" — 2–3 choices). The bullets only render AFTER the commitment. This converts passive expansion into a retrieval attempt — the generation effect — so the reveal lands on a primed brain, not a skimming one.

3. REVEAL + SELF-EXPLAIN (Layer 1). The key-idea bullets stream in. Each bullet carries (i) a source chip ("p.4 ¶2") and (ii) a confidence toggle the student sets: Got it / Shaky / Lost. "Shaky" and "Lost" bullets are visually pulled forward (the ADHD focus mode dims the rest) and queued for the checkpoint.

4. DEEP-DIVE ON DEMAND (Layer 2). Any bullet expands once more into a deep-dive: a tighter explanation, an analogy, and the verbatim source excerpt inline (one-tap "expand to source"). Layer 2 is generated lazily, only for what the student actually opens — this is both pedagogically right (don't drown them) and cost-right (don't generate what's never read).

5. CHECKPOINT (active-recall gate). After every cluster of ~3 key ideas, a checkpoint blocks forward progress: 2–3 free-recall or cloze questions generated from exactly the bullets just revealed, weighted toward whatever the student marked Shaky/Lost. They type/speak an answer; Claude grades it against the source and returns targeted feedback ("close — you missed the *threshold* condition, see p.4"). A miss re-surfaces that node at the top of the next session (spaced retrieval). The checkpoint is the thing that makes this a learning loop and not a generator: you cannot collect the whole summary without proving recall along the way.

6. CONSOLIDATE. At the end, the spine redraws colored by mastery (green = recalled, amber = shaky, the lime accent reserved ONLY for "next best thing to review"). The student gets a 20-second "teach-back": one prompt to summarize the whole doc in their own words, graded for coverage against the spine. That self-generated summary — not Claude's — is saved as their artifact.

WHY THIS RETAINS vs PASSIVE READING: every layer transition is gated by an act of retrieval or commitment (predict, self-rate, recall, teach-back). The student generates and tests, the system only confirms and corrects. Three mechanisms with the strongest evidence base — advance organizers (Layer 0 spine), the testing/generation effect (checkpoints + predict-before-reveal), and spaced retrieval (misses resurface) — are structural, not optional add-ons.
features.claudeUsage:
MODELS (cheapest-that-clears-the-bar per task; all model IDs verified against the bundled claude-api reference):

1. EXTRACTION / SPINE + LAYER-0/1 GENERATION — `claude-opus-4-8`, streaming + structured outputs.
   - Why Opus: this is the one-shot that defines the entire learning object (thesis, the 5–9 concept nodes, their edges, per-node key-idea bullets, AND a precise source anchor for every bullet). Anchoring quality and pedagogical sequencing are the whole product; a weaker model produces plausible-but-misattributed citations, which destroys the "tap-to-source" trust contract. Worth Opus once per document.
   - `thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`, `output_config: {effort: "high"}` — adaptive lets it reason over structure without a fixed budget (budget_tokens is removed on 4.8).
   - STRUCTURED OUTPUT via `output_config.format` (json_schema, strict) so the spine/bullets come back as validated JSON, not prose to re-parse. Schema below.
   - STREAMING (`messages.stream` / `.finalMessage()`): doc context can be large and we want the spine to paint progressively in the UI; also avoids the SDK's non-stream timeout guard at high max_tokens (set max_tokens ~32–64k, stream).
   - SOURCE ANCHORING uses native CITATIONS: send the document as a `document` content block with `citations: {enabled: true}` (via Files API `file_id`, beta `files-api-2025-04-14`), so each bullet's source chip is backed by a real char-range citation, not a hallucinated page number. (Note: citations are incompatible with output_config.format on the SAME call — so we run anchoring as a second pass: pass 1 = structured spine/bullets via output_config.format; pass 2 = a citations-enabled call that maps each bullet's text to exact source spans. Two calls, both Opus, both cacheable.)
   - PROMPT CACHING: the document block + system prompt get `cache_control: {type:"ephemeral"}`. Layer-2 deep-dives and checkpoint generation reuse this cached prefix → ~0.1x input cost on every subsequent call for the same doc within TTL.

2. LAYER-2 DEEP-DIVE (lazy, per-bullet) — `claude-haiku-4-5`, streaming.
   - Why Haiku: input is one already-extracted bullet + its cached source excerpt; the task is "explain tighter + give one analogy." Low-stakes, latency-sensitive (student is waiting on a tap), high-volume. Haiku clears it. Reuses the cached document prefix.

3. CHECKPOINT QUESTION GENERATION — `claude-haiku-4-5`, structured output (json_schema array of {type: cloze|free_recall, prompt, expected_points[], source_anchor}). Generated from the just-revealed bullets (which are in cached context). Cheap and frequent.

4. CHECKPOINT + TEACH-BACK GRADING — `claude-sonnet-4-6`, structured output.
   - Why Sonnet not Haiku: grading a free-recall answer against source for partial credit and generating *useful corrective feedback* ("you missed the threshold condition") is the quality bar that makes the loop teach rather than just mark right/wrong. Sonnet is the sweet spot; Haiku under-grades nuance, Opus is overkill per-checkpoint. Structured output returns {verdict, missed_points[], feedback, resurface: bool}.

KEY PROMPT STRATEGY: system prompt forbids inventing facts not in the source and forbids a citation it can't ground; instructs Opus to sequence nodes as an *advance organizer* (general→specific) and to write Layer-1 bullets as testable claims (so checkpoints can be generated from them). For grading, the rubric is passed as expected_points so Sonnet grades coverage, not phrasing — critical for dyslexic/ADHD students who recall the idea but not the wording.
features.uiLayout:
DESIGN DNA APPLIED (cream #F4F1EA / dark #242424 base, SF Pro Display headings tight-tracked, SF Pro Text body, large rounded soft-shadow cards, floating pill nav, black pill CTAs in light mode, ONE lime #C0FE6F accent reserved for "review next"):

- TOP: floating pill nav (wordmark "OpenBook" only) over a calm whitespace canvas with a faint dotted/halftone texture in the gutters.
- HERO OF THE VIEW (Layer 0): the document thesis as one large tight-tracked black headline, centered, generous whitespace — reads like an editorial title, not a summary box. Below it, the CONCEPT SPINE: rounded node cards connected by thin curved edges (the connected-nodes DNA), arranged vertically on mobile / branching on desktop. Each node card = soft shadow, large radius, the key idea label + a small mastery dot.
- LEFT RAIL (desktop): slim progress rail showing rungs climbed / checkpoints passed, plus the "review next" item glowing in lime.
- EXPANSION: tapping a node expands it IN PLACE into a stack of key-idea bullet cards (accordion, springy 220ms ease-out). Each bullet card has a source chip (pill, monospace "p.4 ¶2"), a 3-state confidence segmented control, and a chevron to open Layer 2. Layer 2 slides the verbatim source excerpt in as an inset card with a subtle left border.
- CHECKPOINT: rises as a focused modal-sheet (glassmorphic, premium) that dims everything behind it — doubles as ADHD focus mode. Big input, generous line spacing.

ACCESSIBILITY TOOLBAR (floating, bottom-center pill like the inspiration's dock): Magnifier/Bold lens, Focus mode, Dyslexia spacing, Colorblind palette, TTS. (Detailed below.)

EMPTY STATE: no source yet → centered dotted-outline drop card "Drop a PDF, paste a YouTube link, or connect Drive" with the black pill CTA; a faint ghost of the concept-spine behind it so the value is visually pre-promised.

LOADING STATE: NOT a spinner. The thesis headline streams in token-by-token first, then node cards pop in one at a time with a soft scale-up as the structured spine streams — the student watches the map being built (progress as content, per streaming best practice). Skeleton bullet cards shimmer under collapsed nodes.

ERROR STATES: (a) extraction failed / unreadable PDF → inline card "We couldn't read 3 pages — they may be scanned images. Retry with OCR?" (we re-send those pages as images to Opus vision). (b) citation grounding failed for a bullet → the source chip shows a muted "unverified" state instead of a fake page number — we NEVER show a citation we couldn't ground (trust contract). (c) grading API error at a checkpoint → student is let through with a soft "we'll re-check this later" rather than blocking on a 529; the bullet is silently queued for re-grade.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS: predict-before-reveal choices animate the correct/incorrect with a single accent pulse (no red/green reliance — colorblind-safe, uses icon + position); confidence toggle haptic on mobile; spine redraw at the end animates each node settling into its mastery color; "review next" node has a slow lime breathing glow — the only place the accent appears.
features.activeLearningRationale:
A passive generator (NotebookLM/StudyFetch) does: upload → emit summary → student reads → forgets. Reading a good summary feels like learning (fluency illusion) but produces almost no durable retention. This design makes retention structural by gating every information reveal behind an act the learner must perform:

- ADVANCE ORGANIZER (Layer 0 spine first, detail collapsed): the brain encodes detail far better when a schema exists to hang it on. We show the map before the territory — by construction, not as an optional toggle.
- GENERATION + TESTING EFFECT (predict-before-reveal + checkpoints): the student commits a prediction or retrieves an answer BEFORE/AFTER each chunk. Retrieval practice is the most replicated finding in learning science; a single successful recall beats multiple re-reads. Our checkpoints are not a bonus quiz at the end — they are the turnstiles between layers.
- DESIRABLE DIFFICULTY via self-rating: the Got it/Shaky/Lost toggle forces metacognitive judgment (students are terrible at this; making it one tap and then immediately testing it calibrates them).
- SPACED RETRIEVAL: misses and "Lost" nodes resurface at the top of the next session, colored in the lime "review next" accent — the system enforces spacing the student would never self-impose.
- TEACH-BACK: the saved artifact is the STUDENT's own summary, graded for coverage — protégé effect. The competitor's output is the product; here the student's output is the product, and Claude is the coach.

Net: Claude never just hands over knowledge. It withholds, prompts, tests, and corrects — the exact loop a good tutor runs.
features.accessibilityNotes:
The secondary market is served INSIDE the core loop, not bolted on:

- ADHD: Focus mode dims everything except the active node/bullet (reuses the checkpoint's glassmorphic dimming). Progressive disclosure itself is an ADHD accommodation — only ~3 ideas on screen before a checkpoint resets attention, so the student is never facing a 2,000-word wall. Checkpoints chunk the session into short, completable wins with visible progress on the left rail (dopamine of completion). The lime "review next" is the single attention anchor — exactly one thing glows.
- DYSLEXIA: a one-tap Dyslexia-spacing mode increases letter/word/line spacing and switches body to a dyslexia-friendly weight while PRESERVING the SF Pro layout (spacing tokens only, no font swap that breaks the design). Crucially, grading is coverage-based against expected_points, so a student who recalls the *idea* but misspells or rephrases still gets credit — dyslexic learners aren't penalized for orthography. TTS reads any layer aloud.
- COLORBLIND: mastery states never rely on color alone — green/amber/lime each pair with a distinct icon and position (check / wave / star). Predict-before-reveal feedback uses icon+motion, not red/green. A colorblind-safe palette toggle swaps amber→blue-ramp. The lime accent is used sparingly and always with shape redundancy.
- WEAK EYESIGHT: the signature OpenBook hover Magnifier/Bold lens zooms or bolds ANY text anywhere — source excerpts, bullets, checkpoint questions — without altering the page layout (an overlay lens, not a reflow). Pairs with system text-scaling; all hit targets are pill-sized (44px+).

All four toggles live in the floating accessibility dock and persist to user.preferences, so the loop adapts the moment the student opens any source.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The sharpest single differentiator: SOURCE-ANCHORED PROGRESSIVE DISCLOSURE GATED BY RECALL. Competitors emit a flat summary you passively read and have to trust. OpenBook's summary is (1) layered so you climb it one earned rung at a time, (2) impossible to fully unlock without passing recall checkpoints along the way, and (3) every single point is one tap from the verbatim source line that proves it — backed by real Claude citations, never a hallucinated page number. NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn give you an answer; OpenBook makes you generate the answer, tests that you kept it, and shows you exactly where it came from. The summary is not the output — your retained understanding is.
features.buildNotes:
DATA MODEL (Supabase / Postgres + pgvector):

- sources: id, user_id, title, kind (pdf|docx|pptx|gdoc|image|youtube|github), storage_path, anthropic_file_id, page_count, char_count, status (uploading|extracting|ready|error), created_at.
- source_chunks: id, source_id, chunk_index, page, paragraph, char_start, char_end, text, embedding vector(1536). (Backs anchoring + RAG for checkpoints; pgvector.)
- summaries: id, source_id, user_id, thesis (text, the Layer-0 one-liner), model, status, created_at. One per (source,user) regenerable version.
- concept_nodes: id, summary_id, order_index, label, layer0_role (general→specific rank), parent_node_id (nullable, for the branching spine edges), mastery (untouched|got_it|shaky|lost|recalled), is_high_yield bool.
- node_edges: id, summary_id, from_node_id, to_node_id (explicit spine connections for the node graph).
- key_ideas (Layer 1): id, node_id, order_index, claim (text, written as a testable statement), source_chunk_id (FK → exact anchor), citation_char_start, citation_char_end, citation_verified bool, confidence (got_it|shaky|lost|null).
- deep_dives (Layer 2): id, key_idea_id, explanation, analogy, source_excerpt, generated_at (nullable until first opened — lazy).
- checkpoints: id, summary_id, after_node_order, status (pending|passed|failed), created_at.
- checkpoint_questions: id, checkpoint_id, key_idea_id, type (cloze|free_recall), prompt, expected_points jsonb, source_anchor.
- checkpoint_attempts: id, question_id, user_id, answer_text, verdict (correct|partial|miss), missed_points jsonb, feedback, resurface bool, attempted_at.
- teachbacks: id, summary_id, user_id, student_text, coverage_score, missing_node_ids jsonb, created_at. (The student's saved artifact.)
- review_queue: id, user_id, node_id, due_at, reason (miss|lost|spaced). Drives the lime "review next" and next-session resurfacing.
- user_preferences: user_id, focus_mode, dyslexia_spacing, colorblind_palette, magnifier, tts, theme (light|dark). RLS on every table by user_id. Realtime channel on summaries/concept_nodes so the spine paints live as Opus streams structured output server-side.

PIPELINE / GOTCHAS:
- Ingest in a Next.js route handler / edge function: PDF/Office/gdoc → text + page/paragraph offsets (unstructured or pdf parse), store chunks + embeddings. YouTube → transcript with timestamps as "paragraphs". GitHub/.ipynb/.md → file/cell as chunks. Scanned pages with no text layer → send page images to Opus vision (OCR fallback, drives the error-state retry).
- Two-pass Opus (structured spine, then citations grounding) because output_config.format and citations can't combine on one call — set citation_verified=false if pass 2 can't ground a bullet, and render the "unverified" chip (never fake it).
- Stream the structured spine server-side and write rows as nodes complete, pushing via Supabase Realtime so the UI pops nodes in (the loading-as-content state).
- Cache the document content block (cache_control ephemeral, 1h TTL since a study session spans >5min) so Layer-2 (Haiku), checkpoint-gen (Haiku), and grading (Sonnet) all hit ~0.1x input cost. Verify via usage.cache_read_input_tokens.
- Grading and teach-back via messages.parse / output_config.format strict schemas; on 529/timeout let the student through and queue re-grade (error state c) rather than blocking the loop.
- Reserve the lime accent in CSS to a single token used only by review_queue's top item.

features.feature: Quizzes — an adaptive diagnostic quizzing engine that doesn't just grade you; it diagnoses your weak concepts, explains every miss against the exact source passage, and converts each gap into a spaced-repetition flashcard. Built on OpenBook's per-document concept graph so every question is tagged to a concept node and a source span.
features.oneLine: A diagnostic quiz that hunts your weak concepts, explains every wrong answer against its source passage, and auto-spawns flashcards from your misses — so the quiz is the start of studying, not a one-shot score.
features.coreInteraction:
The student goes through a continuous diagnose -> attempt -> immediate-feedback -> repair -> re-test loop, not a take-test-then-see-score flow:

1) ENTRY / CALIBRATION. From a document (or chapter), the student picks a focus or accepts "Diagnose me." OpenBook pulls the document's concept nodes (built at upload) and serves a short 5-question calibration spread, one item per concept, mixed difficulty. No score is shown yet — framed as "Let's find your edges."

2) PER-QUESTION ATTEMPT WITH PREDICTION. Each question appears one at a time on a large rounded card. Before answering, the student drags a confidence slider (Guessing / Unsure / Confident). This confidence-vs-correctness signal is the diagnostic core: a confident-wrong answer flags a misconception (high priority), an unsure-right flags fragile knowledge. Item types: MCQ, multi-select, short free-text (Claude-graded), and "locate it" (highlight the supporting sentence in an inline source snippet).

3) IMMEDIATE ADAPTIVE FEEDBACK (the retention moment). On submit, the card flips. Right answers get a one-line reinforce. Wrong/fragile answers stream a targeted explanation that (a) names the misconception, (b) contrasts the student's chosen answer with the correct one, (c) shows the exact source passage with the supporting sentence highlighted and a "jump to source" link, and (d) offers a 10-second "try the repair" micro-retry (a reworded variant) so the student re-encodes immediately rather than reading passively.

4) ADAPTIVE BRANCHING. After each item, the engine re-weights: a missed concept triggers 1–2 follow-up items at adjusted difficulty later in the session (interleaved, not back-to-back, to force retrieval). Mastered concepts are dropped. The student sees a live "concept map" rail where nodes shift color as evidence accumulates (weak -> shaky -> solid) — concrete proof the quiz is targeting them.

5) MISS -> FLASHCARD. Every wrong/fragile item auto-creates a flashcard (front = reworded prompt, back = the explanation + source cite) dropped into a spaced-repetition deck. The student can edit inline or dismiss. This is the bridge from "I got it wrong" to "I will see this again on the right day."

6) DEBRIEF + SCHEDULED RETURN. The end screen is a diagnosis, not a grade: per-concept mastery bars, the top 2 misconceptions in plain language, "N flashcards created," and a single CTA — "Re-quiz weak concepts in 2 days" (writes a due date). The score is secondary and shown small.

Why retention not consumption: the loop forces (a) a retrieval attempt before any answer is shown, (b) a metacognitive confidence prediction that surfaces illusions of knowing, (c) immediate corrective feedback at the exact moment of error with the source, (d) an active repair re-encode, (e) interleaved re-testing, and (f) spaced follow-up via flashcards. Competitors emit a quiz and a score; OpenBook runs the full testing-effect + spacing + interleaving + error-driven-correction loop.
features.claudeUsage:
THREE distinct Claude calls, each model picked to the quality bar:

1) QUESTION GENERATION (batch, structured output) — claude-opus-4-8. Run once per document/chapter at quiz start (and cached). Input: concept nodes + their source chunks (retrieved via pgvector from the doc's embeddings) + the student's prior mastery record. Why Opus: writing genuinely diagnostic distractors that target named misconceptions (not trivially-wrong filler) is the hard quality bar that separates OpenBook from competitors' shallow quizzes; weak distractors kill the diagnostic. Uses structured outputs (output_config.format with a json_schema) so every item returns {stem, type, options[], correct, concept_id, source_chunk_id, supporting_span:{start,end}, target_misconception, difficulty}. thinking:{type:"adaptive"}, effort:"high". NOT streamed (batch, schema-constrained). Prompt-cache the per-document concept+source prefix so re-quizzes and follow-ups are ~90% cheaper.

2) FREE-TEXT GRADING + EXPLANATION (per-miss, streaming) — claude-sonnet-4-6. Fired only when the student submits a free-text answer or gets any item wrong/fragile. Input: the item, the student's exact answer, their confidence, the correct answer, and the source passage. Streamed token-by-token (client.messages.stream) so the explanation appears live the instant the card flips — the feedback feels immediate, which is when re-encoding lands. Sonnet, not Opus: grading-against-a-rubric + explaining one known item is well within Sonnet's bar at a third of the cost and lower latency, and this is the highest-frequency call. Structured-ish: a strict tool `grade_and_explain` returns {is_correct, partial_credit, misconception_label, flashcard_front, flashcard_back} while the prose explanation streams as text.

3) ADAPTIVE NEXT-ITEM SELECTION — no LLM. Pure server logic over the confidence×correctness signal and concept mastery (a lightweight Elo/BKT update), so branching is instant and free. Claude is reserved for the two tasks only it can do well (authoring diagnostic items, explaining errors).

Key prompt strategy: pass the concept graph + source spans as a cached system prefix; instruct Opus to make each distractor map to a specific, named misconception and to cite the exact supporting span by chunk offset (so the UI can highlight it deterministically rather than re-searching). For grading, give Sonnet the rubric and the source so it grades on substance, never surface wording, and explicitly forbid it from revealing the answer in the flashcard front.

dataModel.notes: One row per quiz session. status drives resume. score is secondary, shown small in debrief.
Generated by Opus with json_schema structured output. supporting_span resolves to real chunk offsets — items with unresolvable spans are dropped server-side to prevent hallucinated citations.

columns: repaired boolean (took the micro-retry & got it)
dataModel.notes: confidence x is_correct is the diagnostic signal. Persisted per-item (optimistic) so a dropped session resumes exactly.
Updated by server-side adaptive engine after every attempt (no LLM). Drives concept-map rail colors, branching priority, and the 'Re-quiz in 2 days' due date.
Auto-created from every miss/fragile item (front/back authored by Sonnet in the grade call). Shared with the Flashcards feature's deck; quiz misses start with lower ease so they resurface sooner.
EXISTING table from upload-time ingestion. Reused, not created here — provides the node set + edges for the concept-map rail.
EXISTING table. Provides source passages and exact offsets for deterministic supporting-span highlighting; pgvector used to retrieve relevant chunks per concept at generation time.

features.uiLayout:
Consistent with OpenBook DNA: calm editorial canvas, one large rounded quiz card center-stage, floating pill nav, sparing single green (#C0FE6F) accent, soft shadows, subtle dotted texture in the margins. Both light (#FAFAF7 canvas, black pill CTAs) and dark (#242424 base) modes.

LAYOUT (active quiz): three zones. (a) Center: ONE big rounded card (~720px max-width) holding the current question — tight-tracked SF Pro Display stem, generous whitespace, answer options as large tappable rows. Below the stem, a horizontal confidence slider with three labeled stops. (b) Right rail (collapsible): the live CONCEPT MAP — connected nodes (the doc's concept graph) where each node is a small pill that animates weak->shaky->solid as you answer; the single green accent is reserved for the node you're currently on. This is the "learning-native connected-nodes" DNA made functional. (c) Top: a thin floating pill showing progress as concept coverage ("4 of 7 concepts probed"), NOT "question 3/10" — reinforces it's a diagnosis. The feedback state replaces the option rows in-place with: misconception headline, your-answer-vs-correct contrast chips, the source passage in a quoted block with the supporting sentence highlighted in a soft green wash, a "jump to source" link, and a slim "Try the repair" button.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS: card flip on submit (spring, ~280ms); confidence slider haptic-style snap; concept node color-morph with a subtle pulse when its evidence updates; correct answers get a single restrained green check (no confetti — Apple-calm); flashcard creation animates a small card peeling off the feedback panel into a "Deck +1" pill.

EMPTY STATE: if the document has no concept graph yet (just uploaded), show "Building your concept map…" with a calm skeleton of nodes wiring up, plus a fallback "Quiz the whole document" that generates from raw chunks. If no documents at all: an editorial prompt to upload, with the black pill CTA.

LOADING: question generation shows the quiz card as a soft shimmer skeleton (stem lines + option rows) — never a spinner. Streaming explanation renders progressively with a caret. Concept rail shows nodes greyed until first probe.

ERROR: generation failure -> inline card "Couldn't build questions from this section" with a Retry pill and a "use a different chapter" link; never a dead end. Free-text grading timeout -> fall back to showing the model answer + source and still create the flashcard, with a quiet "graded offline" note. Network drop mid-quiz -> answers are persisted per-item (optimistic, server-confirmed) so the session resumes exactly where it left off.
features.claudeModel: Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8) for diagnostic question authoring; Sonnet 4.6 (claude-sonnet-4-6) for streaming free-text grading + miss explanations; no LLM for adaptive next-item selection (server-side BKT/Elo). Opus where distractor quality is the differentiator, Sonnet for the high-frequency grade/explain bar, code for instant branching.
features.accessibilityNotes: ADHD: a Focus Mode that dims everything except the active question card and shows one item at a time with no visible countdown timer (timers spike anxiety and hurt diagnosis); the "concept coverage" framing replaces an endless-feeling question count so progress feels bounded and finite; the repair micro-retry gives a fast win loop that sustains attention. DYSLEXIA: dyslexia-friendly mode increases letter/line spacing and optional OpenDyslexic toggle on the quiz card; free-text answers are graded on meaning so spelling never costs points; explanations are short, chunked, one idea per line. COLORBLIND: concept mastery never relies on color alone — weak/shaky/solid carry distinct icons + fill patterns + text labels alongside the color, and the palette is a colorblind-safe lime/amber/slate ramp (the green accent is paired with a check glyph). WEAK EYESIGHT: the global hover-magnifier/bold tool works on the quiz stem, options, and source passage (zoom or bold any text in place without breaking layout); minimum 17px body, large tap targets (option rows are full-width), high-contrast mode honored. All states are keyboard-navigable (number keys select options, Enter submits, confidence slider arrow-key adjustable) and screen-reader labeled (each option announces its letter; feedback announces correct/incorrect first, then the explanation).
features.activeLearningRationale: The design is engineered around the four most evidence-backed retention mechanisms and deliberately blocks passive consumption. (1) Testing effect: the student must produce a retrieval attempt before any answer is visible — no answer is shown until they commit, so they can't read-and-nod. (2) Metacognitive calibration: the pre-answer confidence slider exposes the "illusion of knowing" — a confident-wrong answer is the single highest-value learning event, and the system treats it as such by routing it to immediate correction + a flashcard. (3) Error-driven correction at the moment of failure: feedback is immediate, names the specific misconception, and is anchored to the exact source span — corrective feedback is far stickier when delivered at the instant of error with the original evidence, and the 10-second "repair" re-test forces an active re-encode rather than passive reading. (4) Spacing + interleaving: missed concepts return interleaved later in the session (forcing fresh retrieval, not echo) and become spaced-repetition flashcards with a real due date, so a single miss turns into a scheduled future retrieval. A generator (NotebookLM/StudyFetch) ends at the score; OpenBook's loop only begins there — the quiz's product is a diagnosis, a corrected misconception, and a scheduled return, none of which a one-shot quiz produces.
features.buildNotes:
Stack: Next.js 14 App Router + TS + Tailwind + Supabase + Anthropic SDK (@anthropic-ai/sdk).

- Concept graph + source spans are produced at UPLOAD time (separate ingestion feature), stored as concepts + chunks rows with pgvector embeddings; Quizzes consumes them. If absent, fall back to raw-chunk generation.
- Question generation: server action / route handler calls Opus with output_config.format json_schema; validate the returned items against the schema server-side, drop any item whose supporting_span doesn't resolve to a real chunk offset (guard against hallucinated cites). Prompt-cache the per-document concept+chunk prefix (cache_control ephemeral) — re-quizzes and follow-up generations hit the cache (~90% cheaper). Generate in small batches (5–7) so the student starts fast; generate follow-ups lazily as branching demands them.
- Free-text grade/explain: stream from a route handler (Edge/Node) using client.messages.stream over SSE to the client; persist the final structured result (is_correct, misconception_label, flashcard_front/back) on stream end. Set a ~12s timeout with the offline-grade fallback described in error states.
- Adaptive engine: implement a small BKT or Elo update per concept in server code; store after every attempt so a dropped session resumes. Confidence × correctness feeds priority (confident-wrong = top). Interleave follow-ups by inserting them ≥2 items downstream.
- Supabase Realtime optional for syncing the concept-map rail across devices; not required for v1.
- Determinism: have Opus cite supporting_span by chunk_id + char offsets so the UI highlights without a second search — never re-run similarity search at render time.
- Flashcards write into the existing spaced-repetition deck table (shared with the Flashcards feature) with an SM-2-style due date; quiz misses set ease lower so they surface sooner.
- Cost guard: cache hits + Sonnet-for-grading keep per-quiz cost low; cap follow-up generations per session. Tables needed (returned here since dataModel must stay a string-free array): quizzes(id,user_id,document_id,scope,status,created_at,completed_at,score); quiz_items(id,quiz_id,concept_id,chunk_id,type,stem,options_jsonb,correct_jsonb,supporting_span_jsonb,target_misconception,difficulty,is_followup,interleave_after); quiz_attempts(id,quiz_item_id,user_id,answer_jsonb,confidence,is_correct,partial_credit,misconception_label,latency_ms,created_at); concept_mastery(id,user_id,concept_id,mastery_score,evidence_count,last_seen,next_due); flashcards(id,user_id,concept_id,source_quiz_item_id,front,back,source_chunk_id,ease,interval_days,due_at,created_at). Reuses existing concepts(id,document_id,label,summary) and chunks(id,document_id,content,embedding,char_start,char_end).
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The sharpest single differentiator: a wrong answer is never a dead end — it is instantly explained against the exact source sentence that proves the right answer, then converted into a scheduled flashcard, all anchored to a per-concept mastery model that drives what you see next. NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn emit a quiz and a score and stop; OpenBook treats the score as the least important output and the corrected misconception + scheduled return as the product. No competitor closes the loop from miss -> source-grounded explanation -> auto-flashcard -> spaced re-quiz of that specific weak concept.

features.feature: Flashcards — true spaced-repetition active recall, auto-generated from material + quiz misses + highlights, with confidence-rated retrieval (FSRS scheduler).
features.oneLine: A spaced-repetition engine that makes you RETRIEVE before it ever reveals — you commit an answer and rate your confidence, FSRS schedules the next sighting, and weak cards get rewritten by Claude on the spot instead of just repeating.
features.coreInteraction:
The loop is built around the single rule that separates retention from passive review: NO PASSIVE FLIPPING. The card never auto-reveals; the student must produce a retrieval attempt first.

STUDY SESSION LOOP (one card):
1. PROMPT — A large rounded card shows ONLY the front (a question, cloze deletion, or "term → define"). A subtle dotted-texture progress rail at top shows cards-due-today shrinking. No "Flip" button is visible yet — this is deliberate friction that forces recall.
2. COMMIT TO RECALL — The student is required to act before seeing the answer. Two supported retrieval modes per card (chosen by card type):
   - Type-to-recall: a text field ("Type what you remember, even partially"). On submit, Claude (Haiku) grades the free-text attempt against the canonical answer for semantic match, not string match.
   - Reveal-then-self-grade: for diagram/visual/long cards, a single "I've recalled it — reveal" press. The press itself is logged with reveal-latency (time-to-reveal is a proxy for retrieval fluency).
   The student CANNOT skip this step; the reveal is gated behind the attempt.
3. REVEAL + CONFIDENCE GRADE — The back flips in (framer-motion 3D flip, reduced to a cross-fade when prefers-reduced-motion). Below it, FOUR confidence buttons map directly to FSRS grades: "Forgot" (Again=1), "Hard" (2), "Good" (3), "Easy" (4). For type-to-recall cards, Claude's semantic verdict pre-selects a suggested grade (e.g. "You got the mechanism but missed the enzyme — suggest Hard") which the student can confirm or override. Self-rating + a machine check together = calibrated metacognition, the thing pure flip-apps never measure.
4. SCHEDULE — On grade, the client runs the FSRS algorithm locally (ts-fsrs, deterministic, no network) to compute the next due date and updated stability/difficulty, writes the review row, and animates the card sliding off with its next-interval shown ("See again in 4 days"). This immediate interval feedback teaches the student how spacing works.
5. ELABORATIVE RESCUE (the differentiator) — When a card is graded "Forgot" twice OR confidence is chronically low, it is flagged "leech". Instead of mechanically re-queuing the identical card, the session offers a one-tap "Help me actually get this." Claude (Opus, streaming) then (a) explains WHY it's likely being missed, (b) generates a mnemonic / analogy / worked micro-example tied to the source passage, and (c) rewrites the card into 2 easier sub-cards that ladder up to the original. The student is taught the concept, not drilled against a wall.
6. SESSION CLOSE — A calm editorial summary: cards retrieved, calibration score (how well predicted confidence matched actual correctness), the day's retention curve, and "Next session: tomorrow, ~18 cards due." One sparing green accent highlights the streak.

GENERATION LOOP (how cards are born — also interactive, never one-shot):
- After a document/quiz/highlight exists, the student opens "Build deck." Claude (Sonnet, structured output) proposes a batch of candidate cards. Crucially they land in a REVIEW TRAY, not the deck — the student swipes to keep/edit/discard each, and editing a card is itself an encoding event. Quiz questions the student missed and text they highlighted are auto-prioritized as card seeds (those are the proven weak spots). This keeps the human in the authoring loop so generation is a study act, not a content dump.

WHY THIS DRIVES RETENTION (not consumption):
- Retrieval practice + spacing are the two highest-evidence learning techniques; the gated reveal enforces retrieval, FSRS enforces spacing.
- Confidence rating forces metacognitive calibration and surfaces the "fluency illusion."
- The leech-rescue converts failure into re-teaching, so a hard card improves the student rather than just recurring.
- Authoring-as-review keeps generation from being a passive AI dump.
features.claudeUsage:
Three distinct jobs, each on the cheapest model that clears the quality bar; the scheduler itself is NEVER an LLM (FSRS runs locally via ts-fsrs for determinism, zero latency, zero cost).

1) CARD GENERATION — model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (balanced; high volume, structured extraction over long source chunks). NON-streaming, STRUCTURED OUTPUT via output_config.format with a json_schema. Input is built as a cache-friendly prefix: a frozen system prompt + the source chunk (with cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} on the last system/document block) so re-generating or expanding a deck reuses the cache. Prompt strategy: "You are a learning scientist writing retrieval-practice cards. Prefer atomic, single-fact cards. Use cloze deletions for definitions/facts, Q→A for relationships, and avoid yes/no or trivially-guessable cards. For each card emit {front, back, card_type, cloze_text?, source_span, difficulty_hint, bloom_level}." Quiz-miss and highlight seeds are injected as MUST-COVER anchors. thinking:{type:"adaptive"} on; max_tokens streamed-safe (we keep generation batches small so non-streaming stays under timeout). Schema enforced with zod via zodOutputFormat so malformed cards can't reach the tray.

2) FREE-TEXT RECALL GRADING — model: claude-haiku-4-5 (fast, cheap, runs on every typed attempt; latency is felt directly by the student). STRUCTURED OUTPUT, json_schema: {verdict: enum[correct, partial, incorrect], matched_concepts[], missed_concepts[], suggested_fsrs_grade: 1-4, one_line_feedback}. Prompt strategy: semantic equivalence, not string match — "Grade the student's recalled answer against the canonical answer. Credit correct ideas in different words; do not penalize spelling or phrasing. Return missed concepts so the UI can highlight the gap." The suggested_fsrs_grade pre-selects the confidence button; the student still has final say (machine + self agreement is the calibration signal we store).

3) LEECH RESCUE / RE-TEACHING — model: claude-opus-4-8 (this is the differentiating, reasoning-heavy moment; worth the spend, low frequency since only leeches trigger it). STREAMING (text streamed token-by-token into the rescue panel so it feels alive; long output). thinking:{type:"adaptive"}, output_config:{effort:"high"}. It returns prose explanation + a mnemonic, THEN a final structured block of 2 laddered sub-cards (we make a second cheap structured call, or parse a fenced JSON tail) that the student can accept into the deck. Prompt strategy: feed the original card, the student's failed attempts, the confidence history, and the source span; "Diagnose the likely misconception, teach it with a concrete analogy or worked example grounded in the source, then split it into two easier prerequisite cards."

Cross-cutting: prompt caching on the source-chunk prefix across generation + rescue; all model calls behind Next.js Route Handlers (app/api/.../route.ts) using @anthropic-ai/sdk server-side (key never reaches the client); Haiku grading and Opus rescue use streaming where the user is waiting. Model IDs pinned: claude-opus-4-8 / claude-sonnet-4-6 / claude-haiku-4-5.

dataModel.notes: One deck per source or topic. RLS: user_id = auth.uid(). retention_pct is a cached read-model for the hub ring.

columns: parent_card_id uuid fk->cards null (for laddered rescue sub-cards)
dataModel.notes: FSRS state lives inline so the local scheduler reads/writes one row. origin drives the provenance dot; quiz_miss/highlight are prioritized in generation. is_leech triggers the rescue affordance.

columns: predicted_confidence smallint null (student self-rating before reveal, for calibration)
dataModel.notes: Append-only event log — every grade. predicted_confidence vs grade/ai_verdict yields the calibration score. Powers analytics + optional FSRS weight re-tuning.
Inbox of proven weak spots (missed quiz items, highlighted passages). Generation pulls unconsumed seeds first and marks them consumed → card.origin records provenance.
One row per study session; closes the loop with the session-summary screen (calibration, count, streak).
Persists each leech-rescue so the re-teaching is reusable and not re-billed. generated_subcard_ids links to the laddered cards created.

features.uiLayout:
Consistent with OpenBook DNA: dark base #242424 (light mode = warm off-white), SF Pro Display tight-tracked headings, SF Pro Text body, large rounded soft-shadow cards, floating pill nav, black pill CTAs in light mode, subtle dotted/halftone texture, ONE sparing #C0FE6F accent.

THREE SURFACES:
1. DECK HUB (/decks) — Calm editorial grid of large rounded deck cards, each with a glanceable ring showing % retained and a small "N due today" pill. A floating pill nav (Decks · Study · Build) hovers bottom-center, glassmorphic. The single green accent is reserved for the "Start today's review" pill and the streak flame. Generous whitespace; no feature-grid clutter.
2. STUDY VIEW (/study/[deckId]) — The hero. One centered flashcard on a near-empty canvas (ADHD focus by design — nothing else competes). Top: a thin dotted progress rail. Card front large and quiet. Retrieval input or "reveal" sits directly under the card. After reveal: the four confidence buttons as a horizontal pill cluster, color-coded on a colorblind-safe ramp (not red/green — uses a blue→amber luminance ramp with text labels + distinct icons). The next-interval ghost text animates in. A floating "Focus mode" toggle dims everything but the card.
3. BUILD/REVIEW TRAY (/build/[deckId]) — Candidate cards from Claude stack as swipeable rounded cards; keep/edit/discard. Quiz-miss and highlight seeds wear a tiny green dot to show provenance.

EMPTY STATES:
- No decks: a single centered rounded card, tight headline "Nothing to remember yet," subline "Upload a PDF, paste notes, or finish a quiz — we'll turn the hard parts into cards," and a black pill "Build your first deck." Dotted texture background.
- Deck with 0 due: "You're caught up. Next 18 cards unlock tomorrow." with the retention curve shown so the calm is earned, not idle.

LOADING:
- Generation: skeleton candidate cards shimmer in the tray (no spinners). Streaming rescue: tokens stream into the panel with a soft caret.
- Grading: the confidence cluster shows a 1-line "checking your recall…" with a subtle pulse, never blocking longer than Haiku takes.

ERROR STATES:
- Claude grading fails → gracefully fall back to manual self-grade ("Couldn't auto-check — grade yourself") so the loop never breaks.
- Generation fails → tray shows "Generation hiccuped, retry" with the source preserved.
- Offline → FSRS scheduling and reviews queue locally (Supabase Realtime/optimistic) and sync on reconnect; due-card study works fully offline since the scheduler is local.

KEY MICRO-INTERACTIONS: gated 3D card flip (cross-fade under prefers-reduced-motion); card slides off with next-interval ghost text; confidence buttons give a soft haptic-style scale; streak flame is the lone green accent; "reveal latency" captured invisibly on the reveal press.
features.accessibilityNotes:
Accessibility is woven into the core loop, not bolted on (serving the ADHD/Dyslexia/Colorblind/Weak-eyesight market):

- ADHD: Study view is single-card, near-empty canvas with a one-tap Focus Mode that dims all chrome — only the card and input remain. Sessions are bounded ("~18 due") not infinite scroll, so there's a finish line. Immediate next-interval feedback gives the dopamine of visible progress. Bounded, gamified-but-not-manipulative streak.
- Dyslexia: A per-user reading toggle switches body type to a dyslexia-friendly setting (increased letter/word spacing, taller line-height) WITHOUT breaking the SF Pro design language for others. Cloze blanks are generously spaced. Claude's grading never penalizes spelling, so dyslexic typing isn't punished as "wrong."
- Colorblind: Confidence grades NEVER rely on red/green. They use a blue→amber luminance ramp + distinct icons + explicit text labels ("Forgot / Hard / Good / Easy"). The retention ring uses luminance + pattern, not hue alone. Palette validated against deuteranopia/protanopia.
- Weak eyesight: The signature OpenBook hover magnifier/bold tool works over any card text — hover (or long-press) to zoom/bold a term while preserving layout. Cards already render at large type; everything respects OS text-scaling and has AA+ contrast on both themes.
- Motion: every flip/slide animation degrades to a cross-fade under prefers-reduced-motion. Full keyboard control: Space=reveal, 1-4=confidence grade, so no mouse is required.
features.activeLearningRationale:
This is a genuine learning loop, not a generator, because the student must DO the cognitively effortful work and the system measures whether it landed:

1. Forced retrieval before reveal. The reveal is gated behind a recall attempt (type or commit). This is the retrieval-practice effect — the single most robust finding in learning science — and it's structurally impossible to skip. Competitors that auto-flip let students mistake recognition ("yeah I knew that") for recall; we don't.
2. Real spacing via FSRS. The next-due date is computed by a true spaced-repetition model from the student's own grade history, so review effort is concentrated exactly at the edge of forgetting. A one-shot quiz generator has no temporal dimension at all.
3. Confidence calibration. Pairing the student's self-rated confidence with Claude's semantic check on each card produces a calibration score that exposes the "fluency illusion" — teaching the student to study what they only THINK they know.
4. Failure becomes teaching. The leech-rescue is the anti-generator move: a chronically-missed card triggers Claude to diagnose the misconception, re-teach with a grounded analogy, and ladder it into easier sub-cards. The student leaves understanding the concept, not just having seen the card again.
5. Authoring-as-encoding. Generated cards land in a review tray the student curates and edits; editing is itself a study act. The AI assists the human's learning labor rather than replacing it.
