A passive generator emits a deck and walks away. This guides the student through retrieve → calibrate → space → re-teach, every session.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: Sharpest differentiator: it CLOSES THE LOOP ON FAILURE. NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn/Studley generate a flashcard set and stop — when you miss a card, their best move is to show you the same card again. OpenBook detects the leech (missed twice / chronically low confidence), and instead of re-drilling the identical card, Claude Opus diagnoses *why* you're missing it, re-teaches it with a mnemonic/analogy grounded in your own source passage, and rewrites it into two easier laddered sub-cards. Combined with a true FSRS scheduler (not a fixed "review tomorrow") and confidence-calibrated grading, a hard card makes you smarter rather than just recurring. Competitors give you a deck; OpenBook gives you a tutor that fixes the cards you can't learn.
features.buildNotes:
STACK FIT (already installed: next@14.2, @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.102, @supabase/ssr + supabase-js, framer-motion, zod, lucide-react, tailwind-merge/clsx).

SCHEDULER: add `ts-fsrs` (npm i ts-fsrs). Run FSRS entirely client-side for determinism + offline + zero cost. Store FSRS card state (stability, difficulty, due, state, last_review, reps, lapses) on the `cards` row; append every grade to `reviews`. Never schedule with an LLM. Optionally re-tune FSRS weights per user later from review history.

CLAUDE WIRING: all calls in Route Handlers under app/api (server-only key). Generation = `client.messages.create` with `output_config:{format: zodOutputFormat(CardBatchSchema)}` on claude-sonnet-4-6, non-streaming, small batches (<=20 cards) to stay under HTTP timeout. Grading = claude-haiku-4-5 with json_schema (verdict/grade), stream not needed (short). Rescue = claude-opus-4-8 STREAMING via client.messages.stream piped to the client over a streaming Response (ReadableStream) so tokens render live; do a second small structured call for the 2 sub-cards (cleaner than parsing a JSON tail). Use thinking:{type:"adaptive"}; effort:"high" only on rescue. Cache the source-chunk prefix with cache_control:{type:"ephemeral"} so generate+rescue share it.

GOTCHAS: (1) Parse all tool/JSON output with the SDK's parsed_output / JSON.parse — never string-match. (2) Pin exact model IDs; don't append date suffixes. (3) Opus 4.8/Haiku 4.5/Sonnet 4.6: adaptive thinking only — never send budget_tokens or temperature/top_p (400s). (4) For streaming rescue use stream.finalMessage() to capture usage; don't hand-roll promise wrappers. (5) RLS: every table scoped by user_id with Supabase auth.uid() policies. (6) Highlights/quiz-miss seeds: store provenance on the card so the tray can show the green provenance dot and so generation can prioritize proven weak spots. (7) prefers-reduced-motion + full keyboard map (Space, 1-4) are launch requirements, not polish. (8) Source text for cards should reference document chunks already embedded via pgvector so rescue can pull the exact passage.

TABLES (data plane): decks, cards, reviews, card_seeds, study_sessions, rescue_artifacts (see dataModel).

features.feature: Mind Map — an interactive, co-constructed concept graph the student builds node-by-node and quizzes from, the antithesis of a one-shot diagram generator.
features.oneLine: OpenBook seeds a sparse skeleton from your material, then makes YOU grow and defend the graph — every node you add, link you draw, and recall gate you pass is the actual studying.
features.coreInteraction:
The loop is build → connect → defend → recall → repair, run per-node, never as a batch export.

1. SEED (not finish). On open, Claude reads the source doc(s) and streams in ONLY the 5–9 top-level concept nodes plus the central topic — deliberately sparse, with the supporting/child concepts withheld. The canvas shows ghost "+" stubs hanging off each node signalling "something belongs here — you find it." The student is never handed a finished map.
2. GROW. The student clicks a ghost stub or empty canvas to add a node and types what they think the sub-concept is (free recall from the reading). On blur, Claude (structured output) grades the node against the source: status becomes `confirmed` (matches source), `partial` (right idea, imprecise — returns the precise phrasing as a diff the student must accept), `off_source` (not in the material — Claude says where it actually appears or that it doesn't), or `misconception` (contradicts source — flagged red, with the correction). The student types the concept; Claude never auto-fills it.
3. CONNECT + DEFEND. The student drags an edge between two nodes and must label the relationship from a typed palette (causes, is-part-of, contrasts-with, depends-on, example-of, leads-to). Claude validates the labeled edge against the source and, for any `confirmed` link, fires a one-line Socratic follow-up in a side rail ("You linked mitosis → cytokinesis as 'leads-to'. What would break if cytokinesis failed?") that the student answers in one sentence — turning a drawn line into a retrieval rep.
4. RECALL GATE (the retention engine). Any node can be 'quizzed': it collapses to a blanked-out shell and the student must reproduce its definition and its outgoing links from memory. Claude scores the free-recall attempt (structured output: correct / partial / missed-links), updates that node's spaced-repetition state (SM-2 ease + due date), and recolors the node by mastery. A 'Quiz this branch' button walks every node under a parent in due-order.
5. REPAIR + REVISIT. Misconception and partial nodes accumulate into a 'Weak spots' tray. Returning later, the map opens to the due nodes pulsing; the student re-defends them. The map is a living mastery record, not a deliverable.

Why this retains and competitors don't: every state transition is gated on the student PRODUCING (free recall, relationship labeling, Socratic answer) before Claude responds — generation effect + retrieval practice + spaced repetition baked into the data model. A NotebookLM/StudyFetch mind map is read-only output; here the artifact only exists because the student reconstructed it, and the SR scheduler keeps pulling them back to the exact nodes they're weakest on.
features.claudeUsage:
Three-tier routing via the existing src/lib/anthropic.ts model() helper (fast=haiku-4-5, balanced=sonnet-4-6, deep=opus-4-8). All grading/validation calls use structured outputs (output_config.format with a json_schema) — NOT prose — so the client can drive deterministic node-state transitions.

- SEED (deep / opus-4-8, STREAMING): route POST /api/mindmap/seed. Source text (PDF/Doc extracted upstream, or pasted) goes in a cached system block (cache_control ephemeral — the doc is the stable prefix reused across every later grading call in the session). thinking:{type:'adaptive'}, effort 'high'. Streams a sparse skeleton as SSE so nodes pop onto the canvas one-by-one (uses client.messages.stream + .finalMessage()). Prompt strategy: 'Return ONLY the central topic and 5–9 first-order concepts actually present in the source. Do NOT include sub-concepts, definitions, or links — the student supplies those. For each, include a hidden source_anchor (verbatim quote + char offset) used later to grade the student, never shown.' Opus because skeleton quality (right grain, no hallucinated concepts, honest gaps) sets the ceiling for the whole session; it runs once.
- GRADE NODE / VALIDATE EDGE (fast → balanced, NON-streaming, structured output): POST /api/mindmap/grade. Reuses the cached source prefix. Default haiku-4-5 (cheap, called on every blur); auto-escalate to sonnet-4-6 when the node text is long/ambiguous or haiku returns low confidence. Strict JSON schema: {status: enum[confirmed,partial,off_source,misconception], canonical_text, correction, source_anchor_id, confidence:number}. max_tokens ~512.
- SOCRATIC FOLLOW-UP (balanced, STREAMING): one Socratic question per confirmed edge, then a single structured pass to score the student's one-sentence answer (accepted / needs_depth with a nudge). Sonnet for pedagogy quality at acceptable cost.
- RECALL GATE SCORING (balanced, structured output): POST /api/mindmap/recall. Schema {definition_score:enum, recalled_links:string[], missed_links:string[], feedback}. Server maps the enum to an SM-2 update (ease/interval/due) — scheduling math is deterministic server code, the LLM only judges recall quality.

Prompt-caching note: the extracted source is one cached system prefix per document; every grade/recall/socratic call appends only the small varying student input after the breakpoint, so cache_read dominates and per-interaction cost stays near-zero. Verify via usage.cache_read_input_tokens. All schemas live in src/lib/mindmap/schemas.ts (zod) and are passed with zodOutputFormat / messages.parse so node-state transitions are type-safe end to end.

columns: document_id uuid fk documents (the uploaded source)
source_checksum text (invalidate cache/anchors if doc changes)
layout jsonb (saved viewport/positions for stable recall)
dataModel.notes: One map per source. RLS: user_id = auth.uid(). Realtime enabled so a node graded server-side streams its new status back to the canvas.

columns: parent_id uuid fk mind_map_nodes null (tree backbone for 'quiz this branch')
canonical_text text (Claude's precise phrasing; null until graded)
dataModel.notes: ghost = AI-placed stub the student must fill; authored_by=student on every node they grow is what we surface as 'you built 84% of this map'. Status drives node color (colorblind-safe + icon, never color alone).

Hidden grading ground-truth from the SEED pass. pgvector lets grade-node do a cheap semantic prefilter (which anchor is this student node nearest?) before the LLM call, and powers 'show me where this is in the source' on click.
Edges are first-class and student-labeled — the relation label is itself a graded retrieval act.
SM-2 spaced-repetition state per node — the engine behind 'due nodes pulse on reopen' and the Weak-spots tray. Server-computed, never LLM-computed.
Append-only learning-activity log — feeds the progress bar, the 'you constructed this yourself' provenance claim, and future analytics. Cheap to write, never on the read hot path.

features.uiLayout:
Full-bleed canvas on the dark base (#242424) or light, honoring the OpenBook DNA: SF Pro Display tight-tracked for the central topic node, SF Pro Text in nodes; large rounded node cards with soft shadows; ONE sparing use of the lime accent (#C0FE6F) reserved exclusively for the single 'next action' affordance (the pulsing due node or the active ghost stub) so the eye always knows where to go.

- Floating pill nav top-center (Map / Outline / Weak spots), glassmorphic. Black pill CTA in light mode ('Quiz this branch').
- Right rail (collapsible): the Socratic side-rail + grading feedback, so the canvas stays calm. Subtle dotted/halftone texture on the canvas background, never on cards.
- Nodes are connected concept cards with relationship-labeled edges (learning-native UI, not a generic flowchart). Pan/zoom; positions persist.

States:
- EMPTY (pre-seed): centered wordmark-only prompt 'Drop a reading in — we'll find the spine, you grow the branches.' One black/lime CTA. No fake demo graph.
- LOADING/SEEDING: skeleton nodes fade in one at a time as the SSE stream lands (framer-motion staggered scale-in), each with a shimmer until its label resolves. Communicates 'being built for you', and the ghost stubs make the I-have-to-finish-this obvious.
- GRADING (per node): node shows an inline spinner ring; on result, micro-interaction = confirmed nodes do a soft settle/scale-bounce, misconceptions shake once and turn red-with-warning-icon. Edge validation animates the line drawing in.
- ERROR: per-node non-blocking toast in the rail ('Couldn't grade this one — tap to retry'); the node stays editable, never lost. Seed failure → keep the central node, offer retry; never wipe student work.
- OFFLINE/REALTIME-DROP: optimistic local node state, reconciles on reconnect via Supabase Realtime.

Key micro-interactions: ghost-stub pulse (lime) inviting the next node; drag-to-connect with a live relation-label chip following the cursor; 'quiz' flip that blanks a node to a recall shell; due-node breathing pulse on reopen.
features.accessibilityNotes:
Accessibility is integrated, not bolted on — and a graph is the highest-risk layout for these users, so it's load-bearing here.
- Colorblind-safe: node status NEVER encoded by color alone — each status carries a distinct icon + border pattern (confirmed=check/solid, partial=tilde/dashed, misconception=warning/double, ghost=plus/dotted). Palette is the lime/dark system plus a colorblind-safe status set (Okabe-Ito) toggleable in settings.
- Dyslexia: optional dyslexia-friendly mode increases node label letter/word spacing and line-height and left-aligns text; SF Pro stays the face but tracking opens up. Canonical_text diffs shown with generous spacing.
- ADHD focus mode: 'Isolate' dims the whole graph to one node + its direct neighbors and surfaces only the single next action (the lime affordance), killing the overwhelm of a 40-node canvas. 'Quiz this branch' is itself a focus container — one node at a time.
- Weak eyesight / universal: the OpenBook hover magnifier/bold tool works anywhere on the canvas — hover any node or feedback text to zoom/bold it in place without breaking layout; min 44px hit targets on nodes and stubs; full keyboard graph nav (arrow to traverse edges, Enter to grade, Q to quiz) so the map is usable without precise dragging; respects prefers-reduced-motion (pulses become opacity-only).
- All node text is real DOM (SF Pro), not canvas-rendered pixels, so screen readers and the magnifier both work; edges carry aria relationship labels.
features.activeLearningRationale:
This design is engineered around three replicated learning-science effects, and each maps to a gated interaction rather than a passive view:
1. Generation effect — the student must PRODUCE every node label and edge relation from memory of the reading before Claude responds; we grade their production, we never pre-fill it. The map literally cannot complete itself.
2. Retrieval practice — the recall gate (blank a node, reproduce its definition + outgoing links) and the per-edge Socratic one-liner are pure free-recall reps, the highest-yield study activity, performed ON the artifact they care about.
3. Spaced repetition — node_reviews holds real SM-2 state, so the map re-opens to the nodes the student is measurably weakest on, distributing practice over time instead of one cramming pass.
The honest-gaps seeding (sparse skeleton + ghost stubs) creates a desirable difficulty: the visible holes are a standing invitation to retrieve. And the misconception/partial states turn errors into targeted repair loops instead of silent wrong answers. Contrast a passive generator: it maximizes the feeling of understanding (a clean finished diagram) while minimizing the effortful processing that causes retention. OpenBook inverts that — it deliberately makes the student do the cognitively effortful work, because that effort IS the learning.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The map only exists because the student rebuilt it from memory, and it actively schedules them back to their weak nodes. NotebookLM / StudyFetch / YouLearn generate a finished mind map FOR you to look at — a one-shot artifact with zero retrieval demand and no memory of what you know. OpenBook ships a deliberately incomplete skeleton and makes growing, labeling, defending, and recalling each node the unit of studying, with an SM-2 scheduler turning the graph into a living, self-updating mastery record. Theirs is a diagram you consume; ours is a retrieval-practice instrument you operate.
features.buildNotes:
- Rendering: use React Flow (@xyflow/react) for the canvas — custom node types (GhostNode, ConceptNode, RecallShellNode) and a custom labeled edge with the relation chip. It gives pan/zoom, keyboard nav hooks, and DOM-rendered nodes (required for the magnifier + screen readers) out of the box. Persist node x/y via onNodesChange debounced to mind_maps.layout / node rows. framer-motion (already installed) drives the seed stagger and status micro-interactions; gate all of it on prefers-reduced-motion.
- Streaming seed: App Router route handler returns a ReadableStream; client consumes SSE and inserts nodes as they arrive. Use client.messages.stream(...).finalMessage() server-side, emit one SSE frame per parsed skeleton node (parse incrementally or do one structured pass then chunk — simplest: one opus structured-output pass, then server-side stagger the emit for the build-up feel without risking partial-JSON parsing).
- Structured outputs: define zod schemas in src/lib/mindmap/schemas.ts; use messages.parse with zodOutputFormat (SDK v0.102 supports it). Guard parsed_output null (refusal/max_tokens). Strict status enums are what make the client state machine deterministic.
- Prompt caching: put the extracted source in ONE system text block with cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} and keep it byte-identical across seed/grade/recall calls in a session (frozen prefix — no timestamps/IDs before it). Append only the student's varying input after the breakpoint. Source >4096 tokens needed for opus to cache. Re-warm or accept a cold first grade.
- pgvector: embed source_anchors at seed time (Anthropic has no embeddings API — use an embedding provider or Supabase's built-in gte-small via pg_vector/edge function; abstract behind src/lib/embeddings.ts). Nearest-anchor prefilter is an optimization, not correctness — the LLM still does final grading.
- Gotchas: (1) opus-4-8 thinking is adaptive-only, no budget_tokens/temperature (will 400) — match the existing anthropic.ts patterns. (2) Never auto-fill a node label client-side, even on 'partial' — present canonical_text as an accept-diff so the student still does the comparison. (3) SM-2 math is plain server TS in src/lib/mindmap/sr.ts, unit-tested, never LLM-delegated. (4) Realtime: enable on mind_map_nodes so server-side grade results stream to the canvas; reconcile optimistic local state by node id. (5) Cost guardrail: grade on blur/debounce, not keystroke; default haiku, escalate to sonnet only on low confidence.

features.feature: Notes — a living, concept-organized study workspace where every note is a concept node fed by the highlighter and chat. Claude never dumps prose into a note; it acts as a synthesis partner that prompts the student to articulate understanding first, then refines and connects what THEY wrote into a growing concept map. The artifact the student keeps is one they co-authored, not one that was generated for them.
features.oneLine: A concept-graph notebook where the student writes the understanding and Claude organizes, challenges, and connects it — turning passive highlights into an actively-recalled, interlinked knowledge map.
features.coreInteraction:
The retention-driving loop is "Capture -> Articulate -> Synthesize -> Connect -> Recall," and the key is that the STUDENT does the articulation; Claude only scaffolds.

STEP 0 — CAPTURE (passive intake, deliberately friction-light). While reading a source (PDF/PPT/transcript), the student highlights a passage. The highlight lands in a right-rail "Inbox" as an unprocessed snippet (source + page + exact text + their optional margin note). Chat messages can also be "pinned to notes." Nothing becomes a note yet — this is raw material. This separation is the whole thesis: competitors turn a highlight directly into a saved fact (passive). OpenBook treats a highlight as a PROMPT to think.

STEP 1 — ARTICULATE (the active beat). The student drags an Inbox snippet onto the canvas (or clicks "Make a note"). Instead of auto-writing the note, OpenBook opens a "Note Forge" composer with the source quote shown read-only at top and an EMPTY body with one Claude-generated retrieval prompt: e.g. "In your own words, why does X cause Y here? Don't look back at the quote." The student types their explanation. This forces generation/elaboration — the single best-evidenced encoding mechanism (the generation effect + elaborative interrogation). A "I'm stuck" affordance gives a Socratic hint, never the answer.

STEP 2 — SYNTHESIZE (Claude refines, doesn't replace). On submit, Claude streams an inline "synthesis pass" that (a) tightens the student's wording while preserving their voice, (b) flags any claim the source doesn't support ("the source says 'correlated', you wrote 'causes' — soften?"), and (c) extracts a canonical concept title + 2-4 atomic key-points. The student sees a diff-style accept/tweak UI. They must accept — so the final note is theirs, validated. This is "AI helps synthesize, never just dumps text" made literal.

STEP 3 — CONNECT (build the graph). The moment a note is saved, Claude (structured-output tool call, no prose) returns up to 3 suggested links to EXISTING notes in the notebook ("relates-to / contradicts / example-of / prerequisite-of") each with a one-line justification. The student confirms or rejects each link — confirming a link is itself a recall act ("how does this connect to what I already know?"). Confirmed links render as edges in a concept-map view. Over a study session the canvas grows into the student's own knowledge graph.

STEP 4 — RECALL (close the loop, spaced). Each note carries a lightweight recall state. A "Quick Recall" button on any note (or a daily "5-minute review" surfaced by spaced repetition) hides the body and shows only the title + the student's original retrieval prompt; the student re-explains from memory; Claude grades the attempt against the stored key-points (structured output: covered/missed/wrong) and bumps the note's next-review date. Missed key-points get re-surfaced. This converts the note from a dead document into a recurring active-recall object.

WHY THIS RETAINS vs PASSIVE GENERATORS: every note requires the student to (1) generate an explanation before seeing AI text, (2) reconcile their wording against the source, (3) reason about how it connects to prior notes, and (4) re-retrieve it over time. Those are exactly generation, elaboration, interleaving, and spaced retrieval — the four highest-yield techniques in the learning-science literature. NotebookLM/StudyFetch give you a finished summary you nod at and forget; OpenBook makes you do the cognitive work and then keeps testing you on it.
features.claudeUsage:
Three model tiers already configured in src/lib/anthropic.ts (fast=claude-haiku-4-5, balanced=claude-sonnet-4-6, deep=claude-opus-4-8). Pick the cheapest that clears each task's bar.

1) SYNTHESIS PASS (Step 2) — model: balanced (claude-sonnet-4-6). STREAMING via client.messages.stream() in a Next.js Route Handler returning a ReadableStream (Edge or Node runtime; SSE to the composer). Why streaming: the student watches their note get tightened token-by-token, which feels collaborative and keeps them engaged rather than waiting on a spinner. Why Sonnet not Opus: this is constrained rewriting + claim-checking against a provided quote, not open-ended reasoning — Sonnet clears it at 1/8th the Opus output cost and lower latency. thinking: {type:"adaptive"} so it reasons about source-support flags only when needed. Prompt strategy: system prompt is FROZEN (cache_control ephemeral) and hard-codes the non-negotiable rule "NEVER write the explanation for the student; only refine THEIR words, preserve their voice, and surface gaps as questions." The student's text + the source quote go in the user turn (after the cache breakpoint).

2) LINK SUGGESTION (Step 3) — model: fast (claude-haiku-4-5). NON-streaming, STRUCTURED OUTPUT via output_config:{format:{type:"json_schema", schema}} (or client.messages.parse with a zod schema — zod is already a dep). Returns {links:[{targetNoteId, relation: enum[relates_to|contradicts|example_of|prerequisite_of], rationale}]}. Input is the new note's title+keypoints plus a compact list of candidate notes (title + 1-line gist) pre-filtered by pgvector cosine similarity (top ~20) so we never stuff the whole notebook in context. Haiku is right: it's a bounded classification/matching task over short candidates. tool_choice not needed; json_schema guarantees parseable edges.

3) RECALL GRADING (Step 4) — model: fast (claude-haiku-4-5). STRUCTURED OUTPUT, schema {covered:[keypointId], missed:[keypointId], misconceptions:[{text, correction}], score:0-1}. Cheap, frequent, latency-sensitive (student is mid-review) — Haiku. Grading a free-text attempt against 2-4 known key-points is well within Haiku.

4) RETRIEVAL-PROMPT + SOCRATIC HINT generation (Step 1) — model: fast (claude-haiku-4-5), non-streaming for the prompt (short, one-shot), streaming for the "I'm stuck" hint so it feels conversational. Prompt strategy enforces "ask, never answer."

5) EMBEDDINGS for the graph: Anthropic has no embeddings endpoint — generate note embeddings with a dedicated embeddings provider (e.g. Voyage) at note-save time and store in a pgvector column; Claude is only used for the semantic relation+rationale on the pre-filtered candidates.

Cost/cache notes: the synthesis system prompt and the link-suggestion schema are stable -> cache_control on them; per-note content goes after the breakpoint. Set max_tokens ~1024 for synthesis, ~512 for links/grading (bounded outputs).

dataModel.notes: One workspace per course/document set. RLS: user_id = auth.uid().
The uploaded/connected materials highlights point back to. Storage in Supabase Storage.
The Inbox of raw captures. status flips to 'forged' once it becomes a note. Chat-pinned captures use source_id null + a kind flag.

The concept node. body_student is preserved separately from body_synth so we can prove the student authored it and re-show their voice. embedding (pgvector) powers link candidate pre-filtering. Index: ivfflat on embedding, btree on notebook_id.
Atomic recallable facts extracted in the synthesis pass; the grading rubric for Quick Recall.
Edges of the concept graph. Suggested by Claude (status=suggested), promoted to confirmed only by an explicit student act (the recall beat). Unique(source_note_id,target_note_id,relation).
Every active-recall attempt; analytics + drives scheduling.
SM-2-style spaced-repetition state, one row per note. Daily '5-minute review' query = WHERE next_review_at <= now().
Token/cost telemetry per Claude call for cost-tuning the tier routing. Append-only.

features.uiLayout:
Three-pane study workspace, consistent with OpenBook DNA (SF Pro, #242424 dark / paper-white light, ONE lime #C0FE6F accent, large rounded soft-shadow cards, floating pill nav).

LEFT — collapsed source rail: thin strip of source thumbnails; click expands the reader where highlighting happens.

CENTER — the Concept Canvas. Default is an editorial card list (calm, generous whitespace, big tracked-tight SF Pro Display titles); a pill toggle flips to Graph view (concept nodes connected by edges, the learning-native UI). Notes are large rounded cards with soft shadows; the green accent is used ONLY for one thing — the active-recall pulse / 'due for review' dot — so green always means 'do the recall work', reinforcing the thesis visually.

RIGHT — the Inbox rail: stacked raw highlight chips with source + page; a subtle dotted/halftone texture separates 'unprocessed' from the clean canvas. Drag a chip left to start a note.

NOTE FORGE (Step 1-2) is a centered floating composer (glassmorphic, dimmed backdrop): read-only source quote on top, the Claude retrieval prompt in lime-tinted text, an empty body the student types into, an 'I'm stuck' ghost button, and on submit a diff-style synthesis accept/tweak panel.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS (framer-motion, already a dep): chip lifts and casts a deeper shadow on drag; synthesis text streams in with a token-by-token reveal; a confirmed link animates as an edge drawing itself between two cards; accepting the synthesis collapses the diff into the final card with a soft settle; the green recall dot has a slow breathing pulse.

STATES:
- EMPTY notebook: a single centered card 'Highlight something in your reading to plant your first concept' with a faint dotted concept-map ghost behind it (teaches the loop, no fake demo data).
- EMPTY inbox but notes exist: 'Inbox clear — review what's due' nudging Recall.
- LOADING synthesis: the student's submitted text stays visible and editable; a slim lime progress hairline runs under it (never a blocking spinner — they can keep reading).
- LOADING links: skeleton edge shimmer.
- ERROR (Claude call fails): note is ALWAYS saved with body_student first, then a non-blocking toast 'Synthesis unavailable — your note is saved, retry refine?'. Student work is never lost to an AI failure. Link/grade failures degrade silently to 'try again'.
- OFFLINE/queued: highlights queue locally and sync via Supabase Realtime on reconnect.
features.activeLearningRationale:
A passive generator (NotebookLM, StudyFetch) collapses the whole flow to one step: source in, summary out, student reads and forgets. Retention research is blunt about why that fails — rereading and reviewing pre-made summaries produce almost no durable memory gains. The four techniques that DO (generation effect, elaborative interrogation, interleaving/elaboration, and spaced retrieval) all require the learner to effortfully produce, not consume. This design forces all four by construction and makes skipping them impossible:

1) GENERATION: the Note Forge shows an empty body and a 'why?' prompt BEFORE any AI text exists. You cannot get a note without first writing your own explanation. Claude is structurally barred (frozen system prompt) from writing the first draft.
2) ELABORATION + SELF-EXPLANATION: the synthesis pass reflects the student's words back, flags where their claim outran the source, and asks them to reconcile — that reconciliation is elaborative interrogation, the highest-yield comprehension technique.
3) INTERLEAVING/CONNECTION: confirming a suggested link is an explicit retrieval of prior notes ('how does this fit what I already know?'). The graph isn't decoration; building it IS the studying.
4) SPACED RETRIEVAL: every note becomes a recurring Quick Recall object graded against the student's own key-points, scheduled by SM-2. The note keeps earning its keep.

Crucially, Claude's role is inverted from competitors: it's the COACH (prompts, hints, gap-flags, connection hypotheses, grading) and never the AUTHOR. The student's keystrokes are the content. That single inversion is what turns a note-taking tool into a retention engine.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The single sharpest differentiator: OpenBook makes the student write the note and Claude organizes it, where every competitor makes the AI write the note and the student reads it. Concretely — a highlight in NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn becomes a saved AI summary (passive, forgettable). In OpenBook a highlight becomes a retrieval PROMPT that forces the student to explain it from memory first; Claude then only refines their words, checks them against the source, and weaves the note into a confirmable concept graph that is later re-tested via spaced active recall. The kept artifact is a knowledge map the student co-authored and keeps being quizzed on — not a generated document they skimmed once.
features.accessibilityNotes:
Accessibility is woven into the loop, not bolted on — squarely serving the secondary market.

DYSLEXIA: the Note Forge body and all note cards expose a per-user reading mode (increased letter/word spacing, ~1.6 line-height, left-aligned, optional OpenDyslexic toggle) persisted on the user profile; because notes are short, student-authored chunks rather than walls of generated text, cognitive load is already lower by design.

ADHD: the three-pane separation of raw Inbox (noisy, dotted texture) from the calm canvas is itself a focus aid; a 'Focus Mode' dims everything except the single active note/Forge (the dimmed glassmorphic backdrop is reused). The '5-minute review' framing and one-concept-at-a-time recall keep sessions short and bounded — anti-overwhelm by construction. No autoplaying motion; the green recall pulse respects prefers-reduced-motion (becomes a static dot).

COLORBLIND: the four link relations are never encoded by color alone — each has a distinct edge style (solid/dashed/dotted/double) and an icon + text label. The lone green accent is reserved for a single semantic ('recall due') and always paired with a dot shape + label, so no information is lost to deuteranopia/protanopia.

WEAK EYESIGHT: a global hover magnifier/bold tool (the OpenBook signature) zooms or bolds any text — source quote, note body, key-point, edge label — without breaking layout; all type scales with the browser's root font; contrast meets WCAG AA in both light and dark themes. Quote attributions and recall prompts are real text (never baked into images) so screen readers and the magnifier both reach them.
features.buildNotes:
Stack is already wired: @anthropic-ai/sdk 0.102, @supabase/ssr, framer-motion, lucide-react, zod 4, tailwind. Reuse src/lib/anthropic.ts model() tiers (fast/balanced/deep) and src/lib/supabase server/client.

ROUTES (App Router, all server-side so the API key never ships to the client):
- POST /api/notes/synthesize -> streaming Route Handler; client.messages.stream(model=balanced); return new Response(stream.toReadableStream()) and read it in the Forge with EventSource/ReadableStream reader. Persist body_synth + note_keypoints only after the student ACCEPTS (separate POST), so the DB always reflects student-validated content.
- POST /api/notes/links -> non-stream; client.messages.parse({output_config:{format: zodOutputFormat(LinkSchema)}}) model=fast; pre-filter candidates with an RPC: select ... order by embedding <=> $1 limit 20 (pgvector cosine). Insert returned links as status='suggested'.
- POST /api/recall/grade -> non-stream structured output, model=fast; write recall_attempts + update note_schedule (SM-2).
- POST /api/notes/prompt and /api/notes/hint -> model=fast.

GOTCHAS:
- Opus/Sonnet 4.x: adaptive thinking only — use thinking:{type:'adaptive'}; do NOT send budget_tokens/temperature (400). No assistant prefills — use output_config.format for all structured calls (matches what's already implied by the deep tier = claude-opus-4-8).
- Always parse tool/structured JSON with the schema (zod via zodOutputFormat from @anthropic-ai/sdk/helpers/zod) — never string-match.
- Save body_student to notes BEFORE calling Claude, in the same request, so an AI failure never loses student work (drives the error-state UX above).
- pgvector: Anthropic has no embeddings API — add a Voyage (or similar) embeddings call at save time to populate notes.embedding; enable the 'vector' extension and an ivfflat index. If embeddings aren't available at MVP, fall back to a lexical candidate filter (title/keypoint trigram) for link suggestions and keep the same Claude relation step.
- RLS on every table keyed to auth.uid(); use the SSR server client for all writes.
- Supabase Realtime on note_links/notes so the graph updates live and multi-tab stays consistent.
- cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} on the frozen synthesis system prompt and the link schema; log usage (cache_read_input_tokens etc.) into ai_runs to verify cache hits and tune tiers.
- max_tokens: synthesis ~1024, links/grade ~512 (bounded). Stream anything that could run long; these are short so timeouts aren't a risk, but stream synthesis for the UX reveal.

features.feature: Highlighter — highlight any text anywhere in OpenBook and it is auto-captured into Notes with surrounding context and a one-line AI annotation, turning passive reading into active capture that you later have to defend.
features.oneLine: Select text → it becomes a living note with AI context + a recall prompt, and OpenBook quizzes you back on your own highlights so capturing is the first step of a study loop, not the last.
features.coreInteraction:
The loop has five steps and is the same on a PDF, a YouTube transcript, a pasted note, or a chat answer:

1. SELECT. Student drags across any text in any reading surface. On mouseup a floating glass "capture pill" appears anchored to the selection (HIG popover): three controls — Highlight (lime), Annotate, Magnify/Bold (accessibility, see below). Keyboard: select + press H. This is the friction-zero capture; it must feel like a native OS action.

2. CAPTURE + CONTEXT. Highlight writes a row immediately (optimistic, lime mark paints instantly) capturing the exact text, a ~280-char window of text before/after (the "surrounding context"), the source locator (doc_id + char offsets, or PDF page+rect, or video timestamp), and the section heading it sits under. The student is NOT interrupted — capture never blocks reading.

3. AI ANNOTATION (the active twist). Within ~1.5s the note card in the side rail fills in via streaming: a 1-sentence "why this matters / what it connects to" annotation AND — crucially — a single auto-generated *recall question* whose answer is the highlight itself. The student sees their highlight reframed as a question they will have to answer. This reframing at capture time is what flips passive → active: you are told, the instant you highlight, "you'll have to recall this."

4. ACTIVE TRIAGE. The Notes rail is not a dump. Each card has one required micro-action before it "settles": the student taps why-it-matters in their own words (one tap reveals a 1-line input) OR taps "I get it" / "Confused." Confused cards float to the top and get a deeper Claude explanation on demand. This forces a 2-second elaboration at encoding — the single highest-yield retention move (elaborative interrogation).

5. RECALL LATER. Highlights become spaced-recall items. On the next session OpenBook surfaces 3–5 due recall questions generated in step 3 ("Active Recall" card on the dashboard). Student types/speaks an answer; Claude grades it against the original highlight + context and shows the source passage. Getting it wrong reschedules it sooner. The highlight you made on Tuesday interrogates you on Thursday.

Why this retains rather than passively consumes: capture is paired with (a) immediate elaboration (you must say why it matters), (b) a generation-effect reframe (highlight shown as a question), and (c) spaced active retrieval (you answer it later). NotebookLM/StudyFetch stop at step 2 — they store or summarize. OpenBook makes every highlight a promise to test yourself, and keeps that promise.
features.claudeUsage:
Two distinct Claude calls, both cost-tuned against the existing tier map (fast=claude-haiku-4-5, balanced=claude-sonnet-4-6, deep=claude-opus-4-8):

ANNOTATION + RECALL QUESTION (step 3) — model: claude-haiku-4-5 (the `fast` tier). This is a short, schema-bound, latency-critical task fired on every highlight; Haiku 4.5 meets the bar at lowest cost/latency. Use STRUCTURED OUTPUT, not streaming: `client.messages.create` with `output_config: { format: { type: 'json_schema', schema } }` (zodOutputFormat from the SDK helper) returning `{ annotation: string (<=180 chars), recall_question: string, connects_to: string|null, concept_tag: string }`. Schema-constrained output guarantees the card renders without parse failures and the recall question is always present. max_tokens ~400. We do NOT stream this — the payload is tiny and we want one atomic write to Supabase. Prompt strategy: system prompt frozen (cacheable) defining OpenBook's tutor voice + "you are reframing a highlight as a retrieval cue, never just restating it"; user turn carries ONLY the highlight + surrounding context + section heading (volatile content last, so the frozen system prefix caches across every highlight in a session — verify via cache_read_input_tokens). thinking disabled (no reasoning needed for a 1-liner).

ANSWER GRADING (step 5) — model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (the `balanced` tier). Grading a free-text recall answer against the source needs real judgment (partial credit, paraphrase tolerance) but not Opus. Structured output: `{ verdict: 'correct'|'partial'|'incorrect', feedback: string, missed_points: string[] }`. Non-streaming. This drives the spaced-repetition reschedule.

DEEPER EXPLANATION on a "Confused" card (step 4, on-demand only) — model: claude-sonnet-4-6, STREAMING (`client.messages.stream`, render `text` deltas into the card) because it is a longer, user-facing tutoring paragraph where progressive render matters. adaptive thinking on.

No tool use needed — these are bounded generate/extract/grade tasks, so structured output is the right primitive over an agent loop. All calls run in Next.js Route Handlers (server-only `anthropic` client already in src/lib/anthropic.ts); annotation is fire-and-forget from the optimistic client write so reading is never blocked.
features.uiLayout:
Two-pane reading view consistent with OpenBook DNA. LEFT (primary, ~68%): the reading surface (PDF/transcript/doc) on a calm editorial canvas — generous whitespace, dark base #242424 (dark) or warm off-white (light), SF Pro Text body with relaxed line-height. RIGHT: a Notes rail of large rounded cards with soft shadows, one card per highlight. A floating pill nav sits top-center. The ONE pop of #C0FE6F lime is the highlight mark itself and the active recall badge — nowhere else (CTAs are black pills in light mode, per DNA).

CAPTURE PILL: glassmorphic floating popover anchored to selection (framer-motion spring in, 120ms). Highlight / Annotate / Magnify icons (lucide), no labels until hover.

NOTE CARD anatomy: highlighted snippet (lime left-border), then a shimmer placeholder → streamed annotation, then the recall question in muted italic with a small lime "you'll be asked this" dot, then the triage row (I get it / Confused / "why it matters →").

EMPTY: dotted/halftone-textured panel, SF Pro Display tight-tracked headline "Highlight anything. We'll make it stick." + ghosted demo of a selection turning into a card. No generic illustration.

LOADING: per-card skeleton shimmer (not a global spinner) — the highlight mark is already painted, only the AI line shimmers, reinforcing that capture already succeeded.

ERROR: if annotation fails, card shows the highlight intact + a quiet "Retry annotation" inline link (never lose the user's capture); a toast only on persistent failure. Grading failure → "Couldn't grade — here's the source passage" fallback so the recall loop still teaches.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS: lime paint sweep across selection on capture; card slides into rail with spring; "Confused" cards reorder with layout animation; recall badge pulses once when a card becomes due.
features.activeLearningRationale: A passive generator (NotebookLM/StudyFetch) treats a highlight as storage or as input to a one-shot summary. This design makes each highlight trigger three evidence-based retention mechanisms at the moment of capture and after it: (1) ELABORATIVE INTERROGATION — the required "why does this matter?" micro-tap forces the student to connect the fact to prior knowledge, the highest-ROI encoding move; (2) GENERATION EFFECT — showing the highlight reframed as a recall question the instant it's made means the student encodes it as something-to-be-retrieved, not something-to-be-reread (rereading is near-useless; retrieval practice is dominant); (3) SPACED RETRIEVAL — the auto-generated question re-surfaces on a schedule and is graded, so the highlight literally tests its maker days later, with wrong answers rescheduled sooner. The loop is closed: capture → elaborate → reframe → retrieve → regrade. The student is never a passive recipient; every highlight is a debt they must repay by recalling it. That is the difference between a notebook and a tutor.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: Your highlights interrogate you back. In NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn a highlight is inert — it's saved, maybe summarized. In OpenBook, the act of highlighting instantly mints a graded recall question whose answer is what you highlighted, and that question comes back on a spaced schedule and judges your answer. Highlighting stops being the end of studying and becomes the start of a retrieval loop. No competitor turns the user's own selections into an adaptive self-quiz at capture time.
features.buildNotes:
Data model (Supabase Postgres + RLS, all tables keyed on auth.uid()):

TABLE highlights — id uuid pk, user_id uuid (fk auth.users, RLS owner), document_id uuid (fk documents), source_type text ('pdf'|'transcript'|'doc'|'image_ocr'|'chat'), exact_text text, context_before text, context_after text, section_heading text, locator jsonb (page+rects for PDF, char range for doc, {start_s,end_s} for video), color text default '#C0FE6F', created_at timestamptz, deleted_at timestamptz null. Index (user_id, document_id).

TABLE highlight_annotations — id uuid pk, highlight_id uuid (fk, unique — 1:1), annotation text, recall_question text, connects_to text null, concept_tag text, model text, status text ('pending'|'ready'|'failed'), created_at. Split from highlights so the optimistic highlight write never waits on Claude and a failed annotation is independently retryable.

TABLE highlight_triage — highlight_id uuid pk fk, understanding text ('clear'|'confused'|null), user_elaboration text null, updated_at. (1:1; powers the Confused-to-top reorder.)

TABLE recall_reviews — id uuid pk, highlight_id uuid fk, user_id uuid, due_at timestamptz, interval_days int default 1, ease real default 2.5, last_verdict text null, last_user_answer text null, reviewed_at timestamptz null. SM-2-lite scheduler; one row per highlight, due_at drives the dashboard "Active Recall" queue. Index (user_id, due_at).

Gotchas / libs:
- PDF highlighting: react-pdf-highlighter or pdf.js text-layer rects; persist normalized rects in locator so highlights survive re-render/zoom.
- DOM range capture on transcripts/docs: serialize selection as character offsets within a stable data-block-id wrapper (don't store raw DOM XPaths — they break on re-render). Rebuild marks from offsets on load.
- Optimistic write: insert highlight via Supabase, paint mark, THEN POST /api/highlights/[id]/annotate (fire-and-forget) which calls Haiku and upserts highlight_annotations; Supabase Realtime subscription on highlight_annotations streams the card from pending→ready so multi-tab stays in sync.
- Prompt caching: keep the annotation system prompt byte-stable; put highlight+context in the user turn so the frozen prefix caches (watch usage.cache_read_input_tokens).
- Scheduler: a Supabase cron (pg_cron) isn't needed for MVP — compute due items on read (where due_at <= now()), update interval/ease on grade.
- Zod schemas shared between the route handler (zodOutputFormat) and the client card types via src/lib/highlighter/schema.ts.

Accessibility — the third capture-pill control is the subtle accessibility hook: a Magnify/Bold tool that, while held, zooms or bolds the hovered text in place without altering layout (weak-eyesight + dyslexia), implemented as a CSS transform/font-weight overlay on the text layer. Note cards offer a per-card "open dyslexia mode" that applies increased letter/word spacing + a dyslexia-friendly line-height to that card's text only. ADHD: a Focus toggle dims everything except the current paragraph and the one active note card, and the required single-tap triage keeps the interaction lightweight (no long forms). Colorblind: lime #C0FE6F is never the sole signal — highlights also carry a left-border + an underline pattern, and the recall-due state uses a shape/badge, not color alone; a colorblind-safe alternate mark palette is selectable in settings. Magnifier and Focus states are keyboard-triggerable and announced via aria-live so the capture loop is fully operable without a mouse.
features.accessibilityNotes: Capture pill's third tool is a hold-to-Magnify/Bold lens that zooms/bolds hovered text in place with zero layout shift (weak-eyesight, dyslexia). Per-card dyslexia mode adds letter/word spacing + friendly line-height scoped to that card. ADHD Focus mode dims all but the active paragraph + active note card, and triage is a single tap (no forms). Colorblind-safe: lime is never the only cue — highlights add a left-border + underline pattern and recall-due uses a badge shape; alternate mark palette in settings. All states keyboard-operable (select+H to capture, focus-trap on the pill) and announced via aria-live.

features.feature: Dictionary on Hover — instant in-context definitions that turn passive reading into active vocabulary building
features.oneLine: Hover any word in your document and OpenBook tells you what it means in THIS passage — not a generic dictionary entry — then quietly turns the words you looked up into a personal review deck.
features.coreInteraction:
The loop is intentionally three-beat: HOVER -> PREDICT -> CONFIRM -> RETAIN, not "hover -> read -> forget."

1. READ. Student reads a passage in the OpenBook reader. Every content word is silently a hover target (no underlines, no clutter — the page looks like a clean editorial layout).

2. HOVER (intent signal). On hover-with-dwell (~350ms, so accidental passes don't fire) a small glass popover anchors under the word. The student has expressed "I don't know this here."

3. PREDICT (the active step competitors skip). Before the definition resolves, the popover shows the word, its part of speech in this sentence, and a one-tap "I think it means…" affordance: two plausible-but-wrong distractor glosses + the correct one, OR a "reveal" button for students who'd rather not guess (ADHD/low-friction mode). The guess is the retention engine — a 350ms act of retrieval beats 10 seconds of passive reading. Distractors are generated in the same Claude call as the definition so there's no extra latency.

4. CONFIRM (in-context definition). The card resolves to: the contextual definition (what it means in THIS sentence, e.g. "novel" = "new/original," not "a book"), a plain-language gloss, the specific sentence-sense disambiguated, and a 4-word "why here" tie-back to the surrounding text. Correct guess = the green accent pulses once; wrong guess = the card shows the contrast ("you guessed the book-sense; here it's the new-sense"). Contrast-on-error is what makes the wrong guess valuable rather than embarrassing.

5. RETAIN (the loop closes later, not now). Every looked-up word is logged to a per-document vocab list. The word is now faintly dotted in the text (colorblind-safe, not color-only). At the end of the passage / next session, OpenBook surfaces a 5-item micro-review: it shows the original sentence with the word blanked and asks the student to recall the sense. Spaced repetition (SM-2-lite) schedules re-exposure. This is the difference from a dictionary: the lookup is the START of learning, the review is where retention happens.

Why this produces retention rather than consumption: (a) the predict-step forces retrieval, the single most evidence-backed driver of memory; (b) errors are made productive via contrast, not hidden; (c) the word re-surfaces on a spacing schedule in its ORIGINAL context, so the student learns the sense-in-use, not a decontextualized entry; (d) the student is taught a transferable study habit — "guess before you check" — not just fed an answer.
features.claudeUsage:
MODEL CHOICE — claude-haiku-4-5 ($1/$5 per 1M, 200K context) for the core hover card. Rationale: this is the highest-volume, most latency-sensitive surface in the product (a strong reader fires dozens of hovers per page). The task — "define one word in this one sentence + write 2 distractors" — is well within Haiku's quality bar, and Opus/Sonnet would triple cost and add latency for no quality gain. The 'cheapest model that meets the bar' rule points squarely at Haiku here. Escalate to claude-sonnet-4-6 ONLY for the optional "deep dive" (etymology, cross-passage usage, morphology breakdown) the student explicitly taps "Tell me more" for — rare, latency-tolerant, deserves more depth.

STRUCTURED OUTPUT, not streaming, for the card. The card is small (~80 tokens) and must render as a single atomic unit (guess-distractors + definition appear together, or the predict-step breaks). Use output_config: {format: {type: 'json_schema', schema: ...}} (structured outputs are GA on Haiku 4.5) so the client gets guaranteed-parseable JSON every time — no defensive parsing, no half-rendered cards. JSON schema:
{
  type: 'object', additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['word','lemma','pos','contextual_definition','plain_gloss','sense_tag','why_here','distractors','difficulty'],
  properties: {
    word: {type:'string'}, lemma: {type:'string'},
    pos: {type:'string', enum:['noun','verb','adjective','adverb','pronoun','preposition','conjunction','other']},
    contextual_definition: {type:'string'},   // meaning in THIS sentence
    plain_gloss: {type:'string'},             // dyslexia-friendly, <=12 words, simple
    sense_tag: {type:'string'},               // e.g. 'new/original-sense'
    why_here: {type:'string'},                // <=8 words tying to surrounding text
    distractors: {type:'array', items:{type:'string'}}, // 2 plausible wrong senses for the predict-step
    difficulty: {type:'string', enum:['common','academic','technical','rare']}
  }
}
STREAMING is used only on the 'Tell me more' deep dive (Sonnet 4.6, .stream()/.finalMessage()) where output is long and progressive reveal feels good.

PROMPT STRATEGY — the passage is the context, the word+offset is the question. System prompt is FROZEN (a tight rubric: "Define the target word as used in THIS exact sentence. Prefer the sense the surrounding text forces. plain_gloss must be readable by a 12-year-old / dyslexic reader. Distractors must be real senses of the word that are WRONG here, never nonsense. Never define a different word."). The target is passed as char offsets, not just the string, so repeated words ('lead' the metal vs 'lead' the verb) disambiguate correctly. ADAPTIVE THINKING OFF (thinking:{type:'disabled'}) — single-word definition needs no reasoning trace and we want sub-second TTFB; add a final-answer-only note since Haiku won't over-narrate but the schema enforces it anyway.

PROMPT CACHING is the cost lever. Render order is system -> messages. Put a cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} breakpoint on the PASSAGE CHUNK block (the ~1-3K-token surrounding text), with the volatile target word+offset AFTER it. Every hover within the same passage reads that chunk at ~0.1x instead of full price. NOTE: Haiku 4.5's minimum cacheable prefix is 4096 tokens — so we cache [frozen system prompt + passage chunk] together as one prefix to clear the floor; below that the chunk silently won't cache. Verify via usage.cache_read_input_tokens on the 2nd+ hover in a passage. This makes a 40-hover reading session cost roughly the same as ~3 full-price lookups.

dataModel.notes: One row per hover that resolved a card. guessed/guess_correct capture the predict-step outcome (the retention signal). char_start/end disambiguate repeated words in the same doc. This is the raw event log.

One row per (user, lemma, sense) — the spaced-repetition deck. SM-2-lite fields (ease_factor/interval_days/repetitions/due_at) schedule the micro-review re-exposures. UNIQUE(user_id, lemma, sense_tag) so the same sense isn't duplicated across hovers; a repeat hover just bumps repetitions. example_sentence preserves the ORIGINAL context for in-context review.
Each micro-review answer. quality_score (0-5) feeds the SM-2 update of the parent vocab_item. prompt_type e.g. 'blanked_sentence' | 'multiple_choice' | 'recall'.

Server-side cache of resolved cards keyed by (lemma + a fingerprint of the local sentence context). Cuts Claude calls for common words in common contexts ('the', 'said') to near-zero and dedupes hovers across users on the same public doc. UNIQUE(lemma, context_fingerprint). pgvector NOT needed here — exact fingerprint match is enough and faster.

Drives the accessibility behaviors. predict_step_enabled=false gives the low-friction 'just reveal' mode for ADHD/overwhelm. These also gate the green-pulse animation (reduced_motion) and the hover magnifier.

features.uiLayout:
Consistent with OpenBook DNA: clean editorial reader on the dark base (#242424) or light cream, SF Pro Text body with generous line-height, SF Pro Display for the card's headword. The page has ZERO dictionary chrome at rest — no dotted underlines on every word, no highlight noise — preserving the calm whitespace.

THE CARD: a large-radius (16px) glassmorphic popover with a soft shadow, anchored beneath the hovered word via a floating-pill aesthetic. ~280px wide. Layout top-to-bottom: headword (SF Pro Display, tight tracking) + small pos pill; the predict-step row (three tappable gloss chips, or a single 'Reveal' pill); on resolve, the contextual definition in body text, the plain_gloss in a slightly de-emphasized line, and a tiny 'why here' tag. ONE green accent only — used for the correct-answer pulse and the 'add to deck' checkmark; everything else is neutral grey. A subtle dotted/halftone texture on the card back edge nods to the brand.

EMPTY STATE (no hover): nothing visible — the reader is the empty state by design.
LOADING STATE: on dwell, the card frame appears INSTANTLY with the headword + pos (we know those client-side from the token) and a calm shimmer where the definition will land — so the predict-step chips can be shown/answered DURING the ~400ms Claude call (latency hidden behind the active step; the guess fills the wait productively). No spinner.
ERROR STATE: if the call fails, the card degrades gracefully to a cached/offline generic gloss with a small 'in-context sense unavailable — tap to retry' line, never a broken card.
HOVER-OUT: card lingers 600ms then fades (reduced_motion: cuts instantly) so the student can move the cursor into it.

MICRO-REVIEW: a bottom-anchored slim panel at passage end / session start — 5 cards max, the original sentence with the word blanked, large tap targets. Designed for a 60-second ADHD-friendly burst, not a quiz wall.
features.activeLearningRationale:
Competitors' "definitions" (where they exist) are passive: hover, read, move on — zero encoding. OpenBook's design is built around the retrieval-practice and spacing literature, the two best-replicated findings in learning science:
1. The PREDICT step converts every lookup into an act of retrieval. Even a 350ms guess (or a wrong guess corrected by contrast) produces dramatically better retention than reading the answer, because the brain encodes the attempt, not the exposure.
2. CONTEXTUAL definitions teach the sense-in-use. A generic entry ('novel = a book') actively misleads when the passage means 'novel = new.' By disambiguating to THIS sentence, the student learns the word as it actually functions in their material.
3. The RETAIN loop (per-doc vocab deck + SM-2 spaced re-exposure in the original sentence) means the lookup is the beginning of learning, not the end. The word comes back, in context, exactly when the student is about to forget it.
4. It teaches a transferable HABIT — 'guess before you check' — so the student gets better at studying, which is OpenBook's whole thesis. A generator hands you a fish; this builds the reflex.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The definition is sense-disambiguated to THIS passage and is the seed of a spaced-repetition habit — not a generic dictionary lookup. NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn, at most, surface a static glossary entry on click; OpenBook makes you guess first, corrects you with contrast when you're wrong, and re-surfaces the word days later in its original sentence. The single sharpest line: they tell you what a word means once; OpenBook makes sure you still know it next week.
features.accessibilityNotes:
Accessibility is woven into the core loop, not bolted on:
- DYSLEXIA: plain_gloss is schema-enforced to <=12 words of simple language; dyslexia_spacing pref widens letter/word/line spacing across the reader and card without touching the layout grid; SF Pro Text at comfortable measure.
- ADHD: predict_step_enabled=false gives a one-tap 'just reveal' path (no forced guessing when overwhelmed); focus_mode dims everything except the current sentence; the micro-review is capped at 5 items / ~60s so it never becomes a wall; hover-dwell threshold prevents accidental-fire distraction.
- COLORBLIND: looked-up words are marked with a dotted underline (shape, not color); correct/incorrect uses an icon + position, never red/green alone; colorblind_safe_marks default true; the lime accent is paired with a checkmark glyph so it's never the only signal.
- WEAK EYESIGHT: magnifier_on_hover zooms/bolds the hovered word and card text in place while preserving the design (the brand's hover-magnifier tool); all tap targets >=44px in the review panel; reduced_motion kills the pulse/fade so motion-sensitive and low-vision users get instant, stable cards.
features.buildNotes:
- Hover target detection: wrap rendered text in word spans at render time with data-offset attributes (char_start/end from the chunk), NOT runtime DOM walking on mousemove — pre-tokenize when the chunk loads so hover is cheap. Use a 350ms dwell timer + IntersectionObserver to only arm visible text.
- API route: Next.js 14 App Router route handler (app/api/define/route.ts) on the Edge runtime for low TTFB; takes {chunk_id, char_start, char_end}, loads the chunk text server-side (never trust client-sent passage — prevents prompt injection from a tampered client and keeps the cached prefix byte-stable). Check definition_cache first; on miss call Haiku.
- Anthropic call: client.messages.create with model 'claude-haiku-4-5', max_tokens ~300, thinking:{type:'disabled'}, output_config:{format:{type:'json_schema', schema}}, NON-streaming. Put cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} on the [system + passage chunk] prefix block; pad/ensure the cached prefix clears Haiku's 4096-token minimum or it silently won't cache (verify usage.cache_read_input_tokens on hover #2+).
- Parse: with structured outputs the JSON is guaranteed-shaped; still guard parsed_output for null (refusal/max_tokens edge) and fall back to definition_cache generic gloss.
- Write path: insert into lookups; upsert vocab_items on UNIQUE(user_id, lemma, sense_tag) (ON CONFLICT bump repetitions, don't duplicate); compute due_at with SM-2-lite. Do these in a Supabase RPC / single transaction so a fast reader's burst of hovers doesn't race.
- Realtime: Supabase Realtime on vocab_items so the 'words learned today' counter updates live and the review panel knows when items come due.
- RLS: row-level security on lookups/vocab_items/review_events keyed to auth.uid(); definition_cache is shared (no user_id) and read-only to clients (writes via service role in the route).
- Latency budget: client renders card frame + headword + pos instantly from the token (already known), shows predict chips, and only the definition text waits on Claude — so the network round-trip is hidden behind the guess. Target p95 card-resolve < 700ms; cache hits < 150ms.
- Cost guard: rate-limit per-user define calls (debounce identical lemma+offset within a session) and lean hard on definition_cache for stopwords/common words so a power-reader session stays cents-cheap.

features.feature: Communication Mode — a persisted "voice register" the student picks once (Formal/Professional, Casual, Gen Z, Gen Alpha) that Claude applies consistently to every piece of AI output across OpenBook (explanations, quiz feedback, flashcard hints, summaries, chat). Critically, it is NOT a one-shot tone toggle — it is a calibration loop that confirms comprehension improves in the chosen register, then locks the voice in as a per-user setting threaded through every other feature's system prompt.
features.oneLine: Pick the voice you actually think in, and OpenBook teaches you in it — then proves the register raised your recall before locking it in.
features.coreInteraction:
The retention-driving loop (the differentiator vs a passive tone-picker is steps 3-5; without them this is just a setting):

1. CALIBRATE (first-run, 60s): Student is shown one short concept from their own uploaded material rendered live in all four registers, side by side as four cards. They tap the one that "clicks." This is choice, not consumption — the act of comparing registers forces them to notice HOW phrasing changes understanding, which is itself metacognitive learning.

2. STRESS-TEST (the active twist): OpenBook immediately asks ONE rapid free-recall question about that concept, but phrased in the chosen register. Student types/speaks a one-line answer. Claude grades the gist (not wording) and, if wrong, re-explains in the SAME register with a different angle, then re-asks. The student cannot proceed by passively reading — they must produce a correct recall in the chosen voice. This converts a cosmetic preference into an encoding event.

3. A/B PROOF (why it's not passive): Over the student's first ~10 study items, OpenBook silently serves a few explanations in a SECOND candidate register and tracks per-register recall accuracy + time-to-answer + "re-explain" taps. After the sample, it surfaces a one-card verdict: "You recalled 40% faster in Casual than Formal — lock it in?" The student is shown evidence of their own learning, which is the strongest retention cue (testing effect + self-relevance). They confirm or override.

4. LOCK + THREAD: The chosen register_id is written to the user's profile and injected as a stable, cache-friendly prefix block into the system prompt of EVERY OpenBook feature (concept explainer, quiz grader, flashcard generator, chat). One toggle changes the entire platform's voice. A floating pill control lets them flip register mid-session on any screen; flipping triggers a 1-question micro-recall in the new register so switching always costs a tiny act of retrieval (prevents register-surfing as avoidance).

5. RE-CALIBRATE ON DRIFT: If recall accuracy in the locked register drops below the student's rolling baseline across a topic, OpenBook proactively offers to re-run the A/B for THAT topic only ("Organic chem isn't landing in Gen Z — try Formal for this unit?"). Register becomes adaptive per subject, not a global vanity setting.

Why this produces retention not passive consumption: every register interaction is gated behind a free-recall production (steps 2, 4) and every lock-in decision is justified by the student's own measured performance (step 3). The student is not handed a re-worded summary; they are repeatedly tested and shown which voice makes them remember better. The register is the vehicle; retrieval practice + self-referent feedback is the cargo.
features.claudeUsage: See claudeUsage field above — model-per-task, streaming-vs-structured, the cached register-block prompt strategy, and the record_recall_outcome tool are all specified there.

columns: style_block_md text (the frozen system-prompt style guide injected per register)
dataModel.notes: Seeded reference table, not user data. style_block_md is the load-bearing field — it IS the cached system-prompt prefix. Versioned so prompt edits are auditable and cache-busting is explicit.
One row per user. active_register_id is what every feature reads to pick the cached prefix. RLS: user_id = auth.uid().
Enables step 5 per-subject adaptation. A feature resolves register by: topic_register_overrides match first, else user_communication_prefs.active_register_id. Unique(user_id, topic_id).

The authoritative A/B ledger written by the record_recall_outcome tool. All step-3 verdicts and step-5 drift detection are Postgres aggregates over this table (per-register accuracy, avg latency). Index (user_id, topic_id, register_id, created_at). This is also the accessibility/efficacy analytics source.

Caches the Sonnet 4-6 multi-register calibration render so re-opening calibration or flipping registers on a seen concept costs zero tokens. Unique(concept_id, register_id, style_version); invalidated when communication_registers.version bumps.

features.uiLayout:
DESIGN-DNA-CONSISTENT, light + dark:

- Calibration screen: calm editorial layout, generous whitespace, tight-tracked SF Pro Display headline "How should OpenBook talk to you?" over four large rounded cards (soft shadow, 20px radius) — one per register — each showing the SAME concept rephrased, with a small register label pill and emoji. Cards use the dark base (#242424/#2C2C2C in dark mode, off-white in light). Exactly ONE green (#C0FE6F) accent: it fills the selected card's check pill and nothing else. Subtle dotted/halftone texture behind the card grid.

- Floating register pill (persistent, every screen): a glassmorphic pill nav, bottom-center, showing the current register emoji + name; tap expands to the 4 options in a connected-node arc (learning-native UI). This is the always-available mid-session switch.

- Stress-test / recall gate: single focused card, the question in the chosen register, one input line, a black pill CTA "Check" in light mode (lime in dark). Correct = card flips with a soft spring; wrong = gentle re-explain card slides in below (never a red error slap — colorblind-safe: uses an amber dot + icon + label, never color alone).

- A/B verdict card: one clean stat card — a small bar comparing recall % and speed across the two tested registers, green accent only on the winner's bar. "Lock it in" black/lime pill + a quiet "keep exploring" text button.

STATES:
- Empty (pre-calibration): a single muted card "Pick a voice to begin" with a ghost preview cycling slowly through registers; no overwhelming grid.
- Loading (Sonnet multi-render): the four cards shimmer as skeletons in parallel (one structured call fills all four at once, so they resolve together — feels intentional, not janky).
- Streaming (Haiku explanations): text streams token-by-token into the reader with a soft caret.
- Error (model/parse fail): cards fall back to the cached render_cache row if present; if none, a calm inline "Voice unavailable, using your last one" toast — never blocks studying; the feature degrades to the last-good register.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS: card selection spring + haptic; register-flip pill morphs with a fluid emoji crossfade; the green check is the only saturated color event on the screen.
features.activeLearningRationale: A passive competitor ships a tone dropdown: pick "Gen Z," get re-worded summaries, done — zero retention value, pure cosmetics. OpenBook's design refuses to let register be cosmetic. Three mechanisms force genuine encoding: (1) RETRIEVAL GATING — you cannot advance past calibration or a register flip without producing a correct free-recall answer (testing effect; producing > re-reading for long-term retention). (2) COMPARATIVE ENCODING — seeing one concept in four registers and choosing forces you to process the concept four times through different surface forms, which is desirable-difficulty variation that strengthens the underlying schema. (3) SELF-REFERENT EVIDENCE — the A/B proof shows you YOUR OWN recall data per register; self-relevant, performance-based feedback is far stickier than a preference you set blindly, and it makes the student metacognitively aware of how phrasing affects their memory. The register is a Trojan horse: the student thinks they're personalizing tone; the system is actually running spaced, register-varied retrieval practice and feeding back efficacy. That is the line between active learning and a generator.
features.accessibilityNotes: Accessibility is baked into the register itself, not bolted on. Each register's frozen style_block hard-caps max_sentence_words and bans purple prose — this simultaneously keeps Gen Alpha from going cringe AND gives DYSLEXIC readers short, parseable sentences; the registers double as reading-load presets. DYSLEXIA: register blocks enforce dyslexia-friendly sentence length + the reader uses generous letter/line spacing; the global hover magnifier/bold tool works over register text since it is rendered as normal selectable text, not an image. ADHD: streaming Haiku output gives immediate token motion (no blank-wait), the recall gate keeps sessions in short produce-feedback loops (focus-friendly), and Focus Mode dims everything but the single active register card. COLORBLIND: correct/incorrect on the recall gate NEVER relies on red/green alone — always icon + amber/neutral dot + text label; the lone #C0FE6F accent is used only on non-critical affirmation (selected card, winning bar) so colorblind users lose no information if they can't distinguish it. WEAK EYESIGHT: SF Pro at comfortable size, high-contrast dark/light bases, large 44px+ tap targets on the floating pill and cards; the magnifier tool zooms register text in place. The register_recall_events table also lets us later detect if a student with a disability profile recalls markedly better in a given register and default them to it.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The single sharpest differentiator: OpenBook makes register EARN its place with the student's own recall data, gated behind retrieval. NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn/Studley at best offer a static tone setting that re-skins a one-shot output — passive, unmeasured, cosmetic. OpenBook runs a silent A/B across your first study items, gates every register choice and flip behind a free-recall production, and tells you "you remember 40% faster in Casual — lock it in," then threads that proven voice through every feature via one cached system-prompt block. Competitors let you change how the AI sounds; OpenBook proves which voice makes YOU remember and turns the tone-picker into a retrieval-practice engine.
features.buildNotes:
- Resolve register in a single server helper resolveRegister(userId, topicId): SELECT from topic_register_overrides first, fall back to user_communication_prefs.active_register_id, fall back to 'formal'. Every feature's API route calls this and prepends communication_registers.style_block_md (by version) as the cached system block. Centralize so there is exactly one place register enters a prompt.

- Prompt caching: the system block = FROZEN_PEDAGOGY + style_block_md must be byte-identical per (register_id, version) to get cache_read hits across features. Do NOT interpolate the date, user name, or concept into this block — those go in messages[] after the cache_control breakpoint (verify usage.cache_read_input_tokens > 0). Min cacheable prefix is 4096 tokens on Haiku 4-5 / 2048 on Sonnet 4-6 — pad the pedagogy rules accordingly or accept no-cache on tiny blocks.

- The 4-register calibration render is ONE Sonnet 4-6 client.messages.parse() with output_config.format = zodOutputFormat(z.object({formal,casual,gen_z,gen_alpha:z.string()})). Persist all four into register_render_cache keyed by (concept_id, register_id, style_version) so flips and re-opens are free.

- Grading: Haiku 4-5, strict:true json_schema {correct, gist_match_score, missing_concept, next_angle_hint}; prompt explicitly tells grader to ignore register/slang/spelling and score conceptual overlap only. Avoids penalizing a Gen Z answer for not sounding formal.

- record_recall_outcome custom tool writes register_recall_events in the same turn as grading (Supabase service-role insert from the tool handler). Step-3 verdict + step-5 drift = pure Postgres aggregates (window functions over register_recall_events) — no extra Claude calls, keeps cost near zero on the hot path.

- Supabase Realtime on user_communication_prefs so a register flip on one device updates the floating pill on all open tabs instantly.

- Gotcha: structured outputs cannot be combined with citations and reject message prefills on Opus/Sonnet/Haiku 4.x — don't prefill the JSON. Gen Alpha/Gen Z slang drifts stale; version the style_block (communication_registers.version) and bump to refresh both the prompt and the render_cache rather than editing in place.

- RLS on all user-scoped tables (user_id = auth.uid()); register_render_cache is per-concept not per-user so it can be shared/cached org-wide.

features.feature: Analytics — "The Next Action Engine": a retention-driven analytics surface that converts performance signals into one ranked, time-stamped study decision the student executes in-place, then re-measures. Not a dashboard of charts; a closed loop that decides what to review, when, and makes you do it now.
features.oneLine: Analytics that never just shows you a number — every metric is a button that launches the exact 5-minute review it implies, scheduled by a forgetting-curve model and narrated by Claude.
features.coreInteraction:
The loop is "Diagnose -> Decide -> Do -> Re-measure," and the student is forced through the Decide->Do hop on every visit so analytics is never passively consumed.

STEP 1 — DIAGNOSE (automatic, on page load). Each topic the student has studied carries a live "mastery" estimate and a "decay clock." Mastery is computed per concept from the student's own answer history (quizzes, flashcard grades, recall prompts) using a Bayesian estimate; the decay clock is a half-life retention model (SM-2-style ease + half-life regression) that predicts, for each concept, the probability you'd recall it RIGHT NOW. The system surfaces the 1-3 concepts whose recall probability is about to cross below 80% — the scientifically-validated "desirable difficulty" window where review is most efficient. These are the only things shown prominently; everything else is collapsed. This is the anti-vanity move: we hide the 92%-mastered topics and spotlight the 3 that are decaying.

STEP 2 — DECIDE (the forced hop). At the top sits a single "Today's Move" card — one ranked recommendation, e.g. "Review Krebs Cycle — you'll forget it in ~14h, and it's a prerequisite for 3 things on your exam." The student cannot dismiss it without choosing: [Start 4-min review] or [Snooze] (which makes them pick a reason: "already know it" downgrades urgency, "no time" reschedules). Picking a reason is itself a metacognitive judgment — the student is rating their own confidence, which is the active-learning hook. A passive dashboard lets you scroll past; this card blocks until you make a decision, and the decision feeds the model.

STEP 3 — DO (review happens in-surface, never a redirect to a generator). Clicking Start opens a focused review drawer over the analytics page: 3-6 Claude-generated retrieval-practice items targeting EXACTLY the decaying sub-concepts (not the whole topic). Free-recall first ("Explain the Krebs cycle's net ATP yield from memory"), then the student self-grades against a Claude-revealed model answer with a 1-4 confidence button (the SM-2 grade). Retrieval practice + self-grading + spaced timing is the trifecta the learning-science literature says drives durable retention; a one-shot quiz generator gives you items but no scheduling, no confidence calibration, and no re-measurement.

STEP 4 — RE-MEASURE (instant, visible). The moment the student grades themselves, the mastery bar animates to its new value and the decay clock resets to a new, later date — they SEE the curve push out. A "calibration" readout shows whether their predicted confidence matched their actual recall (over/under-confident), training metacognition over time. The next "Today's Move" recomputes and slides in. The loop closes; the student has done a unit of durable learning and watched the analytics prove it.

WHY THIS RETAINS vs PASSIVE CONSUMPTION: a generator ends at "here is a quiz."
