This loop refuses to let the student leave a number un-acted-upon, schedules each item at its individually-optimal spacing interval, forces a confidence judgment (calibration), and shows the retention curve move in real time so the feedback is immediate and motivating. The analytics ARE the study tool, not a readout about it.
features.claudeUsage:
Three-tier routing matching the repo's existing convention (fast=claude-haiku-4-5, balanced=claude-sonnet-4-6, deep=claude-opus-4-8 in src/lib/anthropic.ts). All math (mastery Bayesian update, half-life regression, scheduling) runs in TypeScript/Postgres — NEVER in Claude. Claude does pedagogy + language, not arithmetic.

1) "Today's Move" reasoning + copy — claude-haiku-4-5, structured output (NOT streaming; it's one short JSON object and latency matters on page load). The server computes the ranked candidate (lowest recall-probability concept weighted by prerequisite fan-out and exam proximity) deterministically, then hands Claude only the WINNER plus its stats and asks it to (a) write the one-line rationale in plain, motivating language and (b) pick the review modality. output_config.format with a Zod/json_schema: {rationale: string(<=140 chars), urgencyLabel: enum["now","today","this_week"], modality: enum["free_recall","cloze","application","teach_back"], estMinutes: int}. Haiku because the decision is already made by code — Claude only phrases and tags it. Prompt strategy: system prompt is frozen (caches; >4096 tokens of pedagogy rules + few-shot examples of good/bad rationales) and the volatile per-concept stats go in the user turn AFTER the cache breakpoint.

2) Retrieval-practice item generation — claude-sonnet-4-6, structured output, generated on-demand when the review drawer opens. Input: the concept's source chunks (retrieved via pgvector from the document the concept came from) + the sub-concepts flagged as decaying + the chosen modality + the student's prior wrong answers on this concept (so it targets the actual gap, not random facts). Schema: {items: [{prompt, modelAnswer, gradingRubric, targetsSubConceptId}]}. Sonnet because item quality (plausible distractors, rubric that maps to sub-concepts) is the quality bar; Haiku produces shallow items, Opus is overkill per-item. thinking:{type:"adaptive"}, effort "medium".

3) Self-grade adjudication (optional assist) — claude-haiku-4-5, structured, only fires if the student's self-grade conflicts with their free-text recall (e.g. they clicked "4/perfect" but wrote two sentences that miss the rubric). Returns {agreement: bool, gentleNudge: string} to counter the well-documented self-grading inflation problem. Cheap, high-volume, latency-tolerant.

4) Weekly "Calibration & Coaching" synthesis — claude-opus-4-8, STREAMING (longer output, user is reading it like a letter), runs on a Supabase cron once/week. Opus because this is the deep-pedagogy task: it reads the week's calibration data (predicted vs actual recall), spots patterns ("you consistently overrate visual topics, underrate definitions"), and writes a short coaching note that changes next week's scheduling weights. Streamed into a "Coach" panel. This is the only Opus call and the only streaming one — justified by output length and reasoning depth.

Token/caching: the frozen pedagogy system prompt carries a cache_control breakpoint; per-request concept stats sit after it, so repeated page loads hit the cache. Mastery math is never sent to Claude as a request to compute — only as context to phrase.

columns: source_chunk_ids uuid[] (pgvector chunks for item gen)
dataModel.notes: One row per atomic learnable concept; the prereq DAG drives the 'prerequisite for 3 exam topics' ranking. RLS: user_id = auth.uid().

columns: alpha numeric / beta numeric (Beta-distribution params updated per attempt)
dataModel.notes: The heart of the engine. recall_prob_now = 2^(-hours_since_review/half_life_hours). next_review_at = time when recall_prob hits target retention (default 0.85). A nightly Supabase cron recomputes recall_prob_now and next_review_at for all rows so the decay clock is fresh on load. Unique(user_id, concept_id).

columns: free_text_response text (what the student actually wrote)
dataModel.notes: Append-only event log; every attempt drives one Bayesian + SM-2 update to mastery_states. predicted_confidence vs self_grade/correct is the calibration signal. Feeds both scheduling and the weekly Opus coaching synthesis.

Materializes the forced-decision card so snooze reasons and completion are auditable and feed the model. snooze_reason='already_know' bumps ease; 'too_hard' lowers ease and shrinks the review interval. Idempotent per (user_id, generated_at::date).

columns: scheduling_weight_adjustments jsonb (fed back into next week's ranking)
dataModel.notes: Written by the weekly Opus cron. scheduling_weight_adjustments closes the meta-loop: the coach's findings literally re-tune how daily_moves are ranked the following week.

features.uiLayout:
Consistent with OpenBook DNA: dark base #242424 / light cream-white, SF Pro Display tight-tracked headings, SF Pro Text body, large rounded soft-shadow cards, ONE sparing #C0FE6F accent, floating pill nav.

ABOVE THE FOLD — "Today's Move" card. Single large rounded card, generous whitespace, subtle dotted halftone texture in the corner. Big SF Pro Display headline = the concept name; one Claude-written rationale line beneath. A slim horizontal "decay clock" — a thin track that drains from #C0FE6F (safe) toward a desaturated amber as recall probability falls (the green appears here, used once). Two pill buttons: a black pill CTA "Start 4-min review" (light mode) and a ghost "Snooze". The card cannot be scrolled past as resolved until a choice is made (sticky until status != pending).

MASTERY MAP (below) — a calm editorial grid of concept nodes connected by faint lines showing the prerequisite DAG (the "learning-native connected concept nodes" DNA). Each node is a small card: label + a circular mastery ring (not a bar chart — a ring that fills). Decaying nodes gently pulse; mastered nodes are dimmed and small (de-emphasized on purpose). Hovering a node shows its decay clock and "review now" affordance. This is the only "chart-like" element and it is itself interactive (click a node = launch its review).

REVIEW DRAWER — slides up from bottom (mobile) / in from right (desktop) as a glass/frosted panel over the dimmed map. One item at a time, huge centered prompt, a free-text recall field, a "Reveal model answer" pill, then a 1-4 confidence row. After grading: a satisfying spring animation (framer-motion) where the source node's ring fills and its decay clock visibly resets/extends — the "see the curve move" payoff.

COACH PANEL — a quiet right-rail card with the weekly Opus note, set like a short letter (editorial), updated weekly.

EMPTY STATE (no study history yet): no fake charts. A single centered card: "Analytics unlock as you learn. Finish one review and your first decay clock starts." with a primary pill linking to upload/study. Honest, on-brand, no zero-filled graphs.

LOADING STATE: skeleton cards with a soft shimmer in the node-ring shapes (not spinners); the Today's Move card shows a pulsing placeholder line. Mastery math loads instantly from Postgres; only the Claude rationale streams/fills in, so the card structure appears immediately and the sentence types in.

ERROR STATE: if Claude rationale generation fails, the card still renders with a deterministic fallback rationale built from the raw stats ("Recall dropping below 80% in ~14h — review now") so the loop never breaks on an API failure. If mastery data fails to load, a single inline retry pill, never a blank page.

MICRO-INTERACTIONS: decay clock drains in real time (requestAnimationFrame, recomputed client-side from half_life_hours so it's live without polling); confidence buttons have a tactile press; the next Move slides in with a spring after one resolves; node rings fill with eased animation.
features.activeLearningRationale:
A passive analytics generator (every competitor) ends at display: it renders a chart and the student looks at it. Looking at "you're 60% on Krebs Cycle" produces zero retention. This design is built on three findings from the retrieval-practice / spaced-repetition literature and operationalizes each as a forced interaction:

1) RETRIEVAL PRACTICE > RE-READING. The drawer leads with free recall ("explain from memory") before revealing the answer. The testing effect says the act of retrieval is what strengthens memory — so the analytic itself triggers a retrieval, not a re-read.

2) SPACED REPETITION AT THE DESIRABLE-DIFFICULTY WINDOW. The decay clock schedules each concept's review for the moment recall probability crosses ~80%, the point research shows maximizes long-term gain per minute. The analytics don't just report mastery; they time the intervention. A generator has no concept of "when."

3) METACOGNITIVE CALIBRATION. The student predicts confidence (1-4) BEFORE seeing the answer, then self-grades after. The gap is measured (Brier score) and the weekly coach surfaces systematic over/under-confidence. Calibration training is a documented driver of durable, transferable learning — and it's impossible without the predict-then-check interaction this loop mandates.

The "forced hop" (you cannot dismiss Today's Move without choosing or giving a snooze reason) is the structural guarantee that the student acts rather than consumes. And the real-time re-measurement (ring fills, clock resets, next Move slides in) supplies immediate feedback, which is what makes the loop self-reinforcing and habit-forming rather than a one-time read. The retention is produced BY the analytics interaction, not described by it.
features.differentiatorVsCompetitors: The single sharpest differentiator: OpenBook Analytics is the only one that tells you WHEN to study a specific concept and forces the review to happen inside the analytics surface. NotebookLM/StudyFetch/YouLearn answer "what's in my material" and at most emit a quiz; none model your personal forgetting curve, so none can say "review THIS in 14 hours or you'll lose it" and then put the 4-minute review one tap away with the retention curve visibly resetting. We turn analytics from a rear-view mirror (what you scored) into a forward scheduler (what to do next, and now) — a predictive, action-closing loop instead of a passive report.
features.accessibilityNotes:
ADHD: the entire surface deliberately shows ONE decision (Today's Move) and collapses everything else — single-task focus is the default, not an opt-in mode. Reviews are bounded and time-stamped ("4-min") so the commitment is small and the dopamine of a visibly-resetting decay clock lands immediately. A "Focus" toggle in the pill nav hides the mastery map entirely, leaving only the current item. Snooze's "no time -> reschedule" respects executive-function reality instead of guilt-tripping.

Dyslexia: SF Pro Text body at generous line-height and increased letter/word spacing; a global reading-prefs hook stores per-user spacing/weight in a user_prefs row and applies it to all prompt/answer text. Free-recall fields accept rough phrasing; Claude's rubric grading is meaning-based, never spelling-strict, so dyslexic students aren't penalized for orthography.

Colorblind: the decay clock and mastery rings never rely on red/green alone — the green-to-amber drain is paired with a numeric "% recall now" label and a fill-level (position-encoded), and ring state uses fill amount + a small glyph, not hue. Palette is checked against deuteranopia/protanopia; the lone #C0FE6F accent is used for position/fill, not as a pass/fail color.

Weak eyesight: the hover magnifier/bold tool (a global OpenBook utility) zooms or bolds any prompt, answer, or node label on hover while preserving layout; everything is scalable via rem units and respects OS text-zoom; the one-item-at-a-time review drawer uses large centered type so low-vision users aren't hunting through dense dashboards. prefers-reduced-motion disables the spring/pulse animations (the decay-clock value still updates, just without the drain animation).
features.buildNotes:
STACK FIT: repo already has @anthropic-ai/sdk 0.102, @supabase/ssr, framer-motion 12, zod 4, lucide-react. Reuse src/lib/anthropic.ts model() tiers (fast/balanced/deep already map to haiku/sonnet/opus-4-8) — do NOT hardcode model strings.

MATH LIVES IN CODE, NOT CLAUDE:
- recall_prob_now = 2 ** (-hoursSince(last_reviewed_at) / half_life_hours). Compute client-side for the live clock (requestAnimationFrame) AND server-side in a nightly cron for next_review_at.
- Bayesian mastery: maintain Beta(alpha, beta); correct attempt -> alpha += 1, wrong -> beta += 1; mastery = alpha/(alpha+beta). Decay-aware so old evidence is down-weighted.
- Scheduling: SM-2 ease/interval, with half_life regression: on each grade q(1-4), update ease, recompute half_life, set next_review_at = last_reviewed_at + half_life * log2(1/targetRetention).
- Ranking for daily_moves = (1 - recall_prob_now) * (1 + prereq_fanout_weight) * exam_proximity_weight, then ONLY the winner goes to Claude for phrasing.

CLAUDE CALLS:
- Use output_config:{format:{type:'json_schema',schema}} with zod for moves + item gen (NOT the deprecated output_format). messages.parse() to get typed parsed_output; guard for null (refusal/max_tokens).
- Today's Move: claude-haiku-4-5, no streaming, max_tokens ~256, frozen system prompt with cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} breakpoint (>4096 tokens so it actually caches on Haiku), volatile stats AFTER the breakpoint.
- Item gen: claude-sonnet-4-6, thinking:{type:'adaptive'}, effort 'medium', max_tokens ~2000.
- Weekly coach: claude-opus-4-8, STREAMING (.stream() + .finalMessage()), max_tokens ~2000, run from a Supabase cron / Edge Function; persist streamed text to calibration_weekly.coaching_note.

GOTCHAS:
- ALWAYS provide the deterministic fallback rationale so an Anthropic 429/529 never breaks the loop (wrap Claude call, on any APIError render stats-based string). Use typed SDK exceptions (Anthropic.RateLimitError) not string matching.
- Self-grade inflation: gate the optional Haiku adjudication only when self_grade>=3 AND free_text_response is short/diverges — don't run it every time (cost).
- pgvector retrieval for item gen: source_chunk_ids already on the concept; fetch those chunks rather than re-embedding the query.
- next_review_at recompute cron: a Supabase pg_cron job nightly + an on-demand recompute on each review write (so the post-review clock is instant, not stale till midnight).
- RLS on every table (user_id = auth.uid()); daily_moves idempotent per user per day to avoid duplicate cards on refresh.
- Realtime: subscribe to mastery_states so the ring/clock animate live if a review completes in another tab.
- prefers-reduced-motion + per-user reading prefs gate all framer-motion springs.

architecture.routeStructure:
OpenBook uses the Next.js 14 App Router with three top-level route groups. Defaults: every page is a React Server Component (RSC); client components ("use client") are leaf islands only (editors, canvas, chat input, hover-magnifier). Streaming AI responses live in Route Handlers (app/api/**/route.ts), NOT Server Actions (Server Actions can't stream tokens). Mutations that don't stream (rename doc, save note, toggle setting) use Server Actions. Supabase auth is read in the root layout via @supabase/ssr server client + middleware.ts for session refresh on every request.

ROUTE TREE
app/
  layout.tsx                      RSC root — fonts (SF Pro Display/Text via next/font/local), <html> theme attr, ThemeProvider (client island), Supabase session bootstrap, AccessibilityProvider (ADHD focus / dyslexia spacing / colorblind palette / magnifier — client)
  middleware.ts                   (project root) — Supabase session refresh + auth gate for (app); rate-limit header pass-through
  globals.css                     CSS vars: --accent #C0FE6F, --base #242424/#2C2C2C, light+dark tokens, OpenDyslexic class, focus-mode dimming
  not-found.tsx, error.tsx, loading.tsx

  (marketing)/                    PUBLIC — static/ISR, no auth
    layout.tsx                    floating pill nav (client for scroll state) + footer
    page.tsx                      landing (RSC) — tight-tracked hero, dotted texture, connected concept-node hero graphic, black pill CTA
    pricing/page.tsx
    manifesto/page.tsx            "active learning, not content delivery" thesis
    accessibility/page.tsx        secondary-market (ADHD/dyslexia/colorblind/low-vision) story
    (legal)/privacy|terms/page.tsx

  (auth)/                         UNAUTH ONLY — redirect to /dashboard if session
    layout.tsx                    centered card, brand wordmark
    login/page.tsx                client form -> server action signInWithPassword / OAuth
    signup/page.tsx
    callback/route.ts             OAuth code exchange (Supabase) -> set cookies -> redirect
    confirm/route.ts              email OTP/magic-link verify
    reset-password/page.tsx

  (app)/                          AUTHED workspace — gated in middleware + layout
    layout.tsx                    RSC — fetches profile, pill nav, command-palette mount (client), focus-mode toggle
    dashboard/page.tsx            RSC — recent docs, due flashcards (FSRS), streak, "continue studying" — Suspense streamed
    library/page.tsx              all sources grid (large rounded cards, soft shadow)
    upload/page.tsx               client dropzone + URL/GitHub/YouTube connectors

    documents/[docId]/
      layout.tsx                  RSC — loads doc + sources, split-view shell (reader pane | tutor rail), tabs
      page.tsx                    reader (RSC streams chunks) + HighlightLayer (client) + HoverMagnifier (client)
      tutor/page.tsx              Socratic chat companion (client; streams /api/chat)
      quiz/page.tsx               interactive quiz runner (client; adaptive)
      flashcards/page.tsx         FSRS review session (client; grade -> server action)
      mindmap/page.tsx            React-Flow concept-node canvas (client; streams /api/mindmap)
      notes/page.tsx              notebook (client editor; autosave server action)

    rooms/page.tsx                study rooms list (RSC)
    rooms/[roomId]/page.tsx       live room (client) — Supabase Realtime presence + shared companion
    analytics/page.tsx            mastery/retention dashboard (RSC, server-aggregated)
    settings/(profile|accessibility|billing)/page.tsx

API SURFACE (app/api/**/route.ts — Route Handlers)
  RUNTIME RULE: Anthropic streaming + Node-only parsers (pdf, docx, pptx, ffmpeg-less transcript) run on NODE runtime (export const runtime='nodejs'; maxDuration high). Lightweight token-streaming relays can be Edge, but because ingestion/embeddings/Supabase service-role live on Node, we standardize streaming handlers on Node too for a single auth+caching path. Realtime is delegated to Supabase Realtime (WebSocket), not a custom Edge socket.

  Streaming (Node, SSE via ReadableStream from anthropic.messages.stream):
    /api/chat                     Socratic tutor — RAG retrieve -> stream, model routing Sonnet/Opus, tool use (retrieve_more, quiz_me)
    /api/companion                live study-room shared tutor (writes turns to study_room broadcast)
    /api/explain                  hover "explain this span" micro-tutor (Haiku)
    /api/mindmap                  streams concept nodes/edges as structured JSON deltas

  Generate (Node, structured outputs — messages.parse / output_config.format):
    /api/quiz/generate            question set from chunks (Sonnet, json_schema)
    /api/quiz/grade               free-text answer grading + feedback (Sonnet)
    /api/flashcards/generate      Q/A pairs (Haiku/Sonnet)
    /api/summary                  guided study-guide (NOT one-shot — produces a learning plan)
    /api/notes/improve            note-completion / cloze suggestions

  Ingestion (Node, long-running, returns job id; heavy work backgrounded):
    /api/ingest                   POST {sourceType} -> create document+source rows, enqueue
    /api/ingest/[jobId]/status    poll job state (or Realtime subscription on documents.status)
    /api/connectors/youtube       transcript fetch
    /api/connectors/github        repo tree + file fetch
    /api/connectors/gdrive        Google Docs export

  Webhooks/util (Node):
    /api/storage/sign             issue short-lived signed upload/download URLs
    /api/anthropic/webhook        (future managed-agents) HMAC-verified
    /api/cron/fsrs-due            Vercel Cron — precompute due cards, send reminders
    /api/cron/cache-warm          pre-warm Anthropic prompt cache for hot docs

SERVER VS CLIENT SUMMARY
  Server Components: all page shells, reader, dashboards, library, analytics (data fetched with Supabase server client, RLS-scoped).
  Client Components: ThemeProvider, AccessibilityProvider+HoverMagnifier, chat/companion UIs, quiz/flashcard runners, mindmap canvas, note editor, dropzone, realtime room, command palette.
  Route Handlers: every Anthropic call and every ingestion/embedding call — never from the browser (keeps ANTHROPIC_API_KEY + service-role server-side).
  Server Actions: non-streaming mutations (save note, FSRS grade, rename, settings, create room).
architecture.ingestionPipeline:
Every input passes the same 5-stage pipeline: PARSE -> NORMALIZE -> CHUNK -> EMBED -> STORE. All stages run server-side in Node Route Handlers (and a backgrounded worker for heavy files). On upload, /api/ingest creates documents+sources rows (status='queued') and enqueues; the client subscribes to documents.status via Supabase Realtime for live progress (parsing -> chunking -> embedding -> ready). Each chunk persists exact-span anchors in chunks.loc so RAG can cite back to the precise source location.

PER-INPUT PARSE (concrete libraries; Node runtime):
- PDF: `unpdf` (serverless-friendly, wraps pdf.js) or `pdf-parse` for text + per-page char offsets; for scanned/image PDFs fall back to OCR (see image). Persist {page, char_start, char_end} in loc. (Optional premium path: send the PDF straight to Claude as a document block via the Files API for vision-grade extraction of tables/figures.)
- Word (.docx): `mammoth` -> HTML/text, preserving headings; map heading hierarchy into meta for section-aware chunking.
- PowerPoint (.pptx): `officeparser` (or `pptx-parser`) -> text per slide; loc carries {slide, char_start, char_end}; speaker notes appended.
- Google Docs: OAuth (Drive scope) -> Drive API files.export as text/plain or text/html via /api/connectors/gdrive; then same path as docx.
- Image (PNG/JPG): OCR with `tesseract.js` (lang from sources.meta.ocr_lang); diagrams/handwriting routed to Claude Haiku/Sonnet vision (image block) for a text transcription + description. loc = {bbox} when OCR gives word boxes.
- Code / notebooks: `.md` via `unified`/`remark`; `.ipynb` parsed as JSON -> concatenate markdown + code cells (code fenced) -> loc {file_path, cell_index, line_start, line_end}. Plain code split by language with a Tree-sitter-aware or function-boundary splitter so chunks align to functions/classes.
- YouTube: `/api/connectors/youtube` fetches the transcript (`youtube-transcript` / `youtubei.js`); if no captions, download audio and transcribe (Whisper via Groq/OpenAI or fal). loc carries {t_start_sec, t_end_sec} so citations deep-link to the timestamp.
- GitHub: `/api/connectors/github` uses Octokit (`@octokit/rest`) -> get repo tree (respect .gitignore + size/type allowlist: source, md, configs; skip binaries/lockfiles) -> fetch file blobs. Each file chunked by function/heading; loc {file_path, line_start, line_end, commit_sha}.

NORMALIZE: strip control chars, collapse whitespace, fix mojibake, dehyphenate PDF line-breaks, drop boilerplate (headers/footers/page numbers), language-detect (`franc`) -> sources.meta.

CHUNK: structure-aware recursive splitter (LangChain `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter`-style, or `llm-chunk`) targeting ~500-800 tokens with ~80-120 token overlap, but split on natural boundaries first (heading/slide/cell/function/transcript-paragraph) so a chunk never straddles a section. Token counts measured with `gpt-tokenizer` (good enough for chunk sizing; the actual RAG budget against Claude is verified with the Anthropic count_tokens endpoint). Each chunk carries ordinal + loc.

EMBED: batch chunks (~96/req) to the embeddings provider; store the 1536-dim vector. Embeddings are L2-normalized so the pgvector cosine HNSW index (<=>) is exact-direction. Retry/backoff on rate limits; failed batches re-queued.

STORE: bulk-insert chunks (content, ordinal, loc, embedding) under owner_id (RLS); flip sources.status then documents.status='ready'. Then a follow-up job pre-generates the first artifacts (study guide outline, starter flashcards) so the workspace is immediately interactive — but only as scaffolding for active learning, never as the terminal deliverable.

SCALE: ingestion is idempotent per (source_id, content hash); re-uploads dedupe. Heavy jobs (long videos, big repos) backgrounded so the Route Handler returns a job id fast; concurrency capped per user via the ai_usage/rate gate.
architecture.ragApproach:
EMBEDDING MODEL: Voyage `voyage-3` (1536-dim, strong retrieval, asymmetric query/document modes) as primary; `text-embedding-3-small` (1536-dim, same column width) as a drop-in fallback so no schema change is needed. Embeddings are normalized; pgvector HNSW with vector_cosine_ops (<=>) gives sub-10ms ANN at millions of rows.

CHUNKING (recap for retrieval quality): structure-aware ~500-800 token chunks with ~100-token overlap, never crossing a heading/slide/cell/function/transcript boundary. Overlap preserves cross-boundary context; small chunks keep citations tight.

RETRIEVAL (per question, inside the Route Handler):
1. Embed the user query (query mode).
2. Call match_chunks(document_id, query_vec, k) RPC — security invoker, so RLS guarantees a user only ever retrieves their own chunks. Default k=8, scoped to the active document_id.
3. Hybrid re-rank: combine cosine similarity with a Postgres full-text (ts_rank) keyword pass for exact-term questions (definitions, code symbols), then take top 6. (Optional Voyage `rerank-2` cross-encoder for high-stakes queries.)
4. Build a context block: each retrieved chunk wrapped as `[chunk_id=... | source | loc]` so the model can cite by id.

CITATION BACK TO EXACT SPANS:
- The system prompt requires the model to attach a citation array referencing the chunk_ids it used. We use Anthropic structured outputs / a `cite` field so every claim maps to {chunk_id, loc}.
- The UI resolves chunk_id -> chunks.loc and renders a clickable citation that scrolls the reader to the exact char range / PDF page / slide / code line / and deep-links a YouTube timestamp (t_start_sec). Highlights table reuses the same loc shape, so "cite" and "highlight" share one span-resolution path.
- Because loc was captured at parse time with real offsets (not reconstructed), citations land on the precise span — the differentiator vs competitors that cite whole documents.

ACTIVE-LEARNING FRAMING: retrieval feeds a Socratic tutor, not a summarizer — the model is prompted to ask guiding questions, surface the cited span, and check understanding, rather than dump an answer. Prompt caching (below) keeps the per-document context cheap across a long tutoring session.
architecture.anthropicPatterns:
SDK: @anthropic-ai/sdk v0.102 (already installed), called ONLY from Node Route Handlers / Server Actions. Model IDs: Opus 4.8 `claude-opus-4-8`, Sonnet 4.6 `claude-sonnet-4-6`, Haiku 4.5 `claude-haiku-4-5`. Thinking is adaptive-only on 4.x (`thinking:{type:'adaptive'}`); never send budget_tokens/temperature on Opus 4.8/4.7 (400s). Depth via `output_config:{effort:'low|medium|high|max'}` (max is Opus-tier only).

STREAMING ROUTE HANDLERS (Edge vs Node): all streaming handlers run on NODE (`export const runtime='nodejs'`) because they also touch Supabase service-role + Node parsers, and we want one auth/cache path; Anthropic streaming over Node easily holds the connection. Pattern: `const stream = client.messages.stream({...}); return new Response(toSSE(stream), {headers:{'content-type':'text/event-stream'}})` — bridge anthropic stream events (text_delta, thinking_delta) into a ReadableStream the client reads with EventSource/fetch-reader. Always stream for long output (prevents request timeouts); use `.finalMessage()` server-side to capture usage and persist the assistant turn + token counts. Set a high maxDuration on the route.

TOOL USE: the tutor exposes app tools (not server tools): `retrieve_more_context(query)` (re-runs match_chunks for follow-ups), `generate_quiz(topic,n)`, `make_flashcards(concept)`, `show_citation(chunk_id)`. We use the manual agentic loop (or the beta tool runner) in the Route Handler so we control RLS-scoped tool execution, log every call, and gate side-effecting tools. Tool input is always JSON.parse'd (4.x escaping), never string-matched.

STRUCTURED JSON OUTPUTS: quiz/flashcard/mindmap generation uses `output_config:{format:{type:'json_schema',schema:...}}` (or `messages.parse()` with a Zod schema via zodOutputFormat) so questions, choices, answers, citations, and graph nodes/edges come back schema-valid and insert straight into Postgres. Zod (already installed) defines the schemas once and validates client+server.

MODEL ROUTING (cheapest model that clears the bar):
- Haiku 4.5: hover "explain this span", flashcard Q/A pairs, classification (is-this-answer-correct), OCR/vision transcription, autocomplete in notes.
- Sonnet 4.6: the default Socratic tutor chat, quiz generation + free-text grading with feedback, study-guide planning, mindmap generation. Best speed/intelligence/$ for the interactive loop.
- Opus 4.8: hard reasoning only — deep "explain why I got this wrong across the whole document", multi-source synthesis, complex code/proof tutoring, and any request the router flags as high-difficulty. Run at effort 'high'/'xhigh'.
A small router (heuristic on task type + estimated difficulty + the user's recent error rate) picks the model; escalate Sonnet->Opus on low-confidence or explicit "I still don't get it".

PROMPT CACHING: the per-document RAG context + the (frozen) Socratic system prompt are placed BEFORE the volatile user turn with `cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'}` on the last stable block — so a long tutoring session over one document pays the big context once and reads it at ~0.1x thereafter. System prompt is frozen (no timestamps/UUIDs interpolated; dynamic state injected as later message content) and tool order is deterministic to keep the prefix byte-stable. A /api/cron/cache-warm job pre-warms hot documents with a `max_tokens:0` prefill so the first real turn is fast. We verify hits via usage.cache_read_input_tokens.

COST & ABUSE CONTROLS: every call logs to ai_usage (input/output/cache tokens, computed cost). Per-user/plan quotas enforced before the call (token + request budgets via Upstash/Vercel KV sliding window keyed on auth.uid); 429 with retry-after when exceeded. Non-latency-sensitive bulk jobs (generate flashcards for a whole library, batch-grade) use the Message Batches API at 50% cost. Free tier defaults to Haiku/Sonnet + low effort; Opus gated to paid. SDK auto-retries 429/5xx with backoff. Inputs are never silently truncated — oversized context is chunked/summarized with user notice. count_tokens guards budget before expensive calls.
architecture.realtimeStrategy:
Realtime is delegated entirely to Supabase Realtime (managed WebSocket) — no custom socket server, so it scales horizontally with no sticky sessions.

STUDY ROOMS:
- Presence: each member joins a Realtime channel `room:{roomId}` using Presence to show who's online, their cursor/scroll position in the shared document, and "currently studying X" status.
- Broadcast: ephemeral signals (reactions, "I'm stuck on Q3", pointer, focus-pings) sent via Realtime Broadcast (not persisted) for low latency.
- Postgres Changes: durable room state (members joining/leaving, shared highlights, the active quiz) is written to study_room_members/highlights/quizzes and clients subscribe to the relevant table changes filtered by room_id — RLS ensures only members receive rows.
- Shared live companion: the room's AI tutor runs in /api/companion (Node SSE). The host's (or any member's) prompt streams tokens to that one caller; as each assistant turn completes it's written to chat_messages and Broadcast-fanned to all members so everyone sees the same tutor response in sync. This makes the companion a genuine group active-learning loop, not N private chats.

LIVE COMPANION (solo): the single-user tutor uses the same SSE pattern; document.status and ingestion progress also stream over Realtime Postgres Changes so the upload UI updates live (queued->ready) without polling.

AUTH/SECURITY: Realtime channels enforce RLS via Supabase Realtime Authorization — only authed members of a room can subscribe to its Postgres Changes; Broadcast/Presence channels are gated by a server-checked room membership token. Presence heartbeats clean up stale members automatically.

ACCESSIBILITY IN ROOMS: focus-mode and reduced-motion are per-user (from profiles.prefs) and never forced by the host; the magnifier/bold tool works on the shared document locally without affecting others.
architecture.authStrategy: Supabase Auth via @supabase/ssr (already installed). Cookie-based sessions: a server client in (app)/layout + a middleware.ts at project root that refreshes the session on every request and gates the (app) group (redirect to /login when no session; redirect authed users away from (auth)). Methods: email/password, magic link (OTP), and OAuth (Google — doubles as the Google Docs/Drive connector grant, GitHub — doubles as the repo connector grant). OAuth code exchange handled in (auth)/callback/route.ts; email confirm in (auth)/confirm/route.ts. New users get a profiles row via the on_auth_user_created trigger. All data access is RLS-scoped to auth.uid(); the browser only ever uses the anon key (RLS-enforced), and the service-role key is used exclusively in Route Handlers for privileged writes (chunks, ai_usage). Connector OAuth tokens (Drive/GitHub) stored encrypted server-side, refreshed as needed, never exposed to the client.
architecture.storageStrategy: Supabase Storage, single private bucket `sources` with per-user path prefix `{auth.uid}/{documentId}/{file}`. RLS on storage.objects keys access to the first path segment = auth.uid()::text (policies in the SQL), so users can only read/write their own objects. Uploads use short-lived SIGNED UPLOAD URLs minted by /api/storage/sign (server checks ownership, returns a one-time token) — large files (up to ~500MB) go browser->Storage directly, never through the Route Handler. Downloads/reads for the reader pane use short-lived signed download URLs (e.g. 60-300s TTL). Parsers in the ingestion worker fetch the object with the service-role client. AI-generated artifacts (exported study guides, rendered mindmap PNGs) are written to the same bucket under the document path. Original bytes are retained for re-processing; derived chunks/embeddings live in Postgres.
architecture.summaryMarkdown:
# OpenBook — Technical Architecture

OpenBook is an **active-learning** platform: it guides students through their material with interactive loops (Socratic tutor, adaptive quizzes, FSRS flashcards, concept-node mindmaps) instead of emitting one-shot summaries. The stack is **Next.js 14 (App Router) + TypeScript + Tailwind + Supabase (Postgres/pgvector/Auth/Storage/Realtime) + Anthropic Claude**. Everything below is sized to scale to millions of users by leaning on managed primitives (Supabase Realtime, pgvector HNSW, Anthropic prompt caching + Batches) and keeping all secrets server-side.

## 1. App Router structure
Three route groups: `(marketing)` (public, ISR), `(auth)` (unauth-only), `(app)` (gated). **Server Components are the default**; client components are leaf islands only (chat, quiz/flashcard runners, mindmap canvas, note editor, dropzone, realtime room, and the accessibility layer — hover magnifier/bold, dyslexia spacing, ADHD focus mode, colorblind palette). **Anthropic streaming lives in Node Route Handlers** (`/api/chat`, `/api/companion`, `/api/explain`, `/api/mindmap`) — not Server Actions, which can't stream tokens. Non-streaming mutations (save note, FSRS grade, settings) use Server Actions. `middleware.ts` refreshes the Supabase session and gates `(app)`. Per-document `documents/[docId]/` has a split-view shell with `tutor / quiz / flashcards / mindmap / notes` sub-routes.

## 2. Data model
Full runnable SQL is provided: `profiles` (with accessibility `prefs`), `documents`, `sources`, `chunks` (pgvector `vector(1536)` + **HNSW cosine** index + `match_chunks` security-invoker RPC), `highlights`, `notes`, `quizzes/quiz_questions/quiz_attempts/quiz_answers`, `flashcards` + `flashcard_reviews` (FSRS state: stability/difficulty/due/reps/lapses), `mindmaps`, `study_sessions`, `chat_messages`, `analytics_events`, `study_rooms` + `study_room_members`, and an `ai_usage` ledger. **RLS is enabled on every table** — owner-scoped via `auth.uid()`, with a membership path for rooms (public/host/member). Storage RLS keys objects to `{uid}/...`. Every chunk stores exact-span `loc` (page/char/timestamp/slide/file-line) so citations land precisely.

## 3. Ingestion pipeline (every input type)
One pipeline — **parse → normalize → chunk → embed → store** — runs in Node. Concrete libs: **PDF** `unpdf`/`pdf-parse` (vision fallback via Claude Files API), **Word** `mammoth`, **PPT** `officeparser`, **Google Docs** Drive `files.export`, **image/OCR** `tesseract.js` + Claude vision, **code/.ipynb/.md** `remark`/Tree-sitter function-boundary splitting, **YouTube** `youtube-transcript`/`youtubei.js` (Whisper fallback, timestamp `loc`), **GitHub** Octokit tree+blobs with an allowlist. Chunks are structure-aware (~500–800 tokens, ~100 overlap, never crossing a section). Embeddings batch to **Voyage `voyage-3`** (OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` fallback, same 1536 width). Progress streams to the UI via Supabase Realtime on `documents.status`.

## 4. RAG + citations
Query embedded (asymmetric query mode) → `match_chunks` RPC (RLS-safe, scoped to the document) → hybrid re-rank (cosine + Postgres full-text for exact terms; optional `rerank-2`) → top-6 context block tagged with `chunk_id`/`loc`. The model returns a structured `cite` array; the UI resolves `chunk_id → loc` and scrolls the reader to the exact span / PDF page / slide / code line / YouTube timestamp. Highlights reuse the same span-resolution path. Retrieval feeds a **Socratic tutor**, not a summarizer.

## 5. Anthropic integration
Models: Opus 4.8 `claude-opus-4-8`, Sonnet 4.6 `claude-sonnet-4-6`, Haiku 4.5 `claude-haiku-4-5`. **Adaptive thinking only** on 4.x (no `budget_tokens`/`temperature` — they 400); depth via `output_config.effort`. **Streaming** Node handlers bridge `messages.stream()` into SSE and capture usage with `.finalMessage()`. **Tool use** (manual loop) exposes RLS-scoped app tools (`retrieve_more_context`, `generate_quiz`, `make_flashcards`, `show_citation`); inputs are always `JSON.parse`'d. **Structured outputs** (`output_config.format` / `messages.parse` + Zod) drive quiz/flashcard/mindmap generation straight into Postgres. **Model routing**: Haiku for explain-span/flashcards/OCR/classification, Sonnet for the default tutor + grading + study plans, Opus for hard multi-source reasoning (escalate on low confidence). **Prompt caching** puts the frozen system prompt + per-document context before the volatile turn (`cache_control: ephemeral`), with a cron pre-warm (`max_tokens:0`); a long tutoring session pays the big context once. **Cost/abuse**: per-call `ai_usage` logging, Upstash sliding-window quotas (429 + retry-after), **Batches API at 50%** for bulk jobs, Opus gated to paid.

## 6. Auth, storage, realtime
**Auth**: `@supabase/ssr` cookie sessions; email/OTP + Google (also the Drive grant) + GitHub (also the repo grant) OAuth; trigger seeds `profiles`. **Storage**: one private `sources` bucket, `{uid}/{docId}/file` paths, short-lived **signed upload/download URLs** so big files (≤500MB) bypass the function. **Realtime**: Supabase-managed WebSocket — Presence (who's online + scroll position), Broadcast (reactions, "stuck on Q3"), and Postgres Changes (durable room state) for study rooms; the **shared live companion** streams one tutor turn and Broadcast-fans it to all members so the group learns in sync. Realtime Authorization enforces room RLS.

## 7. Accessibility (secondary market), woven in
`profiles.prefs` drives a client `AccessibilityProvider`: a **hover magnifier/bold** that zooms/bolds any text while preserving layout, dyslexia-friendly spacing + OpenDyslexic toggle, an **ADHD focus mode** (dims everything but the active span and tracks `focus_seconds`), reduced motion, and colorblind-safe palettes — all per-user, never forced by a study-room host, and consistent with the Apple-HIG, lime-accent (`#C0FE6F`) dark/light design system.

## 8. Env + packages
Env vars and the npm package list are returned in their fields — note the project already ships `@anthropic-ai/sdk@0.102`, `@supabase/ssr`, `@supabase/supabase-js`, `zod`, `framer-motion`, and `lucide-react`; the remaining additions are the ingestion parsers, `voyageai`/`openai` embeddings, `@upstash/ratelimit`, `@xyflow/react` (mindmap), `@tiptap/*` (notes), and `ts-fsrs` (spaced repetition).

landing.winningConcept: Spine = Concept 1 "The Page That Reads Back," elevated by Concept 2's keynote-grade craft and shared-element morph, and warmed by Concept 3's first-person tutor voice. Final concept: "The Page That Reads Back." One ordinary document is the protagonist for the entire scroll. It begins flat, grey, and asleep on a calm editorial canvas; under the visitor's own scroll it learns to SEE (highlight what matters), TALK (a tutor in the margin), ASK (active-recall that won't let you advance), REMEMBER (concepts peel into a living node map), and ADAPT TO YOU (accessibility lenses as craft). It ends by turning to face the visitor and asking the next question. We never describe active learning in a feature grid — the page performs it. The demo IS the thesis. We reject Concept 3 as the spine (an abstract orb mascot risks the generic-AI aesthetic and hides the product), but we steal its single most valuable asset: the tutor speaking in warm first person at the emotional beats, which makes the awakening feel like a presence rather than an effect. We reject Concept 2 as the spine (a literal app-canvas keynote is excellent but more conventional and screenshot-dependent) while adopting its strongest mechanics: scroll-as-reversible-scrubber, the shared-element morph (the same document is the through-line from hero to mastery), and the functional drag-and-drop close. Result is one buildable, cinematic, restrained page that proves "active learning, not content delivery" in the medium itself.
landing.heroDirection:
THE SLEEPING PAGE. Near-full-viewport calm stage. Light: paper-warm #F7F7F5 base; Dark: #242424. A barely-there dotted halftone field bleeds in from the top corners (radial-masked, ~3% ink, never a full grid). The floating glass pill nav hovers ~24px from the top, centered, max-width ~720px: wordmark-only "OpenBook" in SF Pro Display medium at left (no icon), center links "Product / Method / Access / Pricing" in SF Pro Text, and a single high-contrast pill CTA at right — black pill "Start learning" in light mode, inverting to a lime #C0FE6F pill with #242424 text in dark mode. Nav has backdrop-blur(20px), a 1px hairline border at ~8% ink, and a soft long shadow; it is the ONLY high-contrast button above the fold.

THE STAGE. The tight-tracked SF Pro Display headline sits upper-left, deliberately overlapping the page card's top margin (editorial overlap, not centered): two lines, "Reading isn't" / "learning." — tracking ~-0.03em, weight 600, large (clamp ~3.5rem to 6rem). Directly beneath, in SF Pro Text grey (#6B6B69 light / #9A9A98 dark, ~1.25rem, max-width ~38ch): "Every other tool hands you a summary and walks away. OpenBook stays — it reads with you, asks the hard questions, and makes it stick." No second button up top; the nav CTA carries it.

THE PROTAGONIST. Centered slightly below and right of the headline sits ONE large rounded card (radius 28px, soft long low shadow, faint 1px glass edge) styled as a real textbook spread: a heading, two-to-three body paragraphs set in SF Pro Text, a small figure block. Crucially it is rendered INERT: grey-on-grey type at ~35% opacity, fully desaturated, slightly soft — visibly asleep. This exact card is the shared element that persists and transforms through every section to the mastery close.

THE SIGNATURE MOMENT (auto-plays once on load, ~2.4s, then idles; also re-triggers on hover). A non-OS "study cursor" drifts in from the right and the page wakes where it is touched: ONE sentence lifts ~2px into focus and de-greys to full ink; a lime #C0FE6F highlight sweeps left-to-right beneath it like a marker drawn by hand (clip-path wipe, ~600ms, ease-out, subtle pen-pressure taper at the ends); then a small tutor pill animates in from the right margin, connected to that exact line by a thin curved connector thread, and types in warm first person with a soft caret shimmer: "Why does this matter?" One restrained pop of green — everything else stays quiet and editorial. The page is left deliberately HALF awake: only one sentence is alive, the rest still grey, so the remaining scroll is literally the act of finishing the awakening. A thin "Scroll to wake the page" cue with a dotted descender sits at the lower center, fading on first scroll.

prefers-reduced-motion: the wake sequence resolves to its final state instantly (sentence in full ink, highlight present, tutor pill shown) with no sweep or drift; all later scroll-scrubbed scenes snap to their end frames.
landing.copyVoice: Plainspoken, confident, editorial — Apple/Anthropic restraint, never hype, never AI jargon. Short declaratives. The thesis is a quiet provocation, not a slogan: "Reading isn't learning." Headlines are 2-5 words, tight-tracked. Body is calm second person ("you upload, it reads with you"). The ONE warm device borrowed from the companion concept: at the genuine emotional beats — the first wake, the caught misconception, the close — the tutor speaks in FIRST PERSON, as a presence, never a feature ("Why does this matter?", "Not quite — let's look again.", "Ready when you are."). Everywhere else, second person. Each section title follows the awakening verb pattern: "It learns to see / talk / ask / remember / It learns YOU." Define the enemy plainly and once: competitors "hand you a summary and walk away" / "a summary is not studying." Accessibility copy frames features as the tutor's patience and craft, never as compliance ("meets you where you are," not "WCAG compliant"). Numbers are stated flatly without exclamation. CTA language escalates the thesis rather than saying "Sign up": primary nav CTA "Start learning"; final CTA "Stop reading. Start learning." with a secondary "Bring something you're struggling with." Forbidden: "revolutionary," "unleash," "supercharge," "powered by AI," emoji, exclamation-heavy lines.
motionPrinciples: Scroll IS the scrubber. Every scene is a scroll-progress-driven timeline, fully reversible — scrolling up un-does the awakening exactly. Use IntersectionObserver + a normalized 0-1 progress per pinned section; never autoplay long timelines on a timer (only the ~2.4s hero wake auto-plays once).

One protagonist, one shared element. The same document card is a persistent layout/shared element from hero to mastery dashboard. Transformations are morphs of that one object (FLIP / Framer Motion layoutId), never cut-to-new-screenshot. The visitor should never feel they left the page.

One pop of green, only to mark a moment of learning. #C0FE6F appears exactly once per beat and only where learning happens — a highlight, a caught misconception, a correct recall, a node lighting up. Never decoration, never a gradient, never on more than one element at a time.

Editorial calm over spectacle. Generous whitespace, slow confident easing (cubic-bezier(0.22,0.61,0.36,1)), durations 400-700ms for UI, sweeps ~600ms. Nothing bounces except one intentional physics moment (the file-drop). No parallax pile-ups, no confetti.
Type leads, motion follows. Text de-greying from ~35% to 100% ink is the core 'awakening' primitive used in every section; color and movement are secondary accents on top of that legibility shift.
Connectors are alive but thin. Tutor pills, recall prompts, and map edges attach to exact lines via hand-drawn-feeling curved 1px threads that draw on (stroke-dashoffset) rather than appear.

Accessibility is motion-honest. Full prefers-reduced-motion path: snap every scene to its end frame, disable sweeps/drifts/pulses, keep all content reachable without scroll-scrubbing. The accessibility section's lenses are real, live DOM transforms on the actual page copy, not mockups.

Performance budget. GPU-friendly transforms/opacity/clip-path only; pin scenes with position:sticky over transform hacks; lazy-mount heavy SVG node maps; target 60fps and no layout thrash on the scrubbed sections.
landing.rationale: Concept 1 is the only spine that makes the page itself the proof of the thesis — a static document waking up under scroll IS 'active learning, not content delivery,' argued in the medium rather than asserted in copy. That is the single most defensible, least-generic, most on-brand idea of the three, and it directly answers the competitive wedge (NotebookLM/StudyFetch hand you an artifact and leave; this page refuses to leave you passive even while you read it). Concept 2's spine is excellent but more conventional and leans on app screenshots; Concept 3's orb risks exactly the generic-AI mascot aesthetic the brief forbids and hides the product behind a metaphor. So I keep Concept 1's awakening narrative and graft in the two things it was missing: (a) Concept 2's rigor — scroll-as-reversible-scrubber, a true shared-element morph so it is ONE object not nine screenshots, and a functional drag-and-drop close that starts the real loop; and (b) Concept 3's warmth — the tutor speaking in first person at the emotional beats, which is what turns a clever scroll effect into a presence that 'reads back.' The merged section plan compresses Concept 1's nine acts into a tighter, build-feasible eight (folding the Method manifesto into the close as a breather) so the page stays cinematic without becoming exhausting. Every choice maps to the curated DNA confirmed in the inspiration images: floating glass pill nav, tight heavy display type, black/lime pill CTA, dotted halftone, soft rounded cards, the connected-node learning UI, the conversational glass surface, and the accessibility chip row — all present in the references, all reused here with one disciplined pop of lime.

landing.finalSpecMarkdown:
# OpenBook Landing Page — Definitive Build Spec
## "The Page That Reads Back"

**Thesis proven in the medium:** one ordinary document starts grey and asleep, and the entire scroll is the act of waking it. It learns to **see → talk → ask → remember → adapt to you**, then turns to face the visitor. We never describe active learning in a feature grid; the page performs it. Spine from Concept 1, rigor (reversible scroll-scrubber, shared-element morph, functional dropzone) from Concept 2, first-person tutor warmth from Concept 3.

---

## 0. Stack & Foundations

- **Framework:** Next.js 14 App Router + TypeScript. Page lives at `src/app/page.tsx` (Server Component shell) composing Client Components in `src/components/landing/*`.
- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS. Motion via existing `framer-motion@12`. Icons: `lucide-react` only where unavoidable (nav is wordmark-only, no icon).
- **No new heavy deps.** Scroll scrubbing = `framer-motion` `useScroll`/`useTransform` + native `IntersectionObserver`. Concept map = inline SVG. Do **not** add GSAP/Lenis unless a perf pass proves it necessary.
- **Fonts:** SF Pro is installed on the build machine. Register via `next/font/local` in `src/app/layout.tsx`:
  - `--font-sf-display` → SF Pro Display (headings)
  - `--font-sf-text` → SF Pro Text (body)
  - Replace the current Geist/Arial body font. Remove `font-family: Arial...` from `globals.css`.

### Design tokens (add to `globals.css` `:root` and a `.dark` class; drive dark mode with a `class` strategy, not only `prefers-color-scheme`, so the nav can offer a toggle later)
```css
:root {
  /* surfaces */
  --ob-bg:        #F7F7F5;   /* paper-warm light base */
  --ob-bg-2:      #FFFFFF;   /* card surface light */
  --ob-ink:       #1A1A18;   /* primary text light */
  --ob-ink-soft:  #6B6B69;   /* secondary text light */
  --ob-ink-asleep:#B9B9B5;   /* grey 'asleep' document text light */
  --ob-line:      rgba(26,26,24,0.08);
  /* brand */
  --ob-lime:      #C0FE6F;   /* THE accent — one pop per beat */
  --ob-lime-ink:  #2A3A12;   /* text on lime */
  /* radii / shadow */
  --ob-r-card:    28px;
  --ob-r-pill:    999px;
  --ob-shadow:    0 24px 60px -24px rgba(26,26,24,0.18), 0 2px 8px -2px rgba(26,26,24,0.08);
}
.dark {
  --ob-bg:        #242424;
  --ob-bg-2:      #2C2C2C;
  --ob-ink:       #F2F2EF;
  --ob-ink-soft:  #9A9A98;
  --ob-ink-asleep:#5A5A57;
  --ob-line:      rgba(242,242,239,0.10);
  --ob-shadow:    0 30px 70px -28px rgba(0,0,0,0.6), 0 2px 10px -2px rgba(0,0,0,0.4);
}
```
Expose in `tailwind.config.ts` under `theme.extend.colors` (`ink`, `ink-soft`, `ink-asleep`, `lime`, `surface`, `paper`), `borderRadius` (`card`, `pill`), `boxShadow.float`, `fontFamily.display`/`fontFamily.text`.

### Global rules
- **One green at a time.** Lint discipline: `--ob-lime` may render on at most one element per viewport. Never a gradient, never decoration, only where learning happens.
- **Dotted halftone:** a reusable `<Halftone/>` = radial-masked CSS `radial-gradient` dot pattern at ~3% ink, bleeding from top corners. Never a full grid.
- **Easing:** `--ease-ob: cubic-bezier(0.22, 0.61, 0.36, 1)`. UI 400–700ms; marker sweeps ~600ms.
- **`prefers-reduced-motion`:** mandatory. Wrap all scroll/auto timelines; under reduced motion every scene renders at its END frame, statically, fully legible. Provide a hook `useReducedMotionSafe()`.

---

## 1. Component map
```
src/components/landing/
  Nav.tsx                 // floating glass pill, progress dot
  Halftone.tsx            // reusable texture
  DocumentCard.tsx        // THE protagonist (shared element, layoutId="doc")
  Hero.tsx                // Scene 0 — Sleeping Page + Wake Sweep
  SceneSee.tsx            // Scene 1 — highlights & triage (scrubbed)
  SceneTalk.tsx           // Scene 2 — tutor in the margin (connector thread)
  SceneAsk.tsx            // Scene 3 — active-recall gate (scroll-snap pin)
  SceneRemember.tsx       // Scene 4 — words peel into concept map (SVG)
  SceneAdapt.tsx          // Scene 5 — accessibility lenses (live DOM)
  SceneSources.tsx        // Scene 6 — input chips funnel
  Proof.tsx               // Scene 7a — quiet evidence + count-up
  Close.tsx               // Scene 7b — turn-to-camera + functional dropzone
  primitives/
    WakeText.tsx          // de-grey + lift primitive (used everywhere)
    MarkerSweep.tsx       // lime clip-path highlight sweep
    TutorPill.tsx         // margin pill + typewriter + connector thread
    ScrubScene.tsx        // sticky-pin + normalized 0..1 progress wrapper
```

### Core primitive — `WakeText`
The single most reused idea. Props: `awake: number` (0→1) or boolean. Animates the run of text from `color: var(--ob-ink-asleep)` + `filter: saturate(0) blur(0.3px)` + `translateY(2px)` to `color: var(--ob-ink)` + `saturate(1) blur(0)` + `translateY(0)`. Every section that "wakes" copy composes this.

### Core wrapper — `ScrubScene`
`position: sticky; top: 0; height: 100vh` inner stage inside a tall `min-h-[220vh]` outer track. Uses `useScroll({ target, offset: ["start start","end end"] })` → `progress` (0..1) passed to children. Under reduced motion, force `progress = 1`.

---

## 2. Section-by-section spec

### Nav (`Nav.tsx`) — persistent
- Fixed, centered, `top: 24px`, `max-width: 720px`, `rounded-pill`, `backdrop-blur-[20px]`, `bg-surface/70`, 1px `--ob-line` border, `shadow-float`.
- Left: wordmark `OpenBook` (SF Pro Display, weight 500, tracking -0.01em). **No icon.**
- Center: `Product · Method · Access · Pricing` (SF Pro Text, `ink-soft`, hover→`ink`).
- Right: pill CTA `Start learning` — light: `bg-ink text-paper`; dark: `bg-lime text-lime-ink`.
- **Interaction:** on scroll past 40px, nav width eases to ~640px and shadow deepens (`useScroll` → `useTransform`). A 6px lime dot on the pill's left edge fills 0→100% mapped to total page scroll progress — the only persistent green; keep ≤6px so it never reads as decoration.

### Scene 0 — Hero, "The Sleeping Page" (`Hero.tsx`)
- Full-viewport stage, `bg-paper`, `<Halftone/>` top corners.
- `<DocumentCard asleep />` centered slightly below/right; rendered with `WakeText awake={0}` throughout (grey, desaturated, soft).
- Headline upper-left, **overlapping** the card's top margin: `Reading isn't` / `learning.` — SF Pro Display, weight 600, `clamp(3.5rem, 7vw, 6rem)`, tracking -0.03em, `leading-[0.95]`.
- Subhead (SF Pro Text, `ink-soft`, ~1.25rem, max 38ch): "Every other tool hands you a summary and walks away. OpenBook stays — it reads with you, asks the hard questions, and makes it stick."
- **Wake Sweep (auto once, ~2.4s, then idle; re-fires on card hover):** timeline —
  1. `t0–0.6s` study-cursor (custom SVG, not OS) drifts in from right.
  2. `t0.5–1.1s` target sentence: `WakeText 0→1` + `translateY` lift.
  3. `t0.8–1.4s` `<MarkerSweep/>` lime highlight, clip-path inset wipe L→R, ~600ms, ease-out, slight opacity taper at both ends (pen pressure).
  4. `t1.3–2.4s` `<TutorPill/>` slides from right margin; curved 1px connector draws on (`stroke-dashoffset`); types **"Why does this matter?"** with caret shimmer.
- Leave page **half awake** (only that one sentence alive). Bottom-center cue "Scroll to wake the page" + dotted descender, fades on first scroll.
- Reduced motion: render final frame instantly.

### Scene 1 — "It learns to see" (`SceneSee.tsx`)
- `ScrubScene`. `DocumentCard` continues (same `layoutId="doc"`).
- Section eyebrow (lime, tiny caps) + title `It learns to see.` + one line: "Not every sentence matters. OpenBook reads for what does."
- **Scrub:** as `progress` 0→1, a vertical pass de-greys ~5 load-bearing sentences (`WakeText`) and adds thin lime underlines to ~2 of them; simultaneously dims filler lines further (`ink-asleep`→ even lower opacity, `saturate(0)`). Margin tally label updates "signal / filler." Fully reversible on scroll-up.
- Green budget: only the active underline is lime, one at a time.

### Scene 2 — "It learns to talk" (`SceneTalk.tsx`)
- `ScrubScene`. A specific paragraph in `DocumentCard` is the anchor.
- **Interaction:** as the paragraph enters the band, a curved connector thread draws from a precise clause to a margin `<TutorPill/>`; the clause pulses once in lime (the anchor). Tutor types a 2-line first-person micro-dialogue; one student reply chip appears beneath as if answered. Copy direction: tutor asks a pointed question about the clause, not a summary. Emphasize "in the margin, on this line" vs. a far-off chatbot.

### Scene 3 — "It learns to ask" — THE HINGE (`SceneAsk.tsx`)
- `ScrubScene` with **scroll-snap pin**: `position: sticky` stage that does not release until engaged. Implementation: track `answered` state; while `!answered`, clamp further scroll reveal (overlay a `pointer-events` gate / use `scroll-snap-align` + a sentinel that only unlocks on interaction). Provide ONE polite escape: a quiet "Reveal answer" link.
- **Beat:** the current paragraph visibly folds shut (height collapse + `WakeText` reverse to grey) into a recall card: title "Without looking — what did this paragraph claim?", 3 option chips or a short input.
- Correct → chip turns lime once, tutor types first-person "That's it." and scroll releases.
- Wrong (intentional) → tutor types **"Not quite — let's look again,"** the source line re-opens and re-highlights in lime; retry. This is the passive→active flip and the page's signature interaction.
- Reduced motion: render the card answered/open with the answer shown, no pin.

### Scene 4 — "It learns to remember" (`SceneRemember.tsx`)
- `ScrubScene`. Inline SVG node graph echoing the "My Learning Plan" node UI from the references.
- **Interaction (words-peel-into-map):** scrubbing lifts the highlighted terms out of the shrinking `DocumentCard` and lands them as connected nodes; edges draw between related concepts (`stroke-dashoffset`). A small spaced-repetition / streak cue sits beside it (e.g., "Review in 2 days").
- Hovering a node back-highlights its origin sentence in the document (bidirectional, reversible). One node may glow lime at a time.

### Scene 5 — "It learns YOU" — Accessibility (`SceneAdapt.tsx`)
- Static (not scrubbed) but interactive. Four lens toggles: **Focus · Read · See · Color**, styled as restrained pills. Each transforms the REAL DOM of this section's copy (not a mockup):
  - **Focus (ADHD):** dim everything except the hovered line; reduce surrounding contrast; optional bionic-style bolded leading characters.
  - **Read (Dyslexia):** increase `letter-spacing`, `word-spacing`, `line-height`; switch to a heavier SF Pro Text weight; off-white tint behind paragraphs.
  - **See (Weak eyesight):** activate the **magnifier-bold cursor** — a lens following the real cursor that zooms + bolds any text it passes, working anywhere on the page (mount globally, gated by this toggle).
  - **Color (Color blindness):** swap to a colorblind-safe palette; demonstrate that lime + a second hue remain distinguishable; never rely on color alone (pair with shape/label).
- Copy frames these as the tutor's **patience** ("meets you where you are"), not compliance. No "WCAG" in user-facing copy.

### Scene 6 — "Any source, one living page" (`SceneSources.tsx`)
- Input chips funnel into the one `DocumentCard`: PDF · Word · PowerPoint · Google Docs · PNG/JPG · `.ipynb`/`.md` · YouTube · GitHub.
- **Interaction:** chips drift toward the card and are absorbed with a single soft lime ripple on contact (one ripple at a time). Message: every input becomes the same active surface.

### Scene 7a — Proof (`Proof.tsx`)
- Quiet, editorial. One disciplined count-up stat (fires once on inView, respects reduced motion → shows final number). One plain testimonial line — direction: "studying didn't feel lonely." Restrained stat row echoing the reference stat-cards but calmer; no logo wall.
- Short manifesto breather line, SF Pro Display, large, centered: **"A summary is not studying."**

### Scene 7b — The Close (`Close.tsx`)
- The protagonist `DocumentCard` performs a **3D turn-to-camera** (`rotateY`/`rotateX` toward viewer, perspective set on parent), now fully awake/full-ink. Tutor types first-person **"Ready when you are."**
- The card face becomes a **functional dropzone + paste field**: real drag-and-drop and URL paste. On drop/paste, kick off the actual ingest call (wire to existing `src/lib/anthropic.ts` / Supabase upload) — the loop literally begins on the landing page.
- Primary CTA pill: **"Stop reading. Start learning."** (black light / lime dark). Secondary ghost link: **"Bring something you're struggling with."**
- Footer: wordmark, minimal links, light/dark toggle, dotted halftone fade-out.

---

## 3. The shared element (critical)
`DocumentCard` must be **one persistent instance** across Hero → See → Talk → Ask → Remember → Sources → Close, transformed in place. Use Framer Motion `layoutId="doc"` (or a single pinned/position-tracked node) so it morphs (grey→lit→folded→shrunk→turned) and never cut-replaces. The visitor must feel they are working one page the whole way down — that continuity *is* the argument.

---

## 4. Accessibility & performance acceptance criteria
- All content readable and all interactions reachable with `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` (scenes at end-frame, no pins, no sweeps).
- The Ask gate must have a non-blocking escape ("Reveal answer") and full keyboard operability; never trap keyboard or screen-reader users.
- Magnifier/lenses are real and global where specified, toggle off cleanly, and never break layout.
- 60fps target on scrubbed scenes: transforms/opacity/clip-path only; `position: sticky` for pins; lazy-mount the SVG map; no layout thrash. Lighthouse a11y ≥ 95.
- Color is never the sole carrier of meaning (pair lime with underline/shape/label).
- Full light + dark parity; dark mode inverts CTA to lime pill.

---

## 5. Build order (suggested)
1. Tokens + fonts + `Nav` + `Halftone` + `DocumentCard` (static, both themes).
2. `WakeText` + `MarkerSweep` + `TutorPill` primitives.
3. `Hero` Wake Sweep (auto-once + reduced-motion path).
4. `ScrubScene` wrapper → `SceneSee`, `SceneTalk`.
5. `SceneAsk` gate (hardest; build the unlock + escape first).
6. `SceneRemember` SVG map; wire shared `layoutId`.
7. `SceneAdapt` lenses + global magnifier.
8. `SceneSources`, `Proof`, `Close` (functional dropzone → real ingest).
9. Reduced-motion + a11y + perf pass; light/dark QA.

It contains all 9 requested sections in order, synthesized into a single internally-consistent source of truth:

1. **Product overview & thesis** — active learning vs. passive generation; the "Claude is the coach, never the author" product law; the three learning-science levers (retrieval/generation/spacing) as recurring constraints.

3. **IA & route tree** — the three route groups, full `src/app` tree, API surface, `src/lib` layout, and server/client/route-handler split.
4. **Supabase schema** — one consolidated runnable SQL block (all 40+ tables, enums, pgvector HNSW, `match_chunks`, full RLS, storage policies) plus RLS notes.
5. **Anthropic/Claude integration** — the 4.x request-surface rules (adaptive-only, no `budget_tokens`/`temperature`, `output_config.effort`), streaming, structured outputs, the citations-vs-structured-output two-pass constraint, tool use, Files API, prompt caching (incl. the 4096-token Opus/Haiku floor), the fast/balanced/deep routing table, and cost/abuse controls.
6. **Ingestion + RAG pipeline** — per-input-type parser table, chunking, embeddings, Realtime progress, hybrid re-rank + citation span resolution.

8. **Landing page** — "The Page That Reads Back" section-by-section with signature interactions, the shared-element `layoutId` rule, copy voice, and acceptance criteria.
9. **Phased roadmap** — Phase 0 (foundation/design system) → 1 (landing) → 2 (auth/ingestion/shell) → 3 (Q&A+RAG) → 4 (recall modes) → 5 (authoring/capture) → 6 (comms mode + rooms) → 7 (analytics + a11y/perf polish), each with concrete deliverables.
